title: Transfer Learning Across Policy Regimes in Adaptive Multi-Agent Systems
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables. Simulation-based methodological study of positive and negative transfer across adaptive policy regimes
summary: Policy models often assume that the relationship between a policy instrument and its outcome remains stable across institutional conditions. In adaptive socio-technical systems this assumption may fail: regulatory change can alter incentives, agents can respond strategically, and the mapping from policy variables to aggregate outcomes can change. This paper studies such regime change as a transfer-learning problem in adaptive multi-agent systems. A policy regime is represented as a learning problem induced by an observable input distribution and a target function mapping policy variables to outcomes. We compare a blank-slate learner that searches a flexible hypothesis class in the new regime with a transfer learner whose effective hypothesis class is restricted by structural knowledge from the previous regime. Transfer is beneficial when this restriction preserves the new target function while reducing effective complexity; it is harmful when the restriction excludes the new target and creates misspecification. A stylized emissions-regulation experimental environment and a dynamic ABM robustness experiment support the claim. When the target regime preserves an affine monotone tax-emissions relation, transfer improves empirical small-sample performance. When the target regime introduces a threshold break, the same transferred structure produces negative transfer: held-out error remains high, online prediction generates more mistakes, and repeated online streams show larger cumulative and final-window error under misspecification. The contribution is methodological: previous regulatory experience should be reused when it captures stable structural invariants, but treated cautiously when policy change alters the policy-outcome relationship.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how policy regime changes affect learning in adaptive multi-agent systems, showing that transfer learning from previous regimes helps when structural relationships remain stable but causes negative transfer when the policy-outcome mapping changes fundamentally.
AI.motivation: Policy models typically assume stable relationships between policy instruments and outcomes across different institutional conditions, but this assumption can fail in adaptive socio-technical systems where regulatory changes can alter incentives and agent responses, changing the mapping from policy variables to outcomes.
AI.method: The paper frames regime change as a transfer-learning problem in adaptive multi-agent systems, comparing blank-slate learners with transfer learners restricted by structural knowledge from previous regimes. It uses a stylized emissions-regulation experimental environment and a dynamic agent-based modeling robustness experiment to test the framework.
AI.result: Transfer learning improves performance when the new regime preserves structural invariants like affine monotone tax-emissions relationships, but causes negative transfer when the regime introduces fundamental changes like threshold breaks, resulting in higher errors, more prediction mistakes, and worse cumulative performance.
AI.conclusion: Previous regulatory experience should be reused when it captures stable structural invariants, but treated cautiously when policy change alters the fundamental policy-outcome relationship, as transferred structural knowledge can create harmful misspecification in new regimes.

title: RideGym: A Standardized Interface for Real-World Large-Scale Ride-Sharing System
summary: Ride-sharing has become an essential component of modern urban transportation and has attracted significant attention across computer science, transportation, and management science. While the field spans a broad range of problems, such as driver relocation, dynamic pricing, and vehicle charging or fueling dispatch, the core challenge remains order assignment and trip bundling, which directly affect urban traffic efficiency and carbon emissions. Despite its importance, existing simulation platforms are typically tailored to specific operational studies or tightly coupled to a particular dispatch algorithm, and rarely expose a standardized, learning-friendly interface. As a result, most researchers still build customized environments from scratch, raising serious concerns about reproducibility and fair comparison, and incurring substantial redundant effort. To address this gap, we present RideGym, the first open-source, standardized Gym-style interface tailored to MARL-based order dispatch in real-world ride-sharing systems. By fully decoupling the environment from the dispatch algorithm, RideGym enables diverse learning-based and model-based methods to be developed and compared under identical, fully specified conditions. It supports efficient, large-scale city-level simulations on real road networks, and offers flexible configurations for vehicle attributes, order specifications, and automatic shortest-path routing. We validate RideGym by reproducing several baselines, and demonstrate its high efficiency, with a one-hour simulation involving thousands of vehicles and tens of thousands of orders completed within one minute across all methods. Moreover, we reveal that the choice of exploration noise can significantly affect both the performance and the relative ranking of MARL solutions, an aspect often overlooked in prior work.

AI.tldr: RideGym: A standardized Gym-style interface for multi-agent reinforcement learning in ride-sharing order dispatch, enabling fair comparisons and reproducibility across algorithms.
AI.motivation: Existing ride-sharing simulation platforms are often tailored to specific studies or tightly coupled with particular dispatch algorithms, lacking standardized, learning-friendly interfaces. This forces researchers to build custom environments from scratch, leading to reproducibility issues, unfair comparisons, and redundant effort.
AI.method: Developed RideGym, an open-source standardized Gym-style interface that fully decouples the environment from dispatch algorithms. It supports large-scale city-level simulations on real road networks with flexible configurations for vehicles, orders, and automatic shortest-path routing.
AI.result: Validated RideGym by reproducing several baselines, demonstrating high efficiency (one-hour simulation with thousands of vehicles and tens of thousands of orders completed within one minute). Also revealed that exploration noise significantly affects both performance and relative ranking of MARL solutions.
AI.conclusion: RideGym addresses the standardization gap in ride-sharing research, providing a common platform for developing and comparing learning-based methods under identical conditions, while uncovering important implementation details (like exploration noise) that impact algorithm performance.

title: Distributed Agent System: Fault-Tolerant Collaboration Among Embodied Agents
summary: AI engineering is shifting from passive text generation by large language models (LLMs) to agent-driven task execution, creating new reliability challenges for long-horizon tasks under resource constraints and environmental uncertainty. Conventional error-elimination optimization strategies fail to address cumulative error propagation. This paper proposes Distributed Agent System (DAS), a device-edge-cloud framework for fault-tolerant collaboration among heterogeneous agents. We redefine agent reliability as system-level fault tolerance rather than single-turn zero-error accuracy, and present a two-layer fault-tolerance architecture: single-agent execution reliability via fault-tolerant alignment, and cross-agent communication reliability via semi-formal language protocols. This framework provides a practical engineering pathway for reliable heterogeneous embodied agents collaboration in industrial scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DAS, a distributed device-edge-cloud framework for fault-tolerant collaboration among heterogeneous AI agents in long-horizon tasks under resource constraints, shifting focus from single-turn accuracy to system-level reliability.
AI.motivation: As AI engineering moves from passive text generation to agent-driven task execution, new reliability challenges emerge for long-horizon tasks under resource constraints and environmental uncertainty. Conventional error-elimination strategies fail to address cumulative error propagation in such complex, multi-step scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose Distributed Agent System (DAS), a device-edge-cloud framework with two-layer fault-tolerance architecture: 1) single-agent execution reliability via fault-tolerant alignment, and 2) cross-agent communication reliability via semi-formal language protocols for heterogeneous agents collaboration.
AI.result: The framework redefines agent reliability as system-level fault tolerance rather than single-turn zero-error accuracy, providing a practical engineering pathway for reliable heterogeneous embodied agents collaboration in industrial scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DAS addresses the critical gap in current AI agent systems by providing a distributed, fault-tolerant framework that handles cumulative error propagation in long-horizon tasks, offering a practical solution for reliable agent collaboration in real-world industrial applications with resource constraints and uncertainty.

title: Auditing Belief-Conditioned LLM Agents in Hidden-Information Social Deduction Games
summary:
Evaluating LLM agents in hidden-information multi-agent settings is hard: final outcomes are high-variance and rarely reveal why an agent decided as it did. We study this in a 9-player Werewolf environment where agents act under strict, code-level information isolation, and we build an auditable framework that maintains an external belief state over hidden roles, logs belief updates and belief-action deviations as structured evidence, and supports a defensive offline improvement loop that reviews bad cases before any strategy change.
  Across 1,080 frozen games spanning belief-disabled, active-belief, kernel-ablation, camp-restricted, consumption-policy, and high-load arms, and including a seed-paired A0/A1 comparison, the active-belief condition is associated with substantially better good-side outcomes: in the 200-seed A0/A1 comparison the good-side win rate rises from 0.205 to 0.390 (paired McNemar $χ^2 = 16.4$, $p < 0.001$), with fewer irreversible witch-poison errors. We do not, however, attribute this shift to belief content. Direct action-belief consistency is low ($\approx 0.21$), and giving belief only to the werewolves helps the good side more than giving it only to the good side, which argues against a simple holder-benefit account; we therefore report the effect as an association and treat its mechanism as unresolved. The contribution is the audit framework itself: it makes the effect measurable, exposes low direct action-belief consistency, rejects an unreliable forced-consumption intervention with evidence, and separates strategy effects from load confounds. We accordingly position external belief in high-noise hidden-information games primarily as an auditable cognitive baseline that also carries decision-relevant signal, turning opaque agent behavior into replayable evidence for safer, controlled iteration.

AI.tldr: An audit framework for LLM agents in hidden-information games (like Werewolf) that maintains external belief states and logs evidence to make opaque agent behavior interpretable and improvable.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLM agents in hidden-information multi-agent settings is difficult because final outcomes are high-variance and rarely reveal why agents made specific decisions. There's a need for transparent, auditable frameworks to understand agent behavior in complex games with information isolation.
AI.method: Developed an auditable framework for a 9-player Werewolf environment with code-level information isolation. The framework maintains an external belief state over hidden roles, logs belief updates and belief-action deviations as structured evidence, and supports a defensive offline improvement loop that reviews bad cases before strategy changes. Tested across 1,080 games with various conditions including belief-disabled, active-belief, and other experimental arms.
AI.result: Active-belief condition associated with substantially better good-side outcomes: win rate rose from 0.205 to 0.390 in paired comparison (p < 0.001). However, low direct action-belief consistency (≈0.21) suggests the effect isn't due to belief content itself. The framework successfully made effects measurable, exposed low consistency, rejected unreliable interventions, and separated strategy effects from load confounds.
AI.conclusion: The main contribution is the audit framework itself, which transforms opaque agent behavior into replayable evidence. External belief in hidden-information games serves primarily as an auditable cognitive baseline that also carries decision-relevant signal, enabling safer, controlled iteration in agent development.

title: From ML Predictions to Informed Diagnostic Assistance Using the Toulmin Model of Argumentation
summary: To provide a structured and interpretable assessment, we decompose the image-based diagnosis into components following the Toulmin model of argumentation. This model consists of a claim, grounds, warrant, qualifier, rebuttal, and backing. Consider a claim generated by a machine learning (ML) model for retinal diagnosis. Rather than accepting this claim at face value, one could either apply explainable AI (XAI) methods or adopt an argumentation-based approach. In our framework, a model specialized in biomarker extraction from images provides the grounds. The warrant-linking the grounds to the claim - is analyzed by an agent equipped with medical knowledge; in our architecture, this role is fulfilled by a MedGemma agent. The qualifier is determined based on the overall quantitative evaluation of both the warrant and grounds models. Finally, a rebuttal is constructed using image similarity measures computed with MedSigLip. All these components are presented to the human expert, enabling a more informed and critical assessment of the ML-generated diagnosis.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an argumentation-based framework for medical image diagnosis that decomposes ML predictions into interpretable Toulmin components using specialized models for each component.
AI.motivation: Traditional ML models for medical image diagnosis lack interpretability and critical assessment capabilities. The authors aim to move beyond simple XAI methods by providing a structured, argumentative decomposition of diagnoses that enables human experts to evaluate ML claims more critically.
AI.method: The framework decomposes diagnosis into Toulmin argument components: using biomarker extraction models as grounds, MedGemma agents as warrants linking evidence to claims, quantitative evaluation for qualifiers, and MedSigLip for rebuttals via image similarity measures.
AI.result: The proposed architecture creates a comprehensive argumentative structure around ML-generated medical claims, presenting human experts with grounds (biomarkers), warrants (medical reasoning), qualifiers (confidence levels), and rebuttals (counter-evidence) for critical assessment.
AI.conclusion: By framing medical image diagnosis as argumentative structures rather than black-box predictions, the approach enhances interpretability and enables more informed expert evaluation, potentially improving diagnostic reliability and trust in AI-assisted medicine.

title: Knowledge Graphs Meet Graph Neural Networks: A Comprehensive Survey
comment: Recently Accepted for publication in ACM Computing Surveys. This is the accepted manuscript version, and the final published version available at ACM Computing Surveys:https://doi.org/10.1145/3814608. Paper list at Github: https://github.com/sunxiaobei/awesome-gnn-based-knowledge-graphs
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm in Knowledge Graphs (KGs) due to their intrinsic ability to model graph-structured data. However, there remains a lack of a systematic review about GNN-based methodologies across the entire knowledge graph technologies pipeline. To address this gap, we first propose a novel two-level taxonomy framework for GNN-based knowledge graph technologies: the KG technologies pipeline and GNN-based perspective. Specifically, the knowledge graph technologies pipeline covers knowledge graph construction, knowledge graph embedding, knowledge reasoning and knowledge graph applications. Meanwhile, the GNN-based perspective provides a new categorization of knowledge graph technologies with GNN models, such as GCN, GAT, and HGNN. Then, we analyze the advantages of GNN technology based on the characteristics of different tasks in the knowledge graph lifecycle. Furthermore, we detailed review various GNN-based models for knowledge graph following the proposed taxonomy, and summarize strengths and limitations. Finally, we discuss unresolved challenges and outline promising directions for future research.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a systematic review of Graph Neural Network (GNN) methodologies applied across the entire knowledge graph technologies pipeline, introducing a novel two-level taxonomy framework for categorization and analysis.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of systematic review about GNN-based methodologies across the entire knowledge graph technologies pipeline. While GNNs have emerged as powerful tools for graph-structured data in KGs, no comprehensive framework exists to categorize and analyze GNN applications throughout the KG lifecycle.
AI.method: Proposes a novel two-level taxonomy framework: 1) KG technologies pipeline (covering knowledge graph construction, embedding, reasoning, and applications), and 2) GNN-based perspective (categorizing technologies with specific GNN models like GCN, GAT, HGNN). The paper then systematically reviews various GNN-based models according to this taxonomy.
AI.result: The analysis provides a comprehensive review of GNN-based models for knowledge graphs, categorizing them using the proposed taxonomy framework. The review identifies strengths and limitations of different GNN approaches across various KG tasks, and analyzes the advantages of GNN technology based on task characteristics throughout the knowledge graph lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a systematic framework for understanding GNN applications in knowledge graphs, highlighting both current achievements and research gaps. It concludes with discussions of unresolved challenges and outlines promising directions for future research in this rapidly evolving field.

title: Multi-Agent LLMs Fail to Explore Each Other
summary: Exploration is essential for reliable autonomy in multi-agent systems, yet it remains unclear whether large language model (LLM) agents can explore effectively when interacting with one another. We show that modern LLM agents fail to do so, often exhibiting myopic and polarized interaction patterns that lead to suboptimal coordination and increased regret. We formalize this challenge as the Multi-Agent Exploration problem, modeling it as a partially observable stochastic game (POSG) problem in which agents must probe peers to infer their capabilities and identify effective interaction strategies. To address this, we introduce Multi- Agent Contextual Exploration (MACE), a lightweight framework that explicitly promotes exploration through structured peer selection. Across both contextual and parametric diversity settings, MACE substantially improves exploration behavior and downstream task performance. We further show theoretically that the value of exploration increases with agent diversity. Overall, our results highlight a fundamental limitation of current LLM agents and underscore the importance of explicitly guided exploration for reliable multi-agent autonomy. Code will be released in https://github.com/deeplearning-wisc/mace

AI.tldr: LLM agents fail at effective exploration in multi-agent systems, showing myopic/polarized behavior. The paper introduces MACE framework to promote structured exploration, improving coordination and performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents in multi-agent systems exhibit poor exploration behavior - they show myopic and polarized interaction patterns that lead to suboptimal coordination and increased regret. There's uncertainty about whether LLM agents can explore effectively when interacting with each other.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the problem as Multi-Agent Exploration, modeling it as a partially observable stochastic game (POSG). They introduce Multi-Agent Contextual Exploration (MACE), a lightweight framework that explicitly promotes exploration through structured peer selection.
AI.result: MACE substantially improves exploration behavior and downstream task performance across both contextual and parametric diversity settings. Theoretical analysis shows that the value of exploration increases with agent diversity.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents have a fundamental limitation in exploration for multi-agent autonomy. The paper underscores the importance of explicitly guided exploration (like MACE) for reliable multi-agent systems.

title: Format Sensitivity Index: Token-Controlled Prompt Wrapper Robustness and Schema Compliance in LLM Benchmarking
summary: Prompt wrappers often differ only in formatting, yet they can change model scores enough to flip leaderboard conclusions. We study this variance under a token-controlled protocol and introduce two complementary metrics: the Format Sensitivity Index (FSI), the accuracy range induced by wrapper choice, and the Parseability Sensitivity Index (PSI), the corresponding range in answer parseability. Across 140,000 OpenRouter generations spanning 7 QA tasks, 5 wrapper families, and 4 instruct models from 7B to 72B parameters, we find that mean FSI varies by over 30x across models and is largely explained by compliance failures. A fixed-effects regression shows that parseability remains a strong predictor of accuracy even after controlling for task, model, and wrapper. We argue that reporting accuracy without wrapper variance and compliance is statistically fragile, and we give practical recommendations for both benchmarking and structured-output deployments.

AI.tldr: Prompt wrapper variance significantly affects model performance and leaderboard conclusions, with format changes causing enough score variation to flip conclusions. Two new metrics (FSI and PSI) quantify this sensitivity across models and tasks.
AI.motivation: Prompt wrappers often differ only in formatting but can substantially change model scores enough to flip leaderboard conclusions, revealing statistical fragility in current benchmarking practices. There's a need to understand and quantify this variance systematically.
AI.method: Used token-controlled protocol to study wrapper variance across 140,000 OpenRouter generations spanning 7 QA tasks, 5 wrapper families, and 4 instruct models (7B to 72B parameters). Introduced two metrics: Format Sensitivity Index (FSI) for accuracy range induced by wrapper choice, and Parseability Sensitivity Index (PSI) for corresponding answer parseability range. Applied fixed-effects regression analysis controlling for task, model, and wrapper.
AI.result: Mean FSI varies by over 30x across models and is largely explained by compliance failures. Parseability remains a strong predictor of accuracy even after controlling for task, model, and wrapper. Format wrapper choice significantly impacts both parseability and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Reporting accuracy without considering wrapper variance and compliance is statistically fragile. The paper provides practical recommendations for both benchmarking and structured-output deployments to address these sensitivity issues.

title: Position: Every Ground Truth is a Human Construction, not an Objective Truth
comment: 13 pages, 1 figure. To be published in Proceedings of the 43 rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026
summary: Ground truth datasets play a fundamental role as reference values in the training and evaluation of machine learning models. This position paper argues that ground truths are not neutral objective measurements that are naturally given, but instead that they are constructed by arrangements of humans and technologies. We argue that the ML community will benefit from articulating and discussing these often invisible or unreported choices and acknowledging that reference data sets are contingent, not universal. Focusing on the situated and context-dependent nature of ground truths can improve reliability by enabling a better informed perspective on where, when, and how the datasets, and the models they have shaped, can best be used. We argue for increasing `situated reliability' which includes articulating the limits and strengths of models and their truth claims. Finally, paying more attention to the construction of ground truths can support transparency, accountability, and interdisciplinary work.

AI.tldr: Ground truths are constructed, not naturally given; recognizing their contingent nature improves reliability and supports transparency in ML.
AI.motivation: To challenge the notion that ground truth datasets are neutral objective measurements and instead recognize them as constructed by human and technological arrangements, which is often invisible in ML practice.
AI.method: A position paper approach that argues theoretically for recognizing the constructed nature of ground truths and proposes the concept of 'situated reliability' while advocating for articulating hidden choices in dataset creation.
AI.result: Developed a framework recognizing ground truths as contingent rather than universal, proposing that acknowledging their situated nature enables better understanding of model applicability limits and improves reliability.
AI.conclusion: The ML community should articulate hidden choices in ground truth construction to improve situated reliability, support transparency, accountability, and interdisciplinary collaboration by acknowledging the contingent nature of reference datasets.

title: An Explainable Agentic System for Detection of Conversational Scams with Summary-Based Memory
summary: Following the rapid progress of generative Artificial Intelligence, there is a growing threat posed by conversational scams. These scams often span over multiple weeks or months, gradually build trust and request for money or sensitive information. Existing scam-detection systems mainly focus on isolated messages, which renders them inadequate against this evolving threat. This paper extends single-message phishing detection and presents an explainable agentic system for detecting sophisticated conversational scams. It also introduces ConScamBench-278, an initial public multi-category benchmark for conversational scam detection spanning eight scam types, released to support reproducible evaluation and future expansion. On isolated messages the single-message detector attains 100% phishing recall, while the conversation-level detector identifies all conversational scams in the public LoveFraud02 corpus (83/83) and reaches 97.8% accuracy (95% CI [95.4, 99.0]) on ConScamBench-278. Two user studies (N = 100 and N = 45) further motivate the system: participants report frequently experiencing uncertainty when judging suspicious conversations. In an uncontrolled pre/post comparison, users self-reported trust, self-confidence, and perceived need for AI-based scam detection all increased (p < 0.001, Wilcoxon signed-rank). The system also receives a System Usability Scale score of 74.7 (95% CI [72.5, 76.9]), above the established usability benchmark.

AI.tldr: An AI system for detecting conversational scams across multiple messages, achieving high accuracy and validated through user studies showing improved user confidence and trust in scam detection.
AI.motivation: Current scam detection systems focus on isolated messages, making them inadequate against sophisticated conversational scams that build trust over weeks or months to extract money or sensitive information.
AI.method: Extends single-message phishing detection to create an explainable agentic system for conversational scam detection, introduces ConScamBench-278 benchmark with 8 scam types, and conducts two user studies (N=100, N=45).
AI.result: Single-message detector achieves 100% phishing recall, conversation-level detector identifies all scams in LoveFraud02 corpus (83/83) and 97.8% accuracy on ConScamBench-278. User studies show increased trust, confidence, and perceived need for AI detection (p<0.001), with System Usability Score of 74.7.
AI.conclusion: The system effectively addresses limitations of existing scam detection by operating at conversation level, achieving high accuracy on benchmarks, and demonstrating improved user experience and trust in AI-assisted scam detection.

title: Faithful, Not Corrective: Message-Format Effects in Multi-Hop Agent Relays Are Tier-Dependent
summary: When LLM agents hand off information to one another, does the message format matter? Two literatures disagree: format-optimization work reports that structured messages cut cost without hurting accuracy, while format-restriction work finds that imposing structure degrades generation -- and neither measures what happens when a message traverses multiple hops, where copy fidelity, not one-shot generation, dominates. We introduce a controlled relay testbed: briefs of twelve programmatically generated atomic facts are re-encoded hop-by-hop in five formats (free NL, precision-instructed NL, JSON, triples, key-value) over six hops, scored by a fixed strong grader against programmatic ground truth, across two relay-capability tiers, a cognitive-load condition, and a paired-fork error injection. We find that message-format effects are tier-dependent. (i) Under faithful-relay instructions a strong relay is nearly lossless -- the documented "telephone-game" collapse does not occur -- and adding per-hop cognitive load leaves format-level fidelity unchanged (within +/-1.8 points) while raising generation cost by 24-53%. (ii) Under a weak (1.5B) relay the across-format spread of six-hop recall grows by a factor of 8.7 (from 2.3 to 20.5 points), driven by two opposing mechanisms -- an encoding toll paid by the rigid formats and drift resistance specific to the fixed-key JSON schema -- that flip the format ranking in transit. (iii) In a paired-fork injection, an injected wrong value, once present, persists to the final hop in 83-100% of chains in every format, closely matching each format's retention of the true value, with no detectable collateral damage to neighboring facts. Structure buys a faithful, error-localizing channel -- not an error-correcting code -- and format choice should follow the weakest relay in the pipeline.

AI.tldr: Message format effects in multi-hop LLM communication depend on relay strength: strong relays maintain fidelity across formats, while weak relays show 8.7x format-dependent variation, with JSON schema providing drift resistance but not error correction.
AI.motivation: Resolve disagreement between two research literatures: format-optimization work finds structured messages reduce costs without hurting accuracy, while format-restriction work finds structure degrades generation. Neither examines multi-hop scenarios where copy fidelity dominates over one-shot generation.
AI.method: Controlled relay testbed with programmatically generated atomic facts re-encoded hop-by-hop in five formats (free NL, precision-instructed NL, JSON, triples, key-value) over six hops. Scored by fixed strong grader against programmatic ground truth, across two relay-capability tiers, cognitive-load condition, and paired-fork error injection.
AI.result: 1) Strong relays are nearly lossless under faithful instructions; cognitive load raises costs 24-53% but leaves fidelity unchanged. 2) Weak relays show 8.7x format-dependent recall spread (2.3 to 20.5 points), with JSON schema providing drift resistance while rigid formats pay encoding toll. 3) Injected errors persist 83-100% in all formats with no collateral damage to neighboring facts.
AI.conclusion: Message structure provides faithful, error-localizing channels but not error correction. Format choice should follow the weakest relay in the pipeline, not the strongest. Different formats serve different roles: JSON provides drift resistance, while rigid formats have encoding costs.

title: AuditWeave: A Tamper-Evident, Auditor-Navigable Evidence Layer for AI-Assisted and Data-Transformation Workflows
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, open-source implementation at pypi.org/project/auditweave
summary: AI systems are increasingly used to assist consequential decisions in regulated domains such as auditing, finance, and healthcare. This creates a recurring obligation: an organization must be able to reconstruct, after the fact, which evidence informed a given conclusion, and to show that the record of that reasoning was not altered. Existing tools address related but distinct problems - model observability, drift monitoring, governance reporting - and are built for the machine-learning engineer operating a system, not the reviewer who must trace one specific conclusion back to its supporting evidence. We present AuditWeave, a lightweight Python library, with no runtime dependencies, that records the steps of AI-assisted and data-transformation workflows into a single append-only, hash-chained ledger. A small, system-agnostic event vocabulary spans both retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines and tabular/lakehouse transformations, so a conclusion that draws on both can be traced end-to-end through one record. Within a sealed ledger, any modification, reordering, insertion, or deletion of events is detectable through chain verification. We describe the design and evaluate recording overhead, scalability, and tamper-detection correctness on the reference implementation. The integrity guarantees cost tens of microseconds per event, and, as the hash-chain construction implies, verification flagged every injected mutation across four mutation classes over 2,000 randomized trials.

AI.tldr: AuditWeave is a Python library for creating tamper-evident audit trails of AI-assisted decisions, addressing traceability gaps in regulated domains.
AI.motivation: Existing tools focus on model observability and drift monitoring for engineers, but lack support for reviewers needing to trace specific conclusions back to supporting evidence in regulated domains.
AI.method: A lightweight Python library that records workflow steps into an append-only, hash-chained ledger using a system-agnostic event vocabulary covering both RAG pipelines and tabular transformations.
AI.result: Recording overhead is tens of microseconds per event; verification successfully flagged all injected mutations across 2,000 randomized trials.
AI.conclusion: AuditWeave provides a practical, low-overhead solution for maintaining immutable audit trails of AI-assisted workflows, enabling reliable reconstruction of decision evidence while detecting any tampering.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for C-V2X RAT Selection
summary: Vehicles are increasingly equipped with advanced V2X communication capabilities. While early V2X apps utilized services such as Cooperative Awareness Messages, recent developments have allowed more advanced applications including cooperative driving, shared perception, and sensor-sharing services. The broader mix of applications leads to heterogeneous requirements for latency and reliability. At the same time multiple communication technologies for V2X are available with pros and cons. Hybrid V2X communication can exploit the distinct advantages at the right moment to fulfill the requirements of the applications. This work studies the decision problem between cellular Uu link, NR-V2X PC5 sidelink, and the simultaneous use of both channels. We address this problem by using the multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm MAPPO and compare it to five baselines consisting of a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach, a static decision tree approach and static channel selection strategies. The methods are evaluated in an urban scenario and with a set of selected communication use cases. The evaluation results show that when compared to the DRL approach, the on-time delivery ratio improves from 0.508 to 0.535 in a single-controlled-vehicle setting and from 0.548 to 0.567 when all vehicles follow the learned policy and reduces the training time by half. The gains result mainly from the advanced applications scenarios, as opposed to scenarios involving exclusively CAM messaging. This indicates future applications will benefit from such adaptive communication strategies and that multi-agent modelling is useful for addressing the underlying decision problem.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-agent reinforcement learning approach (MAPPO) to optimize hybrid V2X communication channel selection between cellular Uu link, NR-V2X PC5 sidelink, or simultaneous use of both to meet heterogeneous latency and reliability requirements for advanced vehicle applications.
AI.motivation: With vehicles gaining advanced V2X capabilities and applications becoming more diverse (cooperative driving, shared perception, sensor-sharing), there are heterogeneous latency/reliability requirements. Multiple communication technologies exist with different pros/cons. Hybrid V2X communication can exploit different technologies' advantages at appropriate times to meet application requirements, creating a decision problem between communication channels.
AI.method: Uses multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm MAPPO (Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization) to solve the channel selection decision problem. Compares MAPPO against five baselines: a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach, a static decision tree approach, and static channel selection strategies. Evaluation conducted in urban scenario with selected communication use cases.
AI.result: MAPPO improves on-time delivery ratio from 0.508 to 0.535 vs DRL in single-controlled-vehicle setting, and from 0.548 to 0.567 when all vehicles follow learned policy. Also reduces training time by half. Gains mainly come from advanced application scenarios rather than basic CAM messaging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Future advanced V2X applications will benefit from adaptive communication strategies like MAPPO, and multi-agent modeling is effective for addressing V2X channel selection decision problems. The approach shows practical improvements in delivery performance while reducing training time.

title: Boltzmann MapReduce: A Partition-Function Reduce for Forkable Sandboxes
summary: To leading order under local asymptotic normality (LAN), the confidence density a worker emits over a chunk of size $n$ is a Gibbs--Boltzmann measure $\exp\{-βE(θ)\}$ whose inverse temperature is the sample size, $β=n$. Three consequences are exact in the Gaussian/linear case and first-order otherwise: disjoint chunks carry independent Boltzmann factors, so the MapReduce \emph{reduce}, read literally, is a partition function $Z=\int\prod_k h_k\,dθ$ whose mode is precision-weighted (inverse-variance) pooling; frequentist consistency is the zero-temperature limit $T=1/n\to0$

AI.tldr: Under local asymptotic normality, confidence densities from data chunks are Gibbs-Boltzmann measures with inverse temperature equal to sample size, enabling MapReduce-like distributed inference via partition function computation.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to bridge statistical inference with statistical mechanics by showing that confidence densities from data analysis can be viewed as Gibbs-Boltzmann measures. This provides a thermodynamic interpretation of statistical learning and enables distributed computation frameworks like MapReduce for statistical inference.
AI.method: The authors use local asymptotic normality (LAN) theory to show that leading-order approximations of confidence densities take Gibbs-Boltzmann form with inverse temperature β=n. They analyze disjoint data chunks producing independent Boltzmann factors, and demonstrate that the MapReduce framework's reduce operation becomes a partition function computation Z=∫∏h_k dθ.
AI.result: Three key results: 1) Confidence densities are Gibbs-Boltzmann measures with β=n; 2) Disjoint data chunks yield independent Boltzmann factors enabling distributed computation; 3) The MapReduce reduce operation is equivalent to computing a partition function whose mode corresponds to precision-weighted pooling. These are exact for Gaussian/linear cases and first-order approximations otherwise.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a deep connection between statistical inference and statistical mechanics, showing frequentist consistency emerges as the zero-temperature limit (T=1/n→0). This provides a thermodynamic foundation for distributed statistical inference and offers new interpretations of classical statistical concepts through the lens of statistical physics.

title: Ablation, Statistical Inference, and Validation for KV-Cache Compression
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, minimum number of citations
summary: This study systematically compares Turbo-Quant and SpectralQuant KV-cache compression, evaluating non-dominated schemes, including WHT rotation with Beta Lloyd-Max and QJL, through a statistical validation methodology that separates systematic codec differences from implementation variance. Key findings reveal that while eigenbasis-based methods fail on heavy-tailed data due to covariance instability, they excel in structured regimes, with the effective semantic dimension ($d_{eff}$) adapting to calibration budgets rather than true data rank. (this is an abstract of the abstract thank you )

AI.tldr: Comparison of Turbo-Quant and SpectralQuant KV-cache compression methods showing that eigenbasis methods fail on heavy-tailed data but excel in structured regimes, with semantic dimension adapting to calibration budgets rather than true data rank.
AI.motivation: The need to systematically compare and evaluate different KV-cache compression techniques for efficient memory usage in large language models, understanding the limitations and optimal use cases of eigenbasis-based methods.
AI.method: Systematic comparison using non-dominated schemes including WHT rotation with Beta Lloyd-Max and QJL, employing statistical validation methodology that separates systematic codec differences from implementation variance.
AI.result: Eigenbasis-based methods fail on heavy-tailed data due to covariance instability but excel in structured regimes. The effective semantic dimension (d_eff) adapts to calibration budgets rather than true data rank.
AI.conclusion: The choice between Turbo-Quant and SpectralQuant compression should consider data characteristics - eigenbasis methods work well in structured regimes but fail with heavy-tailed data, and semantic dimensions are budget-dependent rather than rank-based.

title: Forgetting Our Way to Shared Meaning: Effects of Forgetting on Conceptual Alignment in a Non-Partnership Coordination Game
summary: Shared meaning in language requires people to learn and agree on categories. We ask how characteristics of agents' memories change the emergence and evolution of shared meaning. Without a coordination game, models of conceptual semantics cannot explain how shared meaning emerges and changes in groups of people; however, existing games assume that players share payoffs in a partnership setting. We model conceptual alignment as a non-partnership game and illustrate differences in actual and perceived conceptual convergence from counterfactual simulations using agents with varying levels of adaptiveness and memory degradation. We found that adaptive players achieved actual convergence faster and had closer final conceptual regions than non-adaptive players, while non-adaptive players perceived convergence earlier. Weighing novel information less over time resulted in more stable agreements than fixing the weight of novel information. Memory features are critical to the emergence and evolution of actual and perceived convergence.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how agent memory characteristics affect the emergence and evolution of shared meaning in language through a non-partnership coordination game, finding that adaptive players with dynamic memory weighting achieve better convergence.
AI.motivation: Existing models of conceptual semantics lack mechanisms to explain how shared meaning emerges and changes in groups, and existing coordination games assume partnership settings. There's a need to model conceptual alignment as a non-partnership game while considering how memory features impact convergence.
AI.method: The researchers model conceptual alignment as a non-partnership coordination game and use counterfactual simulations with agents having varying levels of adaptiveness and memory degradation. They examine how different memory strategies affect actual vs. perceived convergence.
AI.result: Adaptive players achieved actual convergence faster with closer final conceptual regions, while non-adaptive players perceived convergence earlier. Weighting novel information less over time resulted in more stable agreements than fixed weighting. Memory features critically impact both actual and perceived convergence.
AI.conclusion: Memory characteristics are crucial for understanding how shared meaning emerges and evolves in groups, with adaptive memory strategies leading to better actual convergence while affecting perceptions of convergence differently.

title: Interpreting Latent CoT Reasoning as Dynamical Systems
summary: Recent latent reasoning methods, such as CODI and COCONUT, face a fundamental interpretability problem: they maintain multiple superimposed candidate traces in the hidden space at each step, unlike explicit- CoT, which follows a single transparent reasoning trace. Existing mechanistic methods show compression, shortcuts, and superposition without explaining how reasoning evolves across latent steps. To address this gap, we model latent token sequences as trajectories in representation space and apply dynamical systems analysis to characterize the evolution of reasoning. Using quantitative measures, such as step-to-step change, direction consistency, and Lyapunov sensitivity, alongside qualitative projections, such as UMAP and DMD/PHATE, we show that latent CoT exhibits structured, non-random dynamics with two distinct stability classes. CODI behaves as a stable attractor, while COCONUT behaves as an unstable expanding system, and SIM-CoT supervision tightens both behaviors without changing the underlying dynamics. This framework advances the interpretability of latent CoT reasoning dynamics and provides actionable insights for improving latent reasoning performance. Code1 and Project page2 available online.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a dynamical systems framework to analyze latent Chain-of-Thought reasoning, showing that methods like CODI behave as stable attractors while COCONUT behaves as unstable expanding systems.
AI.motivation: Recent latent reasoning methods (CODI, COCONUT) have interpretability problems because they maintain multiple superimposed candidate traces in hidden space rather than following a single transparent reasoning trace like explicit CoT. Existing mechanistic methods show compression, shortcuts, and superposition but don't explain how reasoning evolves across latent steps.
AI.method: The authors model latent token sequences as trajectories in representation space and apply dynamical systems analysis. They use quantitative measures like step-to-step change, direction consistency, and Lyapunov sensitivity, alongside qualitative projections like UMAP and DMD/PHATE to analyze reasoning dynamics.
AI.result: Latent CoT exhibits structured, non-random dynamics with two distinct stability classes: CODI behaves as a stable attractor, while COCONUT behaves as an unstable expanding system. SIM-CoT supervision tightens both behaviors without changing the underlying dynamics.
AI.conclusion: This dynamical systems framework advances the interpretability of latent CoT reasoning dynamics and provides actionable insights for improving latent reasoning performance. The approach helps understand how reasoning evolves in latent methods.

title: SciML in the Wild: A Diagnostic Study of When Structural Priors Help and When They Hurt
summary: Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) methods such as Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs), Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), and Universal Differential Equations (UDEs) are most effective when structural priors reflect reliable governing dynamics. We ask what happens when this assumption is violated. Using macroeconomic forecasting as a stress-test domain, we evaluate five model families, ARIMA, LSTM, NODE, PINN, and UDE, across 23 countries using sparse annual data, multiple temporal splits, and five random seeds. Our results show that none of the evaluated models achieve consistently strong forecasting performance, highlighting the difficulty of low-frequency macroeconomic prediction. However, a clear relative hierarchy emerges: less-constrained models, particularly ARIMA and NODE, consistently outperform more-constrained heuristic-prior models such as PINN and UDE. Rather than treating this as a rejection of SciML, we interpret it as a diagnostic result: structural priors can act as misregularizers when they do not match the data-generating process. We identify failure modes including prior misalignment, regime shifts, structural breaks, and optimization instability, and argue that SciML practitioners should test whether structure helps before assuming that more structure is beneficial.

AI.tldr: When structural priors in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) don't match real-world dynamics, they can hurt rather than help forecasting performance, as shown in macroeconomic prediction where less-constrained models outperform constrained ones.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates what happens when the assumption that structural priors in SciML methods reflect reliable governing dynamics is violated, using macroeconomic forecasting as a challenging test case to stress-test this assumption.
AI.method: Evaluated five model families (ARIMA, LSTM, NODE, PINN, and UDE) across 23 countries using sparse annual data with multiple temporal splits and five random seeds, creating a rigorous testbed for macroeconomic forecasting.
AI.result: None of the models achieved consistently strong forecasting performance, but a clear hierarchy emerged: less-constrained models (ARIMA and NODE) consistently outperformed more-constrained heuristic-prior models (PINN and UDE), with structural priors acting as misregularizers when mismatched.
AI.conclusion: Structural priors can be harmful when they don't match the data-generating process, creating failure modes like prior misalignment and regime shifts. Practitioners should empirically test whether structure helps rather than assuming more structure is always beneficial.

title: Closed-Loop Control with Rule-Aligned Small Language Models and Multi-Agent Self-Correction
summary: A key step toward autonomous industrial operation is the ability to create and reconfigure control policies from natural-language requirement specifications, with minimal or no manual redesign. In this setting, policy generation by AI agents can be a credible path when paired with a plant-aware validator (e.g., a digital twin) that can check generated candidate actions before execution. However, practical deployment is constrained by inference latency and compute footprint: large cloud-based models are often too slow, opaque, or data-sensitive for edge closed-loop use. This work investigates whether a compact Small Language Model (SLM) can be retrained for control reasoning and embedded in a validator-guided correction loop. We use a Qwen2.5-1.5B model aligned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), combined with (i) an action agent, (ii) a symbolic/digital-twin-style validation layer, and (iii) a reprompting agent that iteratively steers outputs toward valid actions. In randomized thermal-control simulations (30 experiments with 500 steps each), the framework achieves 91.5% average action-alignment accuracy (86.3%--100% across cases) at 3.84\,s mean inference latency. Under symbolic re-mapping, it maintains a 95% in-range rate, indicating robust physical regulation despite reduced token-level agreement. These results support SLM+validator architectures as a practical path toward reconfigurable autonomous control at the edge.

AI.tldr: A compact Small Language Model (SLM) is retrained and integrated with a digital-twin validator for autonomous industrial control from natural language specifications, achieving high action-alignment accuracy with low inference latency suitable for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Autonomous industrial operation requires creating control policies from natural language with minimal manual redesign. Current AI approaches using large cloud models are too slow, opaque, or data-sensitive for real-time edge closed-loop control applications.
AI.method: Retrained a compact Qwen2.5-1.5B Small Language Model using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Integrated with: (1) an action agent, (2) a symbolic/digital-twin validation layer, and (3) a reprompting agent that iteratively corrects outputs toward valid actions. Tested in randomized thermal-control simulations with 30 experiments (500 steps each).
AI.result: Achieved 91.5% average action-alignment accuracy (range: 86.3%-100%) with 3.84s mean inference latency. Under symbolic re-mapping, maintained 95% in-range rate, showing robust physical regulation despite reduced token-level agreement.
AI.conclusion: SLM+validator architectures provide a practical path toward reconfigurable autonomous control at the edge, demonstrating that compact language models can be effectively retrained for control reasoning when paired with validation-guided correction loops.

title: YUKTI: From Natural-Language Situations to Robust, Verifiable Decisions An Uncertainty-Typed Proposition IR, Assumption-Robust Pareto Frontiers, and a Regret Certificate
summary: Language models turn a worded situation into a numeric plan, and the dominant pipelines (NL4Opt, OptiMUS, ORLM, OR-LLM-Agent) commit to a single objective and point-valued coefficients, then solve once. For decisions that allocate real budget, effort, or clinical attention, that confidence is the failure mode: every objectified number is an assumption, and a plan optimal only if the guesses are exactly right is fragile -- mimicry of computation. YUKTI changes the target of autoformulation. Its representation is a typed-proposition graph whose relationships carry shape priors, coefficient uncertainty, and provenance. YUKTI routes each stage to an exact, nonlinear, or evolutionary solver; couples stages by a distributional Pareto hand-off; and introduces Assumption-Robust Pareto Frontiers (ARPF), resampling assumptions (including structural epsilon-contamination) to score how often each action survives (rho). We prove a bound making rho an exact factor of decision regret, add auditable traceability, and synthesize a benchmark-faithful data foundation when none exists (SRJANA). We validate three ways: under controlled misspecification the robust compromise cuts mean and tail regret by over 90% versus a naive point plan; on a regulated commercial decision we optimize inside a lawful action space and price the downside in euros; and on a real public dataset of 41,188 decisions an out-of-sample backtest beats the logged status quo by 34% and a naive point rule by 4% while reducing the optimizer's curse. The solvers are standard; we claim no benchmark-SOTA win. A head-to-head shows an LLM given the correct numbers, and single-objective optimization, both incur about 47x the held-out regret of YUKTI -- an LLM is a formulator, not a solver. Under long-range causal coupling, the forward hand-off becomes unsound, locating where it must become a backward-induction causal policy.

AI.tldr: YUKTI is a robust optimization framework that replaces single-point objective formulations with typed-proposition graphs that handle uncertainty, shape priors, and provenance, using assumption-robust Pareto frontiers to reduce decision regret by 90+% compared to naive point plans.
AI.motivation: Current language model pipelines (NL4Opt, OptiMUS, ORLM, OR-LLM-Agent) commit to single objectives and point-valued coefficients, treating them as exact. This creates fragile plans that are optimal only if all assumptions are exactly correct, which is unrealistic for real-world decisions involving budget, effort, or clinical attention where uncertainty exists.
AI.method: YUKTI uses a typed-proposition graph representation where relationships carry shape priors, coefficient uncertainty, and provenance. It routes each stage to appropriate solvers (exact, nonlinear, or evolutionary), couples stages via distributional Pareto hand-off, and introduces Assumption-Robust Pareto Frontiers (ARPF) that resample assumptions (including structural epsilon-contamination) to score how often each action survives (rho). It also provides auditable traceability and can synthesize benchmark data (SRJANA) when none exists.
AI.result: Three validation results: 1) Under controlled misspecification, robust compromise cuts mean and tail regret by over 90% vs naive point plans; 2) On a regulated commercial decision, optimization inside lawful action space with downside priced in euros; 3) On a real public dataset of 41,188 decisions, out-of-sample backtest beats logged status quo by 34% and naive point rule by 4% while reducing optimizer's curse. LLMs given correct numbers still incur ~47x more regret than YUKTI.
AI.conclusion: YUKTI provides a robust optimization framework that explicitly handles uncertainty and assumption sensitivity, proving that LLMs are formulators rather than solvers, and showing significant improvements in decision quality across diverse real-world applications. The framework also identifies when forward hand-offs become unsound under long-range causal coupling, indicating where backward-induction causal policies are needed.

title: MawForge: Memory-Bounded Expert Materialization for Local Mixture-of-Experts Inference
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models separate total parameter count from per-token active computation, but local inference systems often still require the full model, key-value cache, runtime buffers, and operatingsystem headroom to fit in fast memory. MawForge tests a different systems hypothesis: local MoE serving can be made practical on constrained unified-memory machines by storing the full model on disk, keeping common tensors resident, and materializing routed expert tensors into a bounded execution cache on demand. The central finding is that MawForge is effective as a bounded execution mechanism and measurement substrate for local MoE inference, but not as a cache-maximization policy. Performance depends on balancing expert reuse against resident footprint, KV-cache size, quantization, route locality, and macOS memory pressure.

AI.tldr: MawForge enables local MoE language model serving on constrained unified-memory machines by storing full models on disk and materializing routed experts into bounded execution cache on demand, rather than requiring all parameters to fit in fast memory.
AI.motivation: Current sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models still require full models, KV cache, runtime buffers, and OS headroom to fit in fast memory for local inference, making local serving impractical on constrained unified-memory machines.
AI.method: MawForge tests storing the full MoE model on disk, keeping common tensors resident, and materializing routed expert tensors into a bounded execution cache on demand, rather than loading the entire model into memory.
AI.result: MawForge is effective as a bounded execution mechanism and measurement substrate for local MoE inference, but not as a cache-maximization policy. Performance depends on balancing expert reuse against resident footprint, KV-cache size, quantization, route locality, and macOS memory pressure.
AI.conclusion: Local MoE serving can be made practical on constrained unified-memory machines through on-demand expert materialization with bounded execution caching, though careful trade-off management is required for optimal performance.

title: Feedback-Coupled Memory Systems in Continuous Time
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures. Extends arXiv:2603.11560 to continuous time. Code: github.com/stevefatz95/fcms-continuous
summary: The Feedback-Coupled Memory Systems (FCMS) architecture formalizes closed-loop coordination through four abstract operators, two of which - the agent update operator $f_i$ and the environmental update operator $Ψ$ - are left axiomatically undefined in the original framework. To address this, $f_i$ is defined by Mechanism-Based Intelligence (MBI), where agents update locally through a decentralized price mechanism and economic principles, and $Ψ$ is defined by the Coupled Memory Graph Process (CMGP), a non-Markovian framework where the environment is treated as a physical substrate that records and responds to trajectory history coherently without external forcing. The resulting continuous-time FCMS instantiation achieves Lyapunov global dissipativity governed by the computable threshold $4β^2 < 2ημγ^2$. This generalizes both the discrete FCMS stability condition $4ηβ^2 < γ$ and CMGP's physical bifurcation threshold $α_c = 1/K$, confirming that memory dissipation must outpace feedback gain as a universal organizing principle. Numerical simulation with $N=2$ agents and mean-field validation at $N=10^6$ confirm the stability threshold and the self-reinforcing coordination cascade that emerges when it is violated.

AI.tldr: The paper defines specific implementations for two previously undefined operators in Feedback-Coupled Memory Systems (FCMS): using Mechanism-Based Intelligence for agent updates and Coupled Memory Graph Process for environmental updates, establishing a continuous-time stability threshold that generalizes previous discrete and physical stability conditions.
AI.motivation: The FCMS architecture originally left two key operators - the agent update operator (f_i) and environmental update operator (Ψ) - axiomatically undefined, creating a need for concrete implementations to make the framework practically applicable and analyzable.
AI.method: Defines f_i using Mechanism-Based Intelligence (MBI) where agents update locally through decentralized price mechanisms and economic principles. Defines Ψ using Coupled Memory Graph Process (CMGP), a non-Markovian framework treating environment as a physical substrate that records and responds to trajectory history. Combines these to create a continuous-time FCMS instantiation.
AI.result: Derives a continuous-time stability threshold 4β² < 2ημγ² that generalizes both discrete FCMS stability condition (4ηβ² < γ) and CMGP's physical bifurcation threshold (α_c = 1/K). Demonstrates through numerical simulation (N=2 agents) and mean-field validation (N=10^6) that stability is maintained when this threshold holds, while violation leads to self-reinforcing coordination cascades.
AI.conclusion: Memory dissipation must outpace feedback gain as a universal organizing principle for stable coordination in coupled memory systems, and the proposed MBI-CMGP instantiation of FCMS provides a concrete framework that achieves this through a computable stability threshold that unifies previously separate stability conditions.

title: GES-TSP: Graph Edge Sparsification for TSP
summary: Solving large-scale instances of the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) exactly is computationally expensive. Researchers often employ graph sparsification methods to improve computational efficiency. Traditional sparsification methods typically rely on fixed heuristics and fail to fully exploit instance-specific structural information. In this paper, we propose Graph Edge Sparsification (GES), a learning-based sparsification approach for Euclidean TSP. By incorporating geometric structural information and combinatorial optimization technology, our proposed method adaptively generates a sparsification graph for different instances, significantly reducing the graph size and accelerating the solving process. Experimental results demonstrate that our sparsification method can prune up to 95% of edges on the MATILDA dataset, while keeping the solution gap within 1% of the optimal value. Moreover, our approach exhibits strong generalization capability on the TSPLIB benchmark.In some large-scale instances, the pruning rate exceeds 99%, while the optimality gap remains below 1%.

AI.tldr: Learning-based graph edge sparsification method for Euclidean TSP that reduces graph size by up to 99% while maintaining optimality gaps below 1%
AI.motivation: Solving large-scale TSP instances exactly is computationally expensive, and traditional graph sparsification methods fail to exploit instance-specific structural information and rely on fixed heuristics
AI.method: Graph Edge Sparsification (GES) - a learning-based approach that incorporates geometric structural information and combinatorial optimization technology to adaptively generate sparsification graphs for different instances
AI.result: The method prunes up to 95% of edges on MATILDA dataset with solution gap within 1% of optimal value; on TSPLIB benchmark, achieves over 99% pruning rate on large-scale instances while maintaining optimality gap below 1%
AI.conclusion: The proposed GES approach significantly reduces graph size and accelerates TSP solving while maintaining solution quality, demonstrating strong generalization capability across different TSP instances

title: Prioritizing Search Space Regions in the Low Autocorrelation Binary Sequences Problem
summary: Low autocorrelation binary sequences problem (LABS) is a hard combinatorial optimization challenge with important applications in communications, signal processing, and satellite navigation. This paper proposes a hybrid search framework that combines Thompson sampling with parallel self-avoiding walks to adaptively allocate computational effort across restriction classes of the LABS search space. By modeling partitions as arms in a multi-armed bandit setting, the proposed method dynamically shifts search resources toward partitions that empirically produce higher merit factors while maintaining exploration of less-sampled regions. The approach is further accelerated through GPU-parallel execution, shared posterior updates, efficient neighborhood evaluation, and a Bloom filter for cycle prevention. In addition, we use a two-stage optimization strategy that first searches constrained partitioned skew-symmetric spaces and then refines the best candidates in the unrestricted space. Experiments on long binary sequences show that the proposed method improves the previously best-known results for 35 sequence lengths in the range $450 \le L \le 527$ and for $L=573$. In particular, we report a new longest sequence with merit factor exceeding $8.0$, obtained for $L=451$. The results also show that Thompson sampling effectively prioritizes partitions with better observed performance, confirming the value of online, data-driven resource allocation in LABS optimization. Overall, the proposed framework provides a scalable and effective strategy for high-performance merit factor maximization.

AI.tldr: A hybrid search framework combining Thompson sampling with parallel self-avoiding walks for the Low Autocorrelation Binary Sequences problem, achieving improved results for 36 sequence lengths including a new record for L=451.
AI.motivation: The Low Autocorrelation Binary Sequences (LABS) problem is a hard combinatorial optimization challenge with important applications in communications, signal processing, and satellite navigation. There's a need for better methods to optimize merit factors for long binary sequences.
AI.method: A hybrid search framework that combines Thompson sampling with parallel self-avoiding walks to adaptively allocate computational effort across restriction classes of the LABS search space. The approach uses multi-armed bandit modeling of partitions, GPU-parallel execution, shared posterior updates, efficient neighborhood evaluation, Bloom filter for cycle prevention, and a two-stage optimization strategy that first searches constrained partitioned skew-symmetric spaces then refines in unrestricted space.
AI.result: The method improves previously best-known results for 35 sequence lengths in the range 450 ≤ L ≤ 527 and for L=573. A new longest sequence with merit factor exceeding 8.0 was obtained for L=451. Thompson sampling effectively prioritizes partitions with better observed performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a scalable and effective strategy for high-performance merit factor maximization in LABS optimization, confirming the value of online, data-driven resource allocation approaches for combinatorial optimization problems.

title: Replicating Belief, Not Bits: Epistemic State Replication for Agentic Systems
summary:
In distributed systems, the classical State Machine Replication (SMR) model assumes that correct replicas execute deterministic transitions to yield identical bitwise states. However, the rise of agentic distributed systems -- where autonomous, stochastic, and model-driven agents orchestrate infrastructure -- presents scenarios where deterministic, bitwise replication is insufficient. Replicas operating with generative models may exhibit divergent reasoning paths, summaries, and token boundaries, yet reach semantically equivalent and correct operational decisions. Forcing bitwise agreement across these stochastic participants degrades execution flexibility, induces context amnesia, and limits performance.
  We argue that in such settings replicas should agree on belief, not bits. We propose Epistemic State Replication (ESR), a belief-replication layer for agentic distributed systems that shifts the replication boundary from data visibility to knowledge visibility. We formalize the epistemic node state as a pair K = (L, B) separating the deterministic, immutable evidence log (L) from the stochastic, evolving belief lineage (B). To govern execution safety, we define Semantic Linearizability, which requires operations to reflect the latest committed operational meaning within a verifier-bounded semantic compatibility metric, and Bounded Eventual Coherence, which bounds expected semantic divergence under fair delivery, monotonic evidence, bounded verifier disturbance, and a contractive graft operator. We outline protocols for propagating derived insights using structured epistemic deltas, and formalize Verifiable Semantic Rollbacks to prune faulty premises from belief lineages without inducing context amnesia. We prototype ESR and report preliminary simulation results that show feasibility under the stated assumptions and illustrate reductions in secondary cognitive faults.

AI.tldr: ESR (Epistemic State Replication) is a belief-replication layer for agentic distributed systems that shifts from bitwise to belief-based agreement, addressing scenarios where stochastic agents reach semantically equivalent decisions despite divergent reasoning paths.
AI.motivation: Traditional State Machine Replication assumes deterministic, bitwise identical states, but this is insufficient for agentic systems where autonomous, stochastic agents using generative models may have divergent reasoning paths while reaching semantically equivalent decisions. Forcing bitwise agreement degrades execution flexibility, causes context amnesia, and limits performance.
AI.method: Proposes Epistemic State Replication with formalized epistemic node state K = (L, B) separating deterministic evidence log (L) from stochastic belief lineage (B). Introduces Semantic Linearizability for safety (reflects latest committed operational meaning) and Bounded Eventual Coherence for bounding semantic divergence. Includes protocols for propagating epistemic deltas and Verifiable Semantic Rollbacks to prune faulty premises without context amnesia.
AI.result: Preliminary simulation results show feasibility under stated assumptions and illustrate reductions in secondary cognitive faults. The approach demonstrates that belief-based replication is viable for agentic distributed systems.
AI.conclusion: ESR provides a novel replication paradigm for agentic systems by shifting from bitwise to belief-based agreement, enabling semantic equivalence while maintaining safety and bounded divergence through formal guarantees and protocols for managing stochastic belief evolution.

title: The Verifier is the Curriculum: Execution-Gated Self-Distillation for Cross-Family Game Generation
summary: Post-training a code generator against a learned judge can optimize proxy features that raise the score without improving the artifact. We study the opposite signal: a deterministic, judge-free, ungameable filter -- whether a generated project launches cleanly under a headless engine (strict-launch). Under this gate, rejection-sampling self-distillation compounds out-of-family generalization. On GameCraft-Bench (mapping a natural-language brief to a complete Godot project), a 14B model (Qwen3-14B+LoRA) distilled under strict-launch raises clean generation on four unseen game families from 8.8% to 42.2% per-candidate and best-of-K coverage from 18/25 to 25/25 (the gold ceiling) over three rounds, each a significant gain (p=0.0019, p<1e-4, p<1e-4). The gain is not from merely adding data: an exactly-matched gold-duplication control regresses below the base model (5.6% vs. 8.8%, p=0.019), while a count-matched decomposition splits the round-1-to-2 jump into comparable quality (+8.8pp) and quantity (+8.5pp) channels. Most directly, rerunning the loop with only the filter swapped -- the lenient BUILD check, which passes 99.9% of generations, in place of the launch gate -- erases the gain entirely (back to base, p=1e-3 vs. the launch-gated round), isolating verifier precision rather than the optimizer. A second ungameable signal, headless execution grounding, rises monotonically across rounds and yields far more grounded candidates than gold-duplication at a matched budget (16 vs. 5), confirming the gains are functional, not launch-but-empty. Game generation is a verifiable testbed for one lesson: the verifier is the curriculum -- what it certifies is what the model learns.

AI.tldr: Self-distillation using a strict-launch filter (verifying projects run in headless engine) dramatically improves code generation quality, while lenient filters or gold duplication fail, showing the verifier defines what models learn.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods using learned judges can be gamed to optimize proxy features without improving actual functionality. The paper aims to study whether deterministic, ungameable filters (like verifying code actually runs) can lead to better generalization in code generation tasks.
AI.method: The method uses rejection-sampling self-distillation with a strict-launch filter that checks whether generated Godot projects launch cleanly under a headless engine. The model (Qwen3-14B+LoRA) is trained on GameCraft-Bench, with distillation over three rounds using this deterministic gate to select successful generations for further training.
AI.result: Strict-launch distillation raised clean generation on four unseen game families from 8.8% to 42.2% per-candidate and achieved perfect best-of-K coverage (25/25 vs. 18/25 baseline). Control experiments show: 1) gold-duplication actually regresses performance (5.6% vs. 8.8%), 2) lenient filters erase gains, 3) execution grounding improves monotonically across rounds (16 vs. 5 grounded candidates with matched budget).
AI.conclusion: The verifier defines the curriculum - what it certifies is what the model learns. Deterministic, ungameable filters like strict-launch verification are crucial for meaningful improvement in code generation, while lenient or gameable signals fail to produce functional gains. Game generation serves as a verifiable testbed demonstrating this principle.

title: What Context Does a Coding Agent Actually Need to Act?
summary: A modern coding agent can hold an entire repository in its context window. Most of its reading is wasted -- and the interesting question is not how much context an agent can use, but what it actually \emph{needs}. We study that question at the moment it matters most: when the agent must \emph{edit} code. Separating \emph{finding} the work site from \emph{acting} on it, we hold localization fixed with an oracle, vary only how the code is represented, and score context against real issue resolution on SWE-bench Verified. The answer is starkly minimal. The signal lives in the code being edited itself: natural-language summaries of it answer almost none of the behavioral questions that the source answers ($4/45$ vs.\ $27/45$, held-out repositories, independent judge), and the gap belongs to the representation, not the summarizer -- a frontier model's summaries score exactly as poorly as a 3B model's. The surrounding context hardly matters either: across every multi-file instance in Verified, under a protocol frozen before any data, rendering a file's remainder as UML skeletons and signatures resolves no more issues than deleting that remainder outright ($N{=}70$, exact McNemar $p{=}0.75$). That was our registered hypothesis, and it failed. Compressed context, meanwhile, matches whole files at a third of the tokens: a resolved issue costs $19$K context tokens, not $94$K. The instrument also yielded a finding the field should keep: temperature-0 API inference flips ${\sim}9\%$ of per-instance outcomes between byte-identical runs. That is a noise floor under every small effect reported on this benchmark, including ours. We release the instrument -- gold-validated environments, per-instance proof that every reference edit is expressible from every arm's context, deterministic patch construction, and pre-registered hypotheses whose nulls we publish.

AI.tldr: Code editing agents need surprisingly little context - just the specific code being edited, with surrounding context and natural-language summaries providing minimal value.
AI.motivation: With modern coding agents having large context windows, the key question isn't how much context they can use, but what they actually need for effective code editing. Most of their reading is wasted.
AI.method: Separated finding work sites from acting on them, holding localization fixed with an oracle. Varied only code representation methods and scored context against real issue resolution on SWE-bench Verified. Used registered hypotheses, gold-validated environments, and deterministic patch construction.
AI.result: Minimal context needed: signal lives mainly in code being edited itself. Natural-language summaries performed poorly (4/45 vs 27/45).
