Surrounding context hardly matters (UML skeletons vs deletion showed no significant difference). Compressed context matches whole files at 1/3 tokens. Temperature-0 API inference introduces ~9% outcome variation.
AI.conclusion: Code editing agents require surprisingly little context beyond the specific code being edited. The field should be aware of the ~9% noise floor in benchmark evaluations due to API inference variations.

title: How Much Does Correctness Cost? Budgeted Placement of Strong Correctors in a Weak Multi-Agent Swarm
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 1 table.. Code and data: https://github.com/YehudaItkin/budgeted-oracle-placement
summary: A cheap swarm of unreliable agents can be steered to a correct consensus by a few strong, expensive "oracle" correctors. We ask how much one must spend, and where to place the oracles. We model the swarm as a consensus on a graph in which each oracle pins one node toward the truth at a cost-coupled, concave strength, and measure quality by the coherence H(R)=tr M(R)^{-1}. Our first result is that H stays submodular (each added oracle helps less than the last) even when the oracles differ in strength, so a cost-benefit greedy comes within 1-1/e of the best placement at any budget. Inverting the budget gives the budget-correctness frontier B*(eps), the least spend that guarantees an eps-correct consensus: closed-form on the complete graph, and a minimal oracle count k* when oracles cost the same. Whether a budget then buys a few strong oracles or many medium onese curvature of the cost-quality law: diminishing returns favour spreadsharply increasion. Measured onthe Qwen3 ladder (0.6-32B), the law is concave for math verificatio convex foremergent code tracing, so the verdict is genuinely task-dependent.https://github.com/YehudaItkin/budgeted-oracle-placemen

AI.tldr: This paper studies how to optimally place "oracle" correctors in a swarm of unreliable agents to achieve consensus, analyzing the cost-benefit trade-off between oracle strength, placement, and budget constraints.
AI.motivation: When using a cheap swarm of unreliable agents to reach consensus, a few strong "oracle" correctors can steer the system toward correct outcomes. The paper investigates how to minimize cost while maximizing correctness by determining optimal oracle placement and strength allocation.
AI.method: The authors model the swarm as consensus on a graph where each oracle pins a node toward truth with cost-coupled concave strength. They measure quality using coherence H(R)=tr M(R)^{-1}, prove H remains submodular even with varying oracle strengths, enabling greedy placement algorithms with performance guarantees.
AI.result: Key findings: 1) Submodularity property allows greedy algorithms to achieve (1-1/e) approximation of optimal placement at any budget; 2) Derives budget-correctness frontier B*(eps) with closed-form solution for complete graphs; 3) Identifies minimal oracle count k* for uniform-cost oracles; 4) Shows optimal strategy depends on cost-quality law curvature: diminishing returns favor spreading budget across more medium oracles, while increasing returns favor fewer strong ones.
AI.conclusion: The optimal oracle placement strategy is task-dependent, as evidenced by experimental results on Qwen3 ladder (0.6-32B parameters) showing concave cost-quality laws for mathematical verification but convex laws for emergent code tracing, highlighting that different tasks require different oracle allocation strategies.

title: Reference-Based Distillation Detection in LLMs
summary: Model distillation -- training on outputs from stronger third-party models -- is widely used to boost performance, but raises concerns about unfair advantages and policy violations. This motivates a fundamental question: can we detect whether a model was distilled from another? We show that, while identifying a teacher model from a student in isolation is highly challenging, it becomes tractable in a reference-based setting: given a model and an earlier-generation checkpoint from the same lineage, we can identify the teacher model used to train the later checkpoint. We introduce a distillation detection method based on reference-based membership inference. By comparing how strongly a student model preferentially aligns with outputs from different candidate teachers relative to a reference checkpoint, our method identifies the most likely teacher and detects evidence of distillation. To handle unknown distillation pipelines such as hidden prompts, we infer proxy prompt templates directly from model outputs. We additionally identify a distinctive glyph-level signal specific to o1/o3 models. Evaluating distillation detection is challenging because modern model lineages are already heavily entangled. To address this, we develop a hybrid evaluation spanning both controlled distillation experiments and real-world models. Across both settings, our approach recovers the true teacher with near-perfect accuracy in single-teacher distillation scenarios, even when the underlying distillation pipeline is largely unknown. We further introduce statistical tests for both teacher attribution and distillation detection, and extend our framework to open-world settings where no teacher is guaranteed to be present among the candidates. Applying our method to contemporary models yields new evidence regarding potential distillation relationships involving QwQ, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-OSS.

AI.tldr: A method for detecting whether a model was distilled from another using reference-based membership inference, comparing alignment with candidate teachers relative to earlier checkpoints, achieving near-perfect accuracy in identifying teachers even with unknown distillation pipelines.
AI.motivation: Model distillation raises concerns about unfair advantages and policy violations, creating a need to detect whether models were trained on outputs from third-party models to ensure proper attribution and compliance.
AI.method: Reference-based membership inference that compares how strongly a student model aligns with outputs from candidate teachers relative to an earlier checkpoint from the same lineage. The approach infers proxy prompt templates from model outputs to handle unknown distillation pipelines and identifies distinctive glyph-level signals specific to certain models.
AI.result: Achieves near-perfect accuracy in recovering true teachers in single-teacher distillation scenarios across both controlled experiments and real-world models. Successfully applied to contemporary models like QwQ, DeepSeek-R1, and GPT-OSS, revealing potential distillation relationships.
AI.conclusion: Distillation detection is feasible through reference-based approaches, providing tools for teacher attribution and statistical detection tests, addressing concerns about model training provenance and policy compliance in the AI ecosystem.

title: Norm Enforcement for AI Agents: Robustly Shaping Behavior in Multi-Agent Systems
comment: ICML2026 Trustworthy AI for Good Workshop
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in shared environments where they pursue diverse goals and compete for rewards. This multi-agent competition can lead to behaviors that serve individual gains at collective cost -- for instance, marketing agents may post misleading content as a result of competing for engagement on social media. Human societies address such problems through norms that constrain acceptable behavior, supported by enforcement mechanisms that detect and penalize violations. Motivated by this, we study norm enforcement mechanisms for language model agents. We find that simple enforcement mechanisms are exploited by misaligned agents for competitive advantage, even when they are not explicitly trained or prompted to do so. We thus turn our attention to designing more robust mechanisms, and identify two key ingredients: estimating each agent's reliability over time, and updating this estimate with escalating penalties for repeated misbehavior. Across three simulated environments and a variety of agent populations, mechanisms built on these principles resist exploitation, while still penalizing norm violations at comparable or lower cost than baselines. Our results position norm enforcement mechanisms as scalable levers for shaping agents' behavior, but only when designed to anticipate becoming part of the system they govern. Our code and data are available at https://yaowenye.com/norm-enforcement.

AI.tldr: Simple AI norm enforcement mechanisms are vulnerable to exploitation by competitive agents, but robust mechanisms using reliability estimation and escalating penalties can resist exploitation while effectively penalizing violations.
AI.motivation: AI agents in competitive multi-agent environments can develop behaviors that benefit individuals at collective cost (like misleading content for engagement). Human societies use norms with enforcement mechanisms, so the authors aim to design effective norm enforcement for language model agents.
AI.method: The authors study simple norm enforcement mechanisms first, find they're exploited by misaligned agents, then design robust mechanisms with two key components: estimating each agent's reliability over time, and updating estimates with escalating penalties for repeated misbehavior. They test these across three simulated environments with various agent populations.
AI.result: Mechanisms based on reliability estimation and escalating penalties resist exploitation while still penalizing norm violations at comparable or lower cost than baseline methods. The effectiveness is demonstrated across multiple simulated environments.
AI.conclusion: Norm enforcement mechanisms can serve as scalable levers for shaping AI agent behavior, but only when designed to anticipate becoming part of the competitive system they govern (i.e., resistant to exploitation attempts).

title: Depth-Entropy Guided Sampling for Training-Free LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become the dominant paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, but it requires expensive training, curated data, and reward signals. Recent work shows that sampling from sharpened base-model distributions at test time recovers much of the RL gain, yet existing methods rely solely on output-layer likelihoods and ignore the transformer's internal forward-pass dynamics. We introduce Depth-Entropy Guided Sampling (DEGS), a training-free, test-time method that exploits layer-wise entropy collapse as an intrinsic quality signal. We observe that stronger reasoners -- including RL-posttrained variants -- exhibit a distinctive "late collapse": logit-lens decoded entropy stays elevated until deeper layers before converging. We define a per-sequence collapse depth $D(\mathbf{x})$ and a joint objective $π(\mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x})^α\exp(βD(\mathbf{x}))$ that combines sequence likelihood with this depth-entropy structure, instantiated inside an MCMC power-sampling framework (DEGS-MCMC). Across three open-weight models and four reasoning benchmarks, this near-chance per-candidate signal compounds over the sampling trajectory into state-of-the-art training-free accuracy, with gains largest out of domain and on the harder splits -- exactly where likelihood alone falls short -- at single-digit-percent wall-clock overhead. DEGS narrowly trails an in-house GRPO reference on the math splits GRPO was trained for, yet surpasses it out of domain on GPQA for all three models, without any training, reward model, or labeled data.

AI.tldr: DEGS is a training-free test-time sampling method that improves reasoning in LLMs by exploiting layer-wise entropy collapse patterns observed in strong reasoners, combining sequence likelihood with entropy structure via MCMC power-sampling.
AI.motivation: Current RL methods for improving LLM reasoning are expensive and require training data and reward signals. While recent work shows test-time sampling from sharpened distributions recovers RL gains, existing methods only use output-layer likelihoods and ignore internal transformer dynamics during forward passes.
AI.method: Proposes Depth-Entropy Guided Sampling (DEGS), which observes that stronger reasoners exhibit "late collapse" where logit-lens decoded entropy stays elevated until deeper layers before converging. Defines per-sequence collapse depth D(x) and creates joint objective π(x) ∝ p(x)^α exp(βD(x)) combining sequence likelihood with depth-entropy structure, implemented via MCMC power-sampling framework (DEGS-MCMC).
AI.result: Across three open-weight models and four reasoning benchmarks, DEGS achieves state-of-the-art training-free accuracy with largest gains out of domain and on harder splits where likelihood alone fails, with single-digit-percent wall-clock overhead. Narrowly trails in-house GRPO on math splits it was trained for, but surpasses it out of domain on GPQA for all three models without any training, reward model, or labeled data.
AI.conclusion: DEGS demonstrates that transformer internal dynamics provide valuable quality signals for reasoning, enabling training-free improvements by exploiting layer-wise entropy collapse patterns. The method offers an efficient alternative to expensive RL training while achieving comparable or better performance, especially in out-of-domain settings.

title: Verification of Adaptive Agentic Controllers through Finite Rule Revision
summary: Industrial agentic AI systems increasingly exhibit a gap between prototype capability and production deployment. In particular, adaptive agents may generate plausible outputs while remaining difficult to verify under non-determinism, confidentiality constraints, limited context, and weak observability. This paper formulates a bounded verification protocol for adaptive agentic controllers represented by finite symbolic rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation. The central research question is: when an adaptive agentic controller is represented through finite rules, explicit diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation, which classes of controller failure can be detected, locally repaired, or rejected without relying on unrestricted human-in-the-loop judgment? The proposed framework treats the controller as a finite revisable object. Diagnostic failures are mapped to predefined rule-level edits, including rule addition, rule deletion, and priority revision. Repaired controllers are then evaluated on held-out simulation seeds or cloned initial states. Experiments in a stylized financially constrained inventory-control benchmark show three outcomes: resource-induced failures that remain non-repairable by one rule edit, partial repairs that are rejected because they violate thresholds or guardrails, and a local one-step repair of an order-volatility failure induced by removing a smoothing rule. The contribution is methodological and provides a simulation-compatible procedure for testing whether specific controller-level failures can be made observable, explainable, locally revisable, and empirically re-tested under controlled conditions.

AI.tldr: A bounded verification protocol for adaptive agentic AI systems that uses finite symbolic rules, diagnostic predicates, explanation logs, and held-out re-evaluation to detect, repair, or reject controller failures without requiring full human intervention.
AI.motivation: Industrial agentic AI systems face a deployment gap where adaptive agents produce plausible outputs but are difficult to verify under non-determinism, confidentiality constraints, limited context, and weak observability conditions.
AI.method: Treats adaptive agentic controllers as finite revisable objects with symbolic rules, diagnostic predicates, and explanation logs. Maps diagnostic failures to rule-level edits (addition, deletion, priority revision) and evaluates repaired controllers on held-out simulation seeds or cloned initial states.
AI.result: In a financially constrained inventory-control benchmark, experiments show: 1) resource-induced failures that remain non-repairable by one rule edit, 2) partial repairs rejected for violating thresholds/guardrails, and 3) successful local one-step repair of an order-volatility failure by removing a smoothing rule.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a simulation-compatible procedure for making specific controller-level failures observable, explainable, locally revisable, and empirically re-testable under controlled conditions, addressing the verification gap in industrial agentic AI deployment.

title: AGM-like Paraconsistent Partial Meet Abductive Expansion Operation
summary: In his 1996 doctoral thesis, Maurice Pagnucco created the first AGM-like abductive expansion operation. Taking his operation as a basis, as well as a taxonomy -- inspired by Atocha Aliseda -- responsible for highlighting and formalizing the main components of abductive reasoning, the main aim of this paper is to present a new paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation -- capable of assimilating contradictory explanatory hypotheses without trivialization and the consequent absurd epistemic state -- with its postulates and its transitively relational partial meet construction. To a large extent, the formal development presented in this paper was only made possible by the recent creation of the paraconsistent logic RCbr, an LFI (Logics of Formal Inconsistencies) that establishes properties especially relevant to belief revision contexts, in particular, the ability to be self-extensional -- i.e., to satisfy the replacement property. This is the first of two papers: the paraconsistent abductive expansion operation announced here -- which is part of a new system called AGMpabd -- despite bringing many interesting features, does not assign any relevant epistemic role to the paraconsistent operators of negation and consistency. Only in a second paper will an analogous paraconsistent abductive expansion operation -- which is part of another new system, AGMcircabd -- be enhanced in this direction. Nevertheless, to the best of my knowledge, the operation developed in this paper is the first of its kind in the AGM literature.

AI.tldr: A paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation is developed that can handle contradictory explanatory hypotheses without logical explosion, building on Pagnucco's original work and using the RCbr logic system.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to create an abductive reasoning operation that can assimilate contradictory explanations without leading to trivialization/absurd epistemic states, addressing a gap in existing AGM belief revision frameworks that collapse when faced with contradictions.
AI.method: The approach builds on Pagnucco's abductive expansion operation and uses Aliseda's taxonomy for abductive reasoning. It develops a paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation (AGMpabd) using the RCbr logic (an LFI - Logic of Formal Inconsistencies) which has self-extensional properties and the replacement property crucial for belief revision contexts.
AI.result: The paper presents the first paraconsistent AGM-like abductive expansion operation in the literature, with formal postulates and a transitively relational partial meet construction. The operation can handle contradictory hypotheses without trivialization, though it doesn't yet assign epistemic roles to paraconsistent negation/consistency operators (reserved for a follow-up paper AGMcircabd).
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the foundation for paraconsistent abductive reasoning in AGM belief revision, overcoming the limitation of classical systems that cannot accommodate contradictory evidence without collapse. It represents an important first step toward more robust epistemic reasoning systems that can handle inconsistency.

title: Low-Rank Attention Residuals
summary: Attention Residuals replace the fixed residual sum with depthwise attention over previous sub-layer outputs in large language models (LLMs), but use each output as both a full-dimensional key and value. This couples routing with representation and makes depth-routing scores scale with the hidden width $d$. We propose Low-Rank Attention Residuals (LR-AttnRes), which keep full-dimensional residual values while using $r$-dimensional keys, with $r \ll d$, for routing. Projected LR-AttnRes emits learned low-rank keys from existing output projections, decoupling routing from residual content and achieving the best validation loss among the variants tested. Sliced LR-AttnRes uses the last $r$ dimensions of each value as the routing key, removing the auxiliary key-projection path and reducing residual-side FLOPs while still improving performance. Comprehensive sweeps show that depthwise routing can be effective with far fewer dimensions than the model width. We release code and models to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: LR-AttnRes reduces computational overhead of attention residuals by using low-rank keys (r << d) instead of full-dimensional keys for routing, achieving better performance with fewer FLOPs.
AI.motivation: Current Attention Residuals use full-dimensional outputs as both keys and values, which couples routing with representation and makes depth-routing scores scale with hidden width d, leading to computational inefficiency.
AI.method: Proposes Low-Rank Attention Residuals (LR-AttnRes) with two variants: (1) Projected LR-AttnRes uses learned low-rank keys from existing output projections to decouple routing from residual content, and (2) Sliced LR-AttnRes uses the last r dimensions of each value as routing keys to eliminate auxiliary projections.
AI.result: Comprehensive sweeps show depthwise routing can be effective with far fewer dimensions than model width; Projected LR-AttnRes achieves best validation loss, while Sliced LR-AttnRes reduces residual-side FLOPs while still improving performance.
AI.conclusion: Low-rank attention residuals provide an efficient alternative to full-dimensional routing, demonstrating that depthwise routing effectiveness doesn't require dimensionality matching with model width, with both variants offering computational benefits.

title: Agentic Context Learning with Self-Discovered Specification
summary: Context learning is an emerging inference-time task where LLMs must learn and apply novel, task-specific knowledge from intricate contexts absent from pre-training; even frontier models score under 24% task success. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive empirical study to understand why this setting remains difficult. A natural hypothesis is that failures stem from content access; yet across twelve retrieval, reflection, and verification baselines on CL-Bench, an extensive context learning benchmark, we find limited gains over direct full-context prompting. Further failure analysis reveals a key finding: unlike typical long-context tasks such as long document understanding, context learning requires not only recovering local content but also acquiring local specifications that are often unspecified in the query but distributed across the context: domain-specific formats, local rules, and completeness conditions. Across all 31,592 rubric items, we find that 55.4% clearly evaluate specification acquisition, while only 22.6% evaluate content acquisition. Moreover, despite 76.7% of specifications being unspecified in the user query, 95.5% are traceable to the context, indicating these are learnable obligations rather than hidden requirements. To validate this diagnosis, we design a deliberately simple intervention PSCI (private specification-contract induction) which extracts local specifications and enforces them through adversarial checking and repair; PSCI achieves state-of-the-art 28.14% with GPT-5.1 (+5.59 pp absolute and +24.8% relative) on CL-Bench, replicated on Qwen3.5-27B (+5.28 pp) and Gemini 3 Pro (+6.17 pp). Seventeen ablations further isolate the role of task-specific specifications. Overall, our results suggest context learning hinges on not only content acquisition but also specification acquisition.

AI.tldr: Context learning is difficult for LLMs because they fail to acquire local specifications (domain-specific formats, rules, completeness conditions) distributed across the context, not just content. A simple intervention (PSCI) that extracts and enforces these specifications significantly improves performance.
AI.motivation: Context learning remains challenging for LLMs, with frontier models scoring under 24% task success. The paper investigates why this setting is difficult, questioning whether failures stem from content access issues or deeper problems with acquiring local specifications.
AI.method: Conducted comprehensive empirical study using CL-Bench benchmark with 12 retrieval, reflection, and verification baselines. Analyzed 31,592 rubric items to quantify specification vs. content acquisition requirements. Introduced PSCI (private specification-contract induction) intervention that extracts local specifications from context and enforces them through adversarial checking and repair.
AI.result: 55.4% of rubric items evaluate specification acquisition, while only 22.6% evaluate content acquisition. 76.7% of specifications are unspecified in user queries but 95.5% are traceable to context. PSCI achieves state-of-the-art 28.14% (+5.59 pp, +24.8% relative) on CL-Bench with GPT-5.1, with similar improvements on Qwen3.5-27B (+5.28 pp) and Gemini 3 Pro (+6.17 pp). 17 ablations confirm the importance of task-specific specifications.
AI.conclusion: Context learning success hinges on specification acquisition, not just content acquisition. Local specifications (domain-specific formats, rules, completeness conditions) distributed across context are critical learnable obligations. The PSCI intervention demonstrates that explicit extraction and enforcement of these specifications significantly improves LLM performance in context learning tasks.

title: Coresets Before Score Sets: Evaluation-Unsupervised Prompt Subset Selection for LLM Benchmarks
summary: We study LLM benchmark coreset selection: selecting a small subset of prompts over multiple benchmarks whose induced model scores and rankings approximate those obtained from the full benchmark suite. In evaluation-unsupervised benchmark coreset selection (our approach), the selection algorithm uses no model evaluation outcomes, and operates on a fine granularity by producing subsets of prompts over multiple benchmarks rather than producing a sub-collection of entire benchmarks. We use submodular subset selection, and we develop and evaluate many different submodular functions for this purpose, including determinantal point process (DPP) based approaches, submodular mutual information functions, and facility location-based functions. On a new large-scale suite of 35 heterogeneous benchmarks spanning five different capability categories, 18 frontier LLMs, and over 61K prompts, we find that the facility location (FL) function operating exclusively on inexpensive semantic prompt embeddings preserves LLM scores better than twelve separate score-based and diversity-based baselines, across a range of coreset budgets. Moreover, we show our proposed objective is not limited to the evaluation-unsupervised regime: in the setting where only a handful of whole benchmarks must be selected and a large amount of model scores are available, the same objective matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the MMLU and MTEB leaderboards, while being substantially cheaper to compute. Together, our results suggest that submodularity, in general, is a strong and reliable tool for benchmark compression.

AI.tldr: Submodular subset selection enables efficient benchmark compression by selecting small prompt subsets that preserve model performance rankings without requiring model evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM benchmarking is computationally expensive, requiring evaluation on thousands of prompts across multiple benchmarks. There's a need for efficient benchmark coreset selection that can approximate full benchmark results while reducing computational costs.
AI.method: The paper proposes evaluation-unsupervised benchmark coreset selection using submodular subset selection algorithms. They explore various submodular functions including determinantal point processes (DPP), submodular mutual information functions, and facility location (FL) functions operating on semantic prompt embeddings.
AI.result: Facility location function using semantic prompt embeddings outperforms 12 baseline methods across 35 benchmarks, 18 LLMs, and 61K prompts. The approach preserves LLM scores better than diversity-based and score-based baselines across different coreset budgets and is also effective when model scores are available.
AI.conclusion: Submodularity is a strong and reliable tool for benchmark compression that enables efficient LLM evaluation while preserving accurate model rankings, applicable in both evaluation-unsupervised and score-aware settings.

title: FedCausal-Dyn: A Causal-Dynamic Paradigm for Federated Learning under Dynamic Feature Drift
summary: This paper addresses the challenging problem of dynamic feature drift in federated learning, where data distributions evolve across clients and over time -- a common scenario in real-world applications like financial technology. Existing approaches often assume static drift, limiting their effectiveness in non-stationary environments. To overcome this, we propose \textbf{FedCausal-Dyn}, a novel federated learning framework built on a causal-dynamic paradigm. Its key innovation is \textit{causal-domain feature separation}, which disentangles domain-invariant causal features from spurious, domain-specific variations via specialized projection heads and adversarial training. This enables \textit{reliable and dynamic prototype aggregation}, weighting local class prototypes by estimated reliability before global aggregation. We further introduce \textit{causal-feature guided collaborative regularization}, unifying prototype contrastive alignment and domain invariance into a cohesive objective. Extensive experiments on three federated domain generalization benchmarks demonstrate that FedCausal-Dyn consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with the highest average accuracy and the most stable results. Ablation studies confirm each component's critical contribution. Our work provides a robust and principled solution for federated learning under dynamic feature drift.

AI.tldr: FedCausal-Dyn: A novel federated learning framework using causal-domain feature separation and dynamic prototype aggregation to handle dynamic feature drift across clients and over time, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world federated learning applications (like fintech) experience dynamic feature drift where data distributions evolve across clients and over time. Existing approaches assume static drift, limiting their effectiveness in non-stationary environments.
AI.method: Proposes FedCausal-Dyn with three key components: 1) Causal-domain feature separation disentangles domain-invariant causal features from spurious domain-specific variations using specialized projection heads and adversarial training; 2) Reliable and dynamic prototype aggregation weights local class prototypes by estimated reliability before global aggregation; 3) Causal-feature guided collaborative regularization unifies prototype contrastive alignment and domain invariance into a cohesive objective.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three federated domain generalization benchmarks show FedCausal-Dyn consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance with highest average accuracy and most stable results. Ablation studies confirm each component's critical contribution.
AI.conclusion: FedCausal-Dyn provides a robust and principled solution for federated learning under dynamic feature drift, addressing limitations of existing static drift assumptions with a causal-dynamic paradigm that effectively handles evolving data distributions in real-world scenarios.

title: Who&When Pro: Can LLMs Really Attribute Failures in AI Agents?
summary: Automated failure attribution uses LLMs to identify where and why agentic systems fail. As agents become more capable, their failures become subtler, making automated attribution increasingly important. We introduce Who&When Pro, a large-scale benchmark for automated failure attribution in agentic systems. Using a strictly controlled pipeline that injects a failure only after exactly replaying a successful prefix, we construct 12,326 failed trajectories with golden labels across 3 modalities and 26 benchmarks covering various scenarios. Beyond benchmarking, we conduct extensive experiments and analyses, revealing systematic patterns in how models attribute failures across modalities, protocols, and model families, and providing empirical guidance for future automated failure attribution systems.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called Who&When Pro for automated failure attribution in AI agents, featuring 12,326 failed trajectories with golden labels across 3 modalities and 26 scenarios.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become more capable, their failures become subtler and harder to detect, making automated failure attribution increasingly important for diagnosing and improving agentic systems.
AI.method: Created a strictly controlled pipeline that injects failures only after exactly replaying a successful prefix, ensuring clean failure attribution. Built a large-scale benchmark with 12,326 failed trajectories across 3 modalities (text, visual, multimodal) and 26 different benchmarks covering various scenarios.
AI.result: Revealed systematic patterns in how models attribute failures across modalities, protocols, and model families. Provided empirical guidance for future automated failure attribution systems through extensive experiments and analyses.
AI.conclusion: Who&When Pro serves as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating automated failure attribution in agentic systems, offering insights into failure patterns and practical guidance for building better attribution systems.

title: A Dynamic Scene Interaction Reasoning Framework for Scene-level Lane-Change Intention and Trajectory Prediction of Multiple Interacting Vehicles
summary: Safe motion planning in advanced driver-assistance systems and autonomous vehicles requires an accurate understanding of how the surrounding traffic scene is likely to evolve. However, many existing lane-change prediction methods remain centered on a single target vehicle, while multi-agent forecasting approaches often describe scene evolution only through future positions and provide limited explicit information about the maneuver associated with each vehicle. This study proposes a dynamic scene graph attention framework that predicts the lane-change intention and future trajectory of every relevant vehicle within a local traffic scene. The scene is represented as a time-varying interaction graph in which vehicles are modeled as nodes and their spatial and kinematic relationships are encoded through explicit edge features. Temporal graph-attention message passing captures evolving inter-vehicle dependencies and pre-maneuver cues, while an intention-guided decoder links each predicted maneuver to its corresponding future motion. A scene-level consistency objective further encourages compatible multi-vehicle futures. Experiments on the NGSIM I-80, NGSIM US-101, and highD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines. DSiGAT achieves intention prediction accuracies of 90.12% and 90.97% on NGSIM I-80 and US-101, respectively, and reduces trajectory RMSE by up to 52.94% relative to the strongest baseline. It also produces lower inter-agent collision rates and joint displacement errors, indicating more coherent scene-level predictions. Ablation, sensitivity, robustness, and qualitative analyses further validate the contribution of the proposed components and the effectiveness of the scene-focused formulation.

AI.tldr: Dynamic scene graph attention framework predicts lane-change intentions and future trajectories for all vehicles in traffic scenes, outperforming existing methods with improved accuracy and coherence.
AI.motivation: Existing lane-change prediction methods focus on single vehicles, while multi-agent forecasting often provides limited explicit maneuver information. Safe motion planning requires understanding how entire traffic scenes evolve with compatible multi-vehicle futures.
AI.method: Uses dynamic scene graph attention framework with time-varying interaction graphs where vehicles are nodes and spatial/kinematic relationships are edge features. Temporal graph-attention message passing captures evolving dependencies, with intention-guided decoder linking maneuvers to future motion and scene-level consistency objective for compatible predictions.
AI.result: Achieves intention prediction accuracies of 90.12% (NGSIM I-80) and 90.97% (NGSIM US-101), reduces trajectory RMSE by up to 52.94% relative to strongest baseline, produces lower inter-agent collision rates and joint displacement errors indicating more coherent scene-level predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scene-focused formulation with dynamic graph attention effectively captures multi-vehicle interactions and produces accurate, consistent traffic scene predictions, validated through comprehensive experiments and analyses.

title: Mitigating Early Training Collapse in CTR Models
summary: Deep neural models for click-through rate prediction often exhibit a sharp decline in validation performance immediately after the first training epoch despite continued improvement in training loss. This instability restricts effective learning and limits model performance. In this study, we analyze this behavior using large-scale industrial datasets and evaluate practical mitigation strategies. While reducing the learning rate provides only incremental gains, controlling feature sparsity yields substantial improvements. Removing highly sparse features and aggregating infrequent feature values stabilizes training, extends useful learning beyond a single epoch, and improves both offline evaluation metrics and online system performance.

AI.tldr: Reducing feature sparsity stabilizes CTR model training, preventing the sharp performance drop after first epoch and improving both offline and online metrics.
AI.motivation: Deep neural models for CTR prediction often show a sharp decline in validation performance immediately after the first training epoch despite continued improvement in training loss, which restricts effective learning and limits model performance.
AI.method: Analyzed this behavior using large-scale industrial datasets and evaluated practical mitigation strategies including reducing learning rate and controlling feature sparsity through removing highly sparse features and aggregating infrequent feature values.
AI.result: While reducing learning rate provided only incremental gains, controlling feature sparsity yielded substantial improvements: it stabilized training, extended useful learning beyond a single epoch, and improved both offline evaluation metrics and online system performance.
AI.conclusion: Feature sparsity control is an effective strategy for stabilizing CTR model training and improving overall performance, outperforming simple learning rate adjustments.

title: Can Agentic Trading Systems Pay for Their Own Intelligence?
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used in trading systems, where model reasoning, tool use, and continual decisions incur costs that are expected to produce trading value. Existing evaluations typically report performance metrics, but rarely examine agentic viability: whether dynamic LLM-mediated decisions convert their induced costs into measurable incremental profit. To apply this criterion, we introduce TradeLens, a trace-grounded diagnostic toolkit for evaluating agentic trading systems from their trading records, runtime traces, and deployment configurations. It reconstructs trading trajectories, attributes profit and cost to interpretable evidence, and diagnoses whether and why an agent pays for its own intelligence. We conduct extensive analysis across backbone models, capital scales, trading frequencies, and system architectures, together with deployment discussion. Our results show that viability hinges on intelligence-to-profit conversion: models exhibit different failure patterns, such as poor asset selection in DeepSeek-V3.2 and negative timing in GLM-4.7, while capital scale, trading frequency, and architecture matter only by amplifying or degrading decision-attributed timing value. These findings reframe the evaluation of LLM-based trading agents from capability-centric performance ranking to trace-grounded diagnosis of intelligence-to-profit conversion. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TradeLens.

AI.tldr: TradeLens is a diagnostic toolkit that evaluates LLM-based trading agents by analyzing whether their dynamic decisions and incurred costs actually convert into measurable profit, shifting focus from performance metrics to intelligence-to-profit conversion.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM trading agents typically report performance metrics but fail to examine whether the costs of model reasoning, tool use, and continual decisions actually produce incremental trading value. There's a need to assess "agentic viability" - whether LLM-mediated decisions convert their induced costs into measurable profit.
AI.method: TradeLens is a trace-grounded diagnostic toolkit that reconstructs trading trajectories from trading records, runtime traces, and deployment configurations. It attributes profit and cost to interpretable evidence, and diagnoses whether an agent pays for its own intelligence through systematic analysis.
AI.result: Analysis across backbone models, capital scales, trading frequencies, and system architectures shows viability depends on intelligence-to-profit conversion. Different models exhibit specific failure patterns: DeepSeek-V3.2 shows poor asset selection while GLM-4.7 has negative timing. Capital scale, trading frequency, and architecture mainly affect decision-attributed timing value rather than viability directly.
AI.conclusion: The findings reframe LLM-based trading agent evaluation from capability-centric performance ranking to trace-grounded diagnosis of intelligence-to-profit conversion. TradeLens enables systematic assessment of whether trading agents actually justify their operational costs through measurable profit generation.

title: Scaffolding the Strategist: Architecture-Dependent Reasoning Interventions in Hotelling Spatial Markets
comment: 26 pages (11 main + 15 appendix), 6 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on LLM Reasoning
summary: We investigate whether structured reasoning interventions improve the strategic economic reasoning of large language models, and whether their effects depend on model architecture. Using Hotelling's linear city model as a diagnostic vehicle, we evaluate GPT-4.1-mini (a standard instruction-following model) and GPT-5-mini (a reasoning-optimized model) under five conditions - an unscaffolded baseline and four reasoning interventions - across eight questions spanning deductive and abductive reasoning, three prompt framings, and three repetitions per condition, yielding 720 individually judged responses. We find a statistically significant crossover interaction between scaffolding type and model architecture ($t(7) = 4.79$, $p = 0.002$, $d = 1.69$): commitment scaffolding improves the standard model ($+0.21$) while degrading the reasoning model ($-0.63$), and principled separation shows the opposite pattern ($-0.40$ vs. $+0.31$). Both crossovers are individually significant (commitment: $p = 0.040$; separation: $p = 0.002$) and hold across all eight questions with 7/8 directional consistency. Adversarial stress-testing harms both models, with $2.6\times$ greater degradation for the reasoning model ($-1.47$ vs. $-0.57$; $p = 0.038$), and the damage correlates negatively with baseline difficulty ($R^2 = 0.36$, $p = 0.014$). We further document a persistent declarative-procedural gap in which both models identify correct strategies at rates far exceeding their ability to execute them; separation fully closes this gap for the reasoning model while no intervention helps the standard model.

AI.tldr: Structured reasoning interventions have architecture-dependent effects on LLMs' economic reasoning, with different scaffolding techniques helping or harming different model types.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether structured reasoning interventions improve strategic economic reasoning in LLMs, and whether these effects depend on model architecture.
AI.method: Used Hotelling's linear city model as diagnostic vehicle to evaluate GPT-4.1-mini (standard instruction-following) and GPT-5-mini (reasoning-optimized) under five conditions: unscaffolded baseline plus four reasoning interventions. Tested across eight deductive/abductive reasoning questions, three prompt framings, three repetitions per condition, yielding 720 individually judged responses.
AI.result: Found statistically significant crossover interaction: commitment scaffolding improved standard model (+0.21) but degraded reasoning model (-0.63), while principled separation showed opposite pattern (-0.40 vs. +0.31). Adversarial stress-testing harmed both models but 2.6× more for reasoning model. Persistent declarative-procedural gap exists where models identify correct strategies better than executing them; separation closed this gap for reasoning model only.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning interventions have architecture-specific effects - what helps one model can harm another. There's no universal reasoning scaffold; effectiveness depends on underlying model architecture and reasoning capabilities.

title: Safe responses matter: Output-aware safety guardrail mitigate over-refusal in MLLMs
summary: Existing safety mechanisms for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) face a fundamental trade-off between safety and utility. Model fine-tuning achieves robust safety but compromises general utility. Input-side safety guardrails offer a lightweight alternative, yet they suffer from severe over-refusal, indiscriminately blocking benign queries or those the model could have safely answered through refusal or advisory responses. We identify that the root cause of over-refusal lies in the input-aware paradigm: safety guardrails make safety decisions without considering whether the model itself is capable of generating safe responses. Usually, MLLMs already possess intrinsic safety mechanisms that can transform harmful inputs into harmless outputs, but input-side safety guardrails override this capability, degrading user experience. Motivated by this insight, we propose a paradigm shift toward output-aware safety guardrails. Our method operates within the model's hidden state space to predict whether the forthcoming generation will be unsafe before it is fully produced. By training a lightweight classifier via multi-instance contrastive learning on hidden state representations, our approach distinguishes between inputs that will lead to unsafe outputs and those that will not, even when the inputs themselves contain risky elements. This enables precise intervention only when the model's actual response would be harmful. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our output-aware safety guardrail matches the safety performance of existing methods while drastically reducing over-refusal, preserving the model's utility and built-in safety capabilities. Code is available at: https://github.com/kunzhan/OutGuard

AI.tldr: Output-aware safety guardrail for MLLMs that predicts unsafe outputs from hidden states to reduce over-refusal while maintaining safety.
AI.motivation: Current safety methods for multimodal LLMs face a trade-off: fine-tuning hurts utility, while input-side guardrails cause over-refusal by blocking queries that models could handle safely. The root problem is making safety decisions without considering if the model can generate safe responses itself.
AI.method: Proposes output-aware safety guardrails that operate in the model's hidden state space. Uses a lightweight classifier trained via multi-instance contrastive learning on hidden state representations to predict whether forthcoming generations will be unsafe before full production.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the output-aware safety guardrail matches existing methods' safety performance while drastically reducing over-refusal, preserving model utility and built-in safety capabilities.
AI.conclusion: A paradigm shift from input-aware to output-aware safety guardrails enables precise intervention only when necessary, avoiding over-refusal while maintaining safety, thus better balancing safety and utility for multimodal LLMs.

title: WattCouncil: Context-Aware Household Energy Scenario Generation With Governed LLMs
summary: The accelerating shift toward low-carbon power systems, together with the widespread adoption of behind-the-meter technologies such as rooftop solar and electric vehicles, is placing new operational and analytical demands on electricity grids. At the same time, smart-grid research increasingly relies on machine learning (ML), yet progress is constrained by limited access to high-resolution household energy data due to privacy concerns, regulatory barriers, and collection costs. This work presents WattCouncil, a data-generation framework in which household electricity demand is generated by a council of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents operating in specialized roles to generate, audit, and validate structured energy scenarios under explicit cultural, temporal, and physical constraints. Rather than acting as static predictors, these agents serve as adaptive decision-makers within a governed pipeline. Motivated by studies highlighting the importance of contextual factors in energy use, our framework produces context-sensitive daily routines through a guided reasoning process that incorporates household composition, temporal factors, and environmental conditions. We evaluate the generated profiles against the detailed CER dataset, which contains over a year of load measurements for 4232 households together with survey-based socio-economic information. We further assess the consistency of the framework through ablation studies. Source code is available at https://github.com/Singularity-AI-Lab/wattcouncil

AI.tldr: WattCouncil is an LLM-based agent framework that generates realistic household electricity demand profiles by simulating daily routines with cultural, temporal, and physical constraints, addressing data scarcity in smart-grid research.
AI.motivation: Smart-grid research faces limited access to high-resolution household energy data due to privacy concerns, regulatory barriers, and collection costs, while the shift to low-carbon systems and behind-the-meter technologies increases the need for such data.
AI.method: A council of LLM-based agents operates in specialized roles (generation, auditing, validation) to produce context-sensitive daily energy routines incorporating household composition, temporal factors, and environmental conditions through guided reasoning under explicit constraints.
AI.result: The framework generates realistic electricity demand profiles evaluated against the detailed CER dataset (4232 households over a year with socio-economic data) and demonstrates consistency through ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: WattCouncil provides a novel approach to generating synthetic yet realistic household energy data, addressing data scarcity in smart-grid ML research while maintaining contextual relevance through structured agent-based simulation.

title: A Theory of Least Autonomy in AI
summary:
Least privilege, the principle that an identity should hold only the permissions strictly required for its task, has been a foundational primitive of access control for decades. We argue that this principle is insufficient for agentic AI systems, which do not merely hold permissions but can combine, approve, and amplify them across workflows and system boundaries.
  We propose least autonomy as an appropriate generalization and develop a formal theory. First, we define a compositional blast radius d(a,b) that measures structural separation between actions in an enterprise hierarchy, combining an ultrametric tree with lattice-valued confidentiality, integrity, and control-context labels. Second, we define a directed agent influence graph G(theta). An arc from U to V requires a directed shared-resource write-to-read meeting or a conservative undirected agent-to-agent (A2A) communication meeting, and a meeting-conditioned influence potential at or above an externally selected policy threshold theta. A catalogue-radius profile supports calibration and audit of theta. Finally, we define a collusion predicate over graph reachability that detects authorization composition, decision manipulation, and cross-domain capability composition.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "least autonomy" as a replacement for the classic "least privilege" principle for agentic AI systems, introducing formal measures of structural separation and agent influence to detect collusion risks across workflows.
AI.motivation: Traditional least privilege principle is insufficient for agentic AI systems because these systems don't just hold permissions but can combine, approve, and amplify them across workflows and system boundaries, creating new security risks.
AI.method: 1) Define compositional blast radius d(a,b) combining ultrametric tree structure with lattice-valued confidentiality/integrity/control labels. 2) Define directed agent influence graph G(theta) based on shared-resource write-to-read and agent-to-agent communication meetings. 3) Define collusion predicate over graph reachability to detect authorization composition, decision manipulation, and cross-domain capability composition.
AI.result: Developed formal theory of least autonomy with practical tools: structural separation measure, agent influence graphs with threshold policy calibration, and collusion detection mechanisms for enterprise AI systems.
AI.conclusion: Least autonomy provides a more appropriate security principle than least privilege for agentic AI systems, offering formal methods to analyze and control how AI agents can combine and amplify permissions across complex workflows.

title: Quantum-Inspired Contextual Learning for Sparse-Ring Fraud Detection in Dynamic Transaction Graphs
summary: We present an exploratory benchmark and quantum-inspired modeling prototype for fraud screening in dynamic financial transaction graphs. Coordinated fraud may not be visible from individual transactions alone, but may emerge as a multi-period relational pattern. We focus on sparse-ring fraud, a stylized pattern in which a completed directed cycle is distributed across several days, requiring models to integrate evidence across both time and graph structure. We study this problem using a synthetic transaction simulator with completed sparse-ring injections and broken-ring decoys. Daily directed transaction graphs are aggregated into rolling windows and represented using raw graph features, persistent-homology summaries, or hybrid feature vectors that combine both. We compare a gated recurrent unit (GRU) baseline with quantum-inspired Contextual Machine Learning (CML) as sequence-level classifiers. Because the benchmark uses synthetic data, a modest sample size, and sequence-level labels, the results are exploratory. Within this scope, topology-only summaries are too compressed to solve the supervised ring-completion task by themselves, largely because they remove account-pair identity and edge direction. The strongest results come from hybrid representations that combine identity-preserving graph features with topological summaries. These findings suggest that topology is most useful as a contextual layer over dynamic graph features, and that CML is a promising candidate model for fraud patterns whose evidence is distributed across temporal and relational context.

AI.tldr: Quantum-inspired contextual machine learning shows promise for detecting coordinated fraud patterns in dynamic financial transaction graphs when combined with hybrid graph-topology representations.
AI.motivation: Coordinated fraud patterns like sparse-ring fraud are not detectable from individual transactions alone, but emerge as multi-period relational patterns that require integration of evidence across both time and graph structure.
AI.method: Used synthetic transaction simulator with sparse-ring injections and broken-ring decoys. Daily directed transaction graphs aggregated into rolling windows, represented using: raw graph features, persistent-homology summaries, or hybrid feature vectors. Compared GRU baseline with quantum-inspired Contextual Machine Learning (CML) as sequence-level classifiers.
AI.result: Topology-only summaries were too compressed to solve supervised ring-completion task alone (remove account-pair identity and edge direction). Strongest results came from hybrid representations combining identity-preserving graph features with topological summaries.
AI.conclusion: Topology is most useful as a contextual layer over dynamic graph features, and CML is a promising candidate model for fraud patterns whose evidence is distributed across temporal and relational context.

title: StructAgent: Harness Long-horizon Digital Agents with Unified Causal Structure
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) have enabled increasingly capable digital agents for computer use. However, real-world tasks are often long-horizon and involve evolving contexts containing accumulated observations, intermediate edits, failed attempts, and partially completed executions. Existing agents typically operate over raw interaction history, making task progress difficult to interpret, verify, and recover, which ultimately limits reliable long-horizon execution. In this paper, we argue that addressing this challenge requires explicitly structuring both the agent's state and workflow around a unified causal representation of task progress. We present \textbf{StructAgent}, a state-centered framework that introduces a unified state for maintaining compact, verifiable task progress and a structured workflow that regulates progress through verifier-backed state transitions. Building on this design, StructAgent further enables explicit progress checkpointing, evidence-driven task completion, targeted failure recovery, and tool-supported execution, while ensuring that all progress updates remain grounded in verification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StructAgent consistently improves a wide range of LLM and VLM backbones on long-horizon computer-use tasks. On OSWorld-Verified, it improves Qwen3.5-9B from 27.0\% to 46.9\% success rate and Qwen3.5-27B from 31.6\% to 62.2\%, while achieving a new open-source state of the art of 78.9\% with MiniMax-M3. Moreover, the same framework generalizes beyond desktop environments to Minecraft, demonstrating the generality of our design.

AI.tldr: StructAgent is a state-centered framework that introduces unified causal task progress representation for long-horizon computer use tasks, improving reliability through verifier-backed state transitions and explicit progress management.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agents struggle with long-horizon real-world tasks involving evolving contexts with accumulated observations, intermediate edits, failed attempts, and partial executions. Operating over raw interaction history makes task progress difficult to interpret, verify, and recover, limiting reliable long-horizon execution.
AI.method: StructAgent introduces a unified state representation for maintaining compact, verifiable task progress and a structured workflow that regulates progress through verifier-backed state transitions. It enables explicit progress checkpointing, evidence-driven task completion, targeted failure recovery, and tool-supported execution while ensuring all progress updates remain grounded in verification.
AI.result: StructAgent consistently improves performance across various LLM and VLM backbones on long-horizon computer-use tasks. On OSWorld-Verified, it improved Qwen3.5-9B from 27.0% to 46.9% and Qwen3.5-27B from 31.6% to 62.2%, achieving a new open-source SOTA of 78.9% with MiniMax-M3. The framework also generalizes to non-desktop environments like Minecraft.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring both agent state and workflow around unified causal task progress representation is crucial for reliable long-horizon execution, enabling interpretable, verifiable, and recoverable task progress management that generalizes across different environments and model backbones.

title: SupplyNetPy: An Open-Source Python Library for High-Fidelity Modeling and Simulation of Arbitrary Supply Chain and Inventory Networks
summary: This paper introduces SupplyNetPy, an open-source, well-documented Python library for modeling and discrete-event simulation of supply chain networks with arbitrary multi-echelon structures. It supports multiple replenishment policies, perishable inventory, node disruptions, and stochastic demand and lead times. All components are extensible via inheritance. Users describe a supply chain as a graph with node and link attributes, while the library handles simulation, providing logs and extensive node and network level performance reports. This paper presents the motivation, design, key features, and architecture of SupplyNetPy, along with detailed validation results (against analytical benchmarks, a commercial tool, and a published case study). A key motivation behind SupplyNetPy's development is programmatic generation and simulation of complex models, enabling design-space exploration, what-if analysis, training data generation, and supply chain digital twins.

AI.tldr: SupplyNetPy is an open-source Python library for modeling and simulating multi-echelon supply chain networks with support for complex features like perishable inventory, disruptions, and stochastic elements.
AI.motivation: To enable programmatic generation and simulation of complex supply chain models for design-space exploration, what-if analysis, training data generation, and building supply chain digital twins.
AI.method: Users describe supply chains as graphs with node and link attributes; the library handles discrete-event simulation and provides extensive logging and performance reporting; all components are extensible via inheritance.
AI.result: The library supports multiple replenishment policies, perishable inventory, node disruptions, stochastic demand/lead times; validated against analytical benchmarks, a commercial tool, and published case studies.
AI.conclusion: SupplyNetPy is a comprehensive, extensible Python tool for supply chain modeling and simulation that facilitates complex analysis and digital twin development.

title: Manifold Constrained Tabular Deep Neural Networks
summary: Tabular classification is often governed by local, condition-triggered rules rather than smooth global patterns. However, tabular deep neural networks (DNNs) are typically built upon Euclidean representations that favor smooth variations and semantic locality. This potential geometric mismatch can make it challenging for tabular DNNs to efficiently represent the discrete, rule-partitioned structures often underlying tabular classification. To address this issue, we propose HDE-Net, a manifold-constrained DNN that enables hierarchical decision modeling in hyperbolic space. We first abstract heterogeneous features into unified Latent Decision Nodes (LDNs) and embed them in the Poincaré ball, forming a continuous representation that resembles tree-structured reasoning. For numerical features, we introduce a Soft Decision Routing mechanism that approximates range-based local rules in a differentiable manner, bringing their LDN semantics closer to those of categorical features. An entropy-aware capacity allocation algorithm further adapts the number of LDNs per numerical feature to balance expressiveness and complexity. On the TALENT-tiny-core classification benchmark (30 datasets), HDE-Net achieves the \textit{best average rank}, outperforming both industrial GBDTs and recent tabular DNNs while maintaining high efficiency.

AI.tldr: HDE-Net is a hyperbolic deep neural network that better represents hierarchical decision structures in tabular data by embedding features in hyperbolic space and using differentiable rule approximations.
AI.motivation: Tabular classification often involves discrete, condition-triggered rules, but current DNNs use Euclidean representations that favor smooth patterns, creating a geometric mismatch that hinders efficient representation of hierarchical decision structures.
AI.method: 1) Abstract features into unified Latent Decision Nodes (LDNs) embedded in Poincaré ball (hyperbolic space), 2) For numerical features, introduce Soft Decision Routing to approximate range-based rules differentiably, 3) Use entropy-aware capacity allocation to adapt number of LDNs per numerical feature for expressiveness-complexity balance.
AI.result: On TALENT-tiny-core benchmark (30 datasets), HDE-Net achieves best average rank, outperforming both industrial GBDTs and recent tabular DNNs while maintaining high efficiency.
AI.conclusion: By using hyperbolic space and differentiable rule approximations, HDE-Net successfully addresses the geometric mismatch in tabular classification, enabling better representation of hierarchical decision structures while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: EvoClawBench: Can Agents Learn Reusable Skills from Their Own Runs?
summary: Existing agent benchmarks primarily test task completion, tool use, or skill utility, but do not isolate whether a runtime can convert evidence from its own runs into reusable skills that improve fresh executions after authoring overhead. We introduce EvoClawBench, a benchmark for this closed-loop skill-learning question on repeated, fixture-backed tasks. EvoClawBench compares direct execution without skills, PreSkill authoring before execution, and PostSkill summarization from first-run evidence followed by a fresh second execution. The suite contains 100 tasks and 502 sub-problems across coding, data, office, security, operations, and domain-document workflows, with support for multiple agent runtimes. Experiments with OpenClaw and nanobot under local execution show that direct baseline performance is strongly runtime-dependent: OpenClaw remains below 20% across models, while nanobot ranges from 56.45% to 96.13%. Self-authored skills have mixed effects. nanobot GPT-5.4 stays above 96% in all modes and MiniMax-M2.7 improves from 90.97% to 94.50% under PostSkill, but nanobot DeepSeek-V4-Pro drops from 77.77% to 4.80% with PreSkill and 0.99% with PostSkill. OpenClaw shows similarly non-monotonic behavior, with some skill runs near baseline and others collapsing. These results indicate that learning reusable skills from an agent's own runs is selective and cost-sensitive, rather than an automatic benefit of adding skill authoring to an agent loop.

AI.tldr: EvoClawBench is a new benchmark that tests whether AI agents can learn reusable skills from their own execution evidence, evaluating different skill-learning strategies across diverse workflow tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on task completion, tool use, or skill utility, but don't isolate whether agents can convert evidence from their own runs into reusable skills that improve fresh executions after authoring overhead.
AI.method: EvoClawBench contains 100 tasks and 502 sub-problems across coding, data, office, security, operations, and domain-document workflows. It compares three approaches: direct execution without skills, PreSkill authoring before execution, and PostSkill summarization from first-run evidence followed by fresh second execution.
AI.result: Performance varies dramatically by runtime: nanobot ranges from 56.45% to 96.13% while OpenClaw remains below 20%. Skill learning has mixed effects - some models improve (MiniMax-M2.7 from 90.97% to 94.50%), while others collapse (DeepSeek-V4-Pro drops from 77.77% to 4.80% with PreSkill).
AI.conclusion: Learning reusable skills from an agent's own runs is selective and cost-sensitive, not an automatic benefit of adding skill authoring to an agent loop. Skill learning effectiveness depends heavily on both runtime and model characteristics.

title: Task-Conditioned Synthetic Data Generation for Improving Machine Learning Performance in Agricultural Prediction Tasks
summary: Machine Learning (ML) algorithms have been widely used to estimate agricultural variables across diverse contexts. However, because the quantity and quality of training data strongly influence performance of ML algorithms, their use can be constrained by limited or incomplete reference data. Synthetic Data Generation (SDG) offers a practical approach to address this issue by producing artificial but realistic samples that preserve key characteristics of the original data. Building on teacher-student knowledge transfer and in-context learning for tabular data, this study proposes a Task-Conditioned SDG (TCSDG) algorithm that pairs a Bayesian Network generator with a transformer-based tabular foundation model (TabICL). The proposed algorithm was evaluated on two agricultural prediction tasks: crop yield prediction and crop type classification. Six benchmark SDG algorithms were also utilized to compare their performance with that of TCSDG. Across twelve study sites, two training-data fractions, four multiplication ratios, and three predictive ML algorithms, augmenting the original data with TCSDG-generated synthetic data improved ML performance in 89% of the crop type classification experiments and 74% of the crop yield prediction experiments. TCSDG also substantially outperformed benchmark SDG algorithms and was the only method to consistently improve ML performance across both tasks at the aggregate level. The study demonstrates that carefully designed and processed synthetic data can improve ML performance in precision-agriculture applications. TCSDG offers a practical and extensible framework for generating synthetic data that supports downstream ML agricultural prediction. The full implementation of TCSDG is publicly available as open source at https://github.com/HamidEbrahimy/TCSDG.

AI.tldr: A Task-Conditioned Synthetic Data Generation (TCSDG) algorithm combining Bayesian Networks with transformer-based tabular foundation models improves ML performance for agricultural prediction tasks when training data is limited.
AI.motivation: ML algorithms in agriculture are constrained by limited or incomplete reference data, as their performance strongly depends on training data quantity and quality. Synthetic Data Generation offers a practical solution by creating realistic artificial samples.
AI.method: Proposes TCSDG algorithm that pairs a Bayesian Network generator with a transformer-based tabular foundation model (TabICL), building on teacher-student knowledge transfer and in-context learning for tabular data.
AI.result: TCSDG improved ML performance in 89% of crop type classification and 74% of crop yield prediction experiments across various conditions. It substantially outperformed six benchmark SDG algorithms and was the only method to consistently improve performance across both tasks.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed synthetic data can improve ML performance in precision agriculture. TCSDG offers a practical and extensible framework for generating synthetic data to support downstream ML agricultural prediction tasks.

title: ERP Data Provisioning Financial Control Testing
summary: Financial control testing increasingly depends on representative enterprise resource planning (ERP) data in quality environments, yet direct production copies expose personal, supplier, banking, and commercially sensitive records. This work presents Secure ERP Quality Provisioning for Financial Control Testing (SEQ-FCT), a governed data-provisioning framework that combines deterministic masking, synthetic scenario expansion, referential tokenization, policy-based release approval, and automated validation for reconciliation, fraud-rule testing, and audit analytics. A single synthetic dataset is used for evaluation. It contains 186,000 finance-process records from six subsidiaries over 2022-2025, including accounts payable invoices, payments, general-ledger journals, accounts receivable receipts, and bank-statement lines. The dataset includes entity relationships, monetary values, approval paths, tax attributes, banking markers, exception labels, fraud-rule triggers, and control-failure outcomes. Because the dataset is synthetic, reported results demonstrate controlled internal consistency rather than production validation. Against a production-clone upper bound, static masking, rules-only synthesis, conditional tabular generative synthesis, and a hybrid baseline, SEQ-FCT achieved 0.932 reconciliation F1, 0.887 fraud-trigger recall, 0.914 control-failure F1, and an estimated leakage-risk score of 0.018. The analysis indicates that financial process behavior can be preserved more reliably when masking, synthetic data, and governance checks are evaluated as a single release pipeline instead of independent utilities.

AI.tldr: SEQ-FCT is a governed data-provisioning framework that combines deterministic masking, synthetic scenario expansion, referential tokenization, policy-based release approval, and automated validation to provide secure ERP data for financial control testing while protecting sensitive information.
AI.motivation: Financial control testing requires representative ERP data from quality environments, but direct production copies expose sensitive personal, supplier, banking, and commercial records, creating privacy and security risks.
AI.method: The SEQ-FCT framework combines multiple techniques: deterministic masking for data anonymization, synthetic scenario expansion to enhance coverage, referential tokenization for maintaining data relationships, policy-based release approval for governance, and automated validation for reconciliation, fraud-rule testing, and audit analytics.
AI.result: Using a synthetic dataset of 186,000 finance-process records, SEQ-FCT achieved strong performance metrics: 0.932 reconciliation F1, 0.887 fraud-trigger recall, 0.914 control-failure F1, and a low estimated leakage-risk score of 0.018, outperforming baseline methods including static masking, rules-only synthesis, and conditional tabular generative synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Financial process behavior can be preserved more effectively when masking, synthetic data generation, and governance checks are treated as an integrated release pipeline rather than independent utilities, providing secure yet representative data for financial control testing.

title: LegalFarePlan: A Label-Setting Framework for Fare-Transparent Urban Rail Route Planning under Non-Additive Fare Rules
comment: 6 pages, 4 tables; author preprint version, not the conference camera-ready version
summary: Urban rail fare systems may be non-additive: the fare of a single paid journey from an origin to a destination can differ from the sum of fares over multiple legally separated journey legs. This paper presents LegalFarePlan, a fare-transparent route-planning framework that models legal exit-and-reentry operations as explicit, auditable constraints. Given a transit network, fare function, transfer rules, station-level exit/re-entry costs, an extra-time budget, and a split limit, the planner computes explainable route plans over paid journey segments. The artifact implements Dijkstra shortest-time and direct route-planner baselines, a greedy split heuristic, bounded exact label-setting, and Pareto-frontier search. Evaluation uses controlled synthetic data and a 57-station semi-synthetic benchmark with 360 OD pairs. On the semi-synthetic benchmark, bounded exact search identifies positive modeled fare reductions for 71.11% of OD pairs, with mean reduction 3.78 and maximum reduction 9.0 synthetic fare units under a 45-minute extra-time budget. These results demonstrate method behavior and reproducibility; they are not empirical conclusions about MTR or any transit operator.

AI.tldr: LegalFarePlan is a route-planning framework that accounts for non-additive urban rail fares by modeling legal exit-and-reentry operations as constraints to find explainable route plans with potential fare savings.
AI.motivation: Urban rail fare systems can be non-additive - a single journey fare may differ from the sum of multiple legally separated journey legs. Traditional route planners don't account for these fare complexities, potentially missing opportunities for fare savings through legal exit-and-reentry operations.
AI.method: LegalFarePlan models legal exit-and-reentry operations as explicit constraints in a transit network. It uses Dijkstra shortest-time baselines, greedy split heuristics, bounded exact label-setting, and Pareto-frontier search to compute explainable route plans over paid journey segments given fare functions, transfer rules, exit/re-entry costs, extra-time budget, and split limits.
AI.result: Evaluation on a 57-station semi-synthetic benchmark with 360 OD pairs shows bounded exact search identifies fare reductions for 71.11% of OD pairs, with mean reduction of 3.78 and maximum reduction of 9.0 synthetic fare units under a 45-minute extra-time budget.
AI.conclusion: LegalFarePlan successfully demonstrates a fare-transparent route-planning framework that can identify fare savings through legal exit-and-reentry operations. The results validate the method's effectiveness and reproducibility, though they are not empirical conclusions about any specific transit operator.

title: Gauge dependence and structured-output corruption in sign-branched repetition penalties: measurements across models, inference stacks, and alternative repetition controls
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Code, data, per-stack survey and git genealogy: https://github.com/captainpete/repetition-penalty-gauge
summary: The multiplicative repetition penalty shipped across the LLM inference ecosystem (HuggingFace, vLLM, llama.cpp, and a dozen further engines) branches on the sign of each raw logit (divide positives by theta, multiply negatives). But the softmax is unchanged by adding a constant to every logit, so a model's logit zero-point is arbitrary, and the sign-branch reads that arbitrary point. The sign-branch is itself the accepted fix for an earlier bug, so the accepted fix branches on a quantity the training objective leaves unconstrained. Two measurable consequences follow. (1) The penalty is not well-defined: re-centring a model's logits by a constant is a provable no-op at theta=1, yet at a routine theta=1.3 it changes 58-96% of greedy tokens, where subtractive and normalized penalties change none; real checkpoints sit at widely different zero-points, so a fixed repetition_penalty is a different operation on every model. (2) It corrupts structured output: on 200 real-world JSON schemas, theta=1.3 drops the rate of valid, schema-conformant output from 97% to 23%.
