In our measurements, applying the penalty to normalized log-probabilities instead of raw logits removes both effects. HuggingFace already ships that operator (LogitNormalization); today it is off by default and applied after the penalty. This note gives the mechanism, the measurements (five models up to 7B, base and RLHF, on WikiText-103 prefixes; two code models on HumanEval and JSONSchemaBench; both effects replicated inside vLLM and llama.cpp through their own samplers on the same inputs), and the normalized variant.

AI.tldr: The widely-used multiplicative repetition penalty in LLM inference systems is flawed because it branches on logit signs, which are arbitrary due to softmax invariance. This causes inconsistent behavior across models and corrupts structured output generation.
AI.motivation: Current multiplicative repetition penalty implementations (used in HuggingFace, vLLM, llama.cpp, etc.) have a fundamental flaw: they branch on the sign of raw logits, but softmax is invariant to adding constants to logits. This means a model's logit zero-point is arbitrary, and the penalty branches on this arbitrary quantity, making the operation ill-defined and inconsistent across different models.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the mathematical mechanism of the problem, measures its consequences across multiple models (up to 7B parameters, base and RLHF) and inference engines. They conduct experiments on WikiText-103 prefixes, HumanEval, JSONSchemaBench, and real-world JSON schemas, replicating effects in vLLM and llama.cpp. They propose using normalized log-probabilities instead of raw logits.
AI.result: Two measurable consequences: (1) The penalty is not well-defined - at theta=1.3, re-centering logits by a constant changes 58-96% of greedy tokens (vs. none with subtractive/normalized penalties), making the same repetition_penalty value perform differently across models. (2) It corrupts structured output - on 200 real-world JSON schemas, theta=1.3 drops valid schema-conformant output from 97% to 23%. Applying penalty to normalized log-probabilities instead eliminates both problems.
AI.conclusion: The current multiplicative repetition penalty implementation is fundamentally flawed and should be replaced by applying penalties to normalized log-probabilities rather than raw logits. HuggingFace already has this operator (LogitNormalization), but it's off by default and applied after the penalty - it should be enabled and applied before the penalty.

title: BatteryLake: Agentic, Physics-Grounded Curation of Heterogeneous Battery Aging Data and Benchmarking
comment: The platform, benchmark, and curation protocol are publicly available at https://tianwen1209.github.io/batterylake/
summary: Public battery aging datasets are a critical asset for advanced health management, but their practical use is often limited by inconsistent formats, unclear schemas, and metadata scattered across repositories and publications. Current curation remains largely manual and hard to reproduce, while general-purpose data integration tools miss the domain-specific semantics of electrochemical time-series data. We present BatteryLake, a governed data lakehouse that turns raw public battery data into benchmark-ready assets through an agentic, physics-grounded curation framework, with three contributions. First, LLM agents extract metadata and synthesize dataset-specific converters, grounding every output in verbatim evidence and abstaining when none supports a value. Second, a human-in-the-loop mechanism frames verification as selective prediction and gates admitted data through 26 schema, statistical, and physical-plausibility rules. Third, we release an open benchmark of 41 datasets from over 25 institutions, with standardized SOH and RUL tasks, three split protocols, and eight baseline model families. The platform, benchmark, and curation protocol are publicly available at https://tianwen1209.github.io/batterylake/.

AI.tldr: BatteryLake is an agentic, physics-grounded curation framework that transforms raw public battery aging data into standardized benchmark-ready datasets using LLM agents, human-in-the-loop verification, and domain-specific validation rules.
AI.motivation: Public battery aging datasets suffer from inconsistent formats, unclear schemas, and scattered metadata, making them difficult to use practically. Current curation is manual and non-reproducible, while general-purpose data integration tools lack domain-specific understanding of electrochemical time-series data.
AI.method: The system uses LLM agents to extract metadata and create dataset-specific converters, grounding outputs in verbatim evidence. A human-in-the-loop mechanism verifies data through selective prediction, applying 26 schema, statistical, and physical-plausibility rules. The framework is implemented as a governed data lakehouse called BatteryLake.
AI.result: The authors release an open benchmark with 41 datasets from over 25 institutions, featuring standardized SOH and RUL tasks, three split protocols, and eight baseline model families. All platform components, benchmark data, and curation protocols are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: BatteryLake addresses critical data quality issues in battery aging research by providing a reproducible, physics-grounded curation framework that transforms fragmented public data into standardized benchmarks for reliable battery health management research.

title: Metadata-Free Meta-Reweighted Direct Preference Optimization under Noisy Preference Labels
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has become an important method for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences because it removes the need for explicit reward modeling and reinforcement learning optimization. However, its performance depends heavily on the quality of preference data, and noisy preference data in real-world settings can weaken alignment performance. To address this issue, we propose a bilevel optimization framework and prove, under certain assumptions, that this framework can recover the DPO optimum under clean data. We further derive a prior form for the learnable weighting function under asymmetric label-flipping noise. Considering that high-quality metadata may be difficult to obtain, we propose a task-agnostic meta-knowledge-driven method that enables meta-learning even when metadata is completely unavailable. To reduce the high cost of higher-order gradients in LLM meta-learning, we combine central-difference approximation with LoRA fine-tuning and develop a scalable training scheme. Experiments on TL;DR summarization and Anthropic HH single-turn dialogue show that the proposed method improves training performance over multiple DPO baselines under different noise rates.

AI.tldr: A bilevel optimization framework for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that improves alignment performance under noisy preference data through meta-learning and scalable training techniques.
AI.motivation: DPO's performance depends heavily on clean preference data, but real-world settings often have noisy preference data that weakens alignment performance. The paper aims to address this limitation by making DPO more robust to noisy preference data.
AI.method: Proposes a bilevel optimization framework that can recover DPO optimum under clean data, derives prior form for learnable weighting function under asymmetric label-flipping noise, develops a task-agnostic meta-knowledge-driven method for when metadata is unavailable, and combines central-difference approximation with LoRA fine-tuning for scalable training.
AI.result: Experimental results on TL;DR summarization and Anthropic HH single-turn dialogue show that the proposed method improves training performance over multiple DPO baselines under different noise rates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the noise sensitivity problem in DPO, providing more robust alignment of LLMs with human preferences even in noisy real-world settings, while maintaining scalability through efficient training techniques.

title: JEPA for AI-Native 6G: Predictive Representations and Open Challenges
comment: 14 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Tutorial and review on Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for AI-native 6G. Submitted to IEEE Communications Magazine
summary: Sixth-generation (6G) networks are moving toward AI-native operation, where learning modules are embedded across the radio access network (RAN), edge, and core. This transition requires learning from limited labels, heterogeneous wireless and network data, partial observations, non-stationary propagation, and latency-constrained control loops. Joint-embedding predictive architecture (JEPA) is a promising self-supervised paradigm for this setting because it predicts missing or future representations in latent space instead of reconstructing raw measurements or using contrastive negative samples. This article presents a wireless-oriented tutorial on JEPA for 6G intelligence. We define the JEPA training mechanism, describe how CSI, beam measurements, KPIs, topology graphs, and sensing observations can be tokenized and masked, and position the learned encoder as a predictive representation layer for RAN, O-RAN, edge, and core functions, with task-specific heads or controllers producing final decisions. Then we present an illustrative, beam-management case study suggesting that a wireless-aware target, specifically an auxiliary future beam-energy target during self-supervised pretraining, can improve label efficiency and robustness across shifted deployment conditions relative to a supervised source domain. Finally, we outline open challenges in multi-timescale prediction, action-conditioned modeling, distributed training, trustworthiness, efficient deployment, benchmarking, and standardization.

AI.tldr: Wireless-oriented tutorial on Joint-embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for 6G AI-native networks, focusing on self-supervised learning from heterogeneous wireless data and presenting a beam-management case study showing improved label efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: 6G networks are transitioning to AI-native operation, requiring learning from limited labels, heterogeneous data, partial observations, non-stationary propagation, and latency-constrained control loops. Traditional methods struggle with these challenges, creating a need for self-supervised paradigms suitable for wireless environments.
AI.method: The paper presents Joint-embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) as a self-supervised paradigm that predicts missing or future representations in latent space. It details how to tokenize and mask wireless data (CSI, beam measurements, KPIs, topology graphs, sensing observations) and use the learned encoder as a predictive representation layer for various network functions. The method includes a beam-management case study using an auxiliary future beam-energy target during self-supervised pretraining.
AI.result: The beam-management case study demonstrates that a wireless-aware target (auxiliary future beam-energy target) during self-supervised pretraining can improve label efficiency and robustness across shifted deployment conditions compared to supervised approaches from source domains.
AI.conclusion: JEPA is a promising self-supervised paradigm for 6G AI-native networks that addresses challenges of limited labels, heterogeneous data, and non-stationary environments. The paper provides a tutorial framework and identifies open challenges in multi-timescale prediction, action-conditioned modeling, distributed training, trustworthiness, efficient deployment, benchmarking, and standardization for future research.

title: The Silent Freeze: Predicting When Low-Precision Training Stops Learning
summary: Training in reduced floating-point precision can silently halt learning: when a gradient-descent weight update falls below half the unit in the last place (ULP) of the weight, it rounds away and that coordinate freezes while its gradient is still nonzero. The freeze is deterministic, governed by a per-coordinate half-ULP condition, and predictable from a high-precision trajectory and the target mantissa length alone, without low-precision data. In a small GPT trained under the standard AdamW-plus-cosine recipe with bf16-equivalent stored weights, training proceeds normally and then permanently freezes just past mid-run, within four steps of the a-priori prediction. In a $124$-million-parameter GPT-2 transformer whose weights are constrained to the $8$-bit floating-point grid after every optimizer step, with no master weights, the dense weights freeze at initialization in both fp8 formats -- predicted \emph{a priori} from an fp32 reference -- and validation loss plateaus while full precision keeps improving. Stochastic rounding removes the persistent freeze, and the same reference predicts that too. The condition transfers across frozen-feature regression, a mantissa-truncation emulator spanning $128\times$ in precision, small networks, and a CNN on MNIST: a computable axis of low-precision training, not diffuse noise.

AI.tldr: Training in low-precision floating-point can cause deterministic freezing when weight updates fall below half the unit in last place, predicted from high-precision trajectories without low-precision data.
AI.motivation: To understand why low-precision floating-point training can silently fail - when gradient descent weight updates become too small and round away, causing parameters to freeze despite nonzero gradients.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the half-ULP condition, experiments with GPT models using bf16 and fp8 formats, and testing across various settings including frozen-feature regression, mantissa-truncation emulators, small networks, and CNNs on MNIST.
AI.result: Training in bf16-equivalent GPT halts predictably within four steps of theoretical prediction; fp8 GPT-2 weights freeze at initialization as predicted; validation loss plateaus while full precision keeps improving; stochastic rounding eliminates the freeze.
AI.conclusion: Low-precision training failures are deterministic and predictable from high-precision trajectories, not random noise; stochastic rounding can prevent freezing; this provides a computable axis for understanding precision limits in neural network training.

title: Discovering Latent Response Laws in Forced Physical Systems
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures and 2 tables. Supplementary Information provided as an ancillary file
summary: Governing equations provide compact descriptions of physical systems, yet the variables in which they are simple are often hidden in high-dimensional measurements. This challenge is sharper for forced systems, whose responses depend on both intrinsic dynamics and time-dependent inputs. Here we introduce FLARE, a forced latent autoencoder for response equations that learns compact response coordinates, identifies sparse input-dependent latent dynamics and decodes equation rollouts to full responses. By estimating latent dimension from data and separating state estimation from external forcing, FLARE enables forecasts to be initialized from past responses and driven by prescribed future inputs. Across known dynamical systems, application-scale forced responses and visual observations, FLARE recovers compact forced dynamics and predicts long-horizon high-dimensional responses under inputs not used for training. By turning learned coordinates into a dynamical interface, FLARE extends equation discovery to systems whose effective states are hidden within complex observations, providing a route for interpretable modelling and prediction of high-dimensional responses in forced dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: FLARE learns compact latent coordinates and sparse forced dynamics from high-dimensional response data, enabling long-horizon predictions under unseen inputs.
AI.motivation: Governing equations are often simple in hidden variables within high-dimensional measurements, especially challenging for forced systems whose responses depend on both intrinsic dynamics and time-dependent inputs.
AI.method: FLARE is a forced latent autoencoder for response equations that learns compact response coordinates, identifies sparse input-dependent latent dynamics, decodes equation rollouts to full responses, estimates latent dimension from data, and separates state estimation from external forcing.
AI.result: FLARE recovers compact forced dynamics across known systems, application-scale forced responses, and visual observations, predicting long-horizon high-dimensional responses under inputs not used for training.
AI.conclusion: By turning learned coordinates into a dynamical interface, FLARE extends equation discovery to systems with hidden effective states, providing interpretable modeling and prediction of high-dimensional forced dynamical system responses.

title: EvoCUA-1.5: Online Reinforcement Learning for Multi-turn Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents must solve long-horizon tasks through repeated interaction with partially observable, multimodal desktop environments. Although imitation learning and offline trajectory refinement provide strong priors, static traces cannot cover the causal feedback loop of real computer use: each action changes the screen state, future action space, and recovery options. EvoCUA-1.5 extends self-evolving computer-use agents from offline experience learning to online reinforcement learning, where policies interact with executable sandbox environments and improve from verifiable task outcomes. Online RL in this setting requires more than directly reusing single-turn language-RL recipes. Multi-turn interaction introduces context-managed observations, sparse terminal rewards, variable-length trajectories, and slow environment feedback. EvoCUA-1.5 addresses these challenges with Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO), which preserves trajectory-level advantage balance after decomposition into step-level samples; policy-aware filtering and pass-rate calibration over verifiable synthesized tasks; Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC), which combines learnable tasks, difficult positive replay, and controlled infeasible-task exposure; and a fully asynchronous RL infrastructure with staleness control and mini-group batching. Experiments show that these components improve training stability and downstream performance. EvoCUA-1.5 achieves 63.2\% success on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming comparable 32B/35B-scale open-weight baselines and even approaching models with significantly larger parameter counts. Overall, EvoCUA-1.5 provides a practical framework for scaling online RL in multi-turn computer-use agents.

AI.tldr: EvoCUA-1.5 introduces an online reinforcement learning framework for computer-use agents that addresses multi-turn interaction challenges in desktop environments, achieving state-of-the-art performance on OSWorld-Verified benchmark.
AI.motivation: Computer-use agents need to handle long-horizon tasks in partially observable, multimodal desktop environments. While imitation learning and offline trajectory refinement provide priors, static traces cannot capture the causal feedback loop of real computer use where each action changes screen state, action space, and recovery options. Existing methods lack effective online learning from actual environment interactions.
AI.method: EvoCUA-1.5 extends self-evolving computer-use agents from offline to online RL with executable sandbox environments. Key components: 1) Step-Level Policy Optimization (STEPO) - preserves trajectory-level advantage balance after step-level decomposition; 2) Policy-aware filtering and pass-rate calibration over verifiable synthesized tasks; 3) Dynamic Tri-Adaptive Curriculum (DTAC) - combines learnable tasks, difficult positive replay, and controlled infeasible-task exposure; 4) Fully asynchronous RL infrastructure with staleness control and mini-group batching.
AI.result: EvoCUA-1.5 achieves 63.2% success rate on OSWorld-Verified benchmark, outperforming comparable 32B/35B-scale open-weight baselines and approaching performance of models with significantly larger parameter counts. The proposed components improve training stability and downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: EvoCUA-1.5 provides a practical framework for scaling online RL in multi-turn computer-use agents, successfully addressing challenges of multi-turn interaction including context-managed observations, sparse rewards, variable-length trajectories, and slow environment feedback. The approach enables agents to learn from actual environment interactions rather than just static traces.

title: Quota Marketplace: Dynamic Pricing for Efficient Allocation of ML Training Resources
summary:
The escalating demand for Machine Learning (ML) training resources in recent years has resulted in a substantial gap between the high demand and the available supply. Efficient allocation of these scarce and expensive resources is crucial for organizations to maximize their return on investment. Existing resource allocation mechanisms, like Karma [OSDI'23], are designed to guarantee Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness in settings with dynamic (time-varying) user demands, but fail to preserve these key properties in the presence of demands with heterogeneous values. Given the ubiquity and inevitability of heterogeneity in organizational values of different workloads, effective resource allocation policies must accommodate these variations.
  In this paper, we describe the design, implementation, deployment, and theoretical analysis of Quota Marketplace, a market-based mechanism to efficiently allocate ML training chips (like GPUs), explicitly addressing scenarios with demands of heterogeneous value. We detail the implementation of this mechanism within Google and present metrics that demonstrate its impact. We also discuss many business-critical requirements that the Quota Marketplace handles quite effectively, and document the gains and opportunities it has unlocked. We establish theoretically how this market-based approach achieves the essential properties of Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness by allowing the users to express the value of their workloads and enabling dynamic resource pricing based on supply and demand fluctuations. Ultimately, the market facilitates resource allocation that aligns with organizational priorities.

AI.tldr: Quota Marketplace is a market-based mechanism for allocating scarce ML training resources (like GPUs) that handles heterogeneous workload values while maintaining Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness.
AI.motivation: There's a substantial gap between high demand and limited supply of ML training resources. Existing allocation mechanisms like Karma fail to preserve key properties (Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness) when dealing with demands that have heterogeneous values, which is ubiquitous in organizational settings.
AI.method: Designed and implemented a market-based mechanism called Quota Marketplace that allows users to express the value of their workloads and enables dynamic resource pricing based on supply and demand fluctuations. The system was implemented within Google.
AI.result: The paper presents implementation metrics demonstrating the system's impact, discusses how it handles business-critical requirements, and documents gains and opportunities unlocked. The market-based approach facilitates resource allocation aligned with organizational priorities.
AI.conclusion: Quota Marketplace effectively addresses heterogeneous value scenarios in ML resource allocation, achieving Pareto efficiency and max-min fairness through market mechanisms while aligning resource allocation with organizational priorities.

title: From Patterns to Maze Structures: SMT-Based Path Synthesis and 2D/3D Construction
summary: We present a pipeline for constructing maze structures from input patterns such as text or shapes. The central path-synthesis problem is encoded in Satisfiability Modulo Theories as global constraints on adjacency, continuity, and pattern-constrained coverage, allowing each fixed-bound instance to be solved in one call. The resulting path is either a planar, self-avoiding route or a layered traversal with prescribed over--under crossings, and it serves as a scaffold for constructing planar mazes and three-dimensional realizations of woven mazes. This report extends the published Bridges 2026 conference paper with more representative SMT-LIB examples and a fuller account of how synthesized paths become concrete maze constructions in planar and three-dimensional form.

AI.tldr: A pipeline for generating maze structures from input patterns using SMT encoding to solve path synthesis problems, producing planar mazes or 3D woven mazes.
AI.motivation: To create a systematic method for constructing maze structures from arbitrary input patterns (text or shapes) by solving the path synthesis problem, enabling both 2D planar mazes and 3D woven maze constructions.
AI.method: Encode the path-synthesis problem in Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) with global constraints on adjacency, continuity, and pattern-constrained coverage. Each fixed-bound instance is solved in one SMT call to generate either planar self-avoiding routes or layered traversals with prescribed over-under crossings.
AI.result: Developed a pipeline that produces maze paths from input patterns, with the resulting path serving as a scaffold for constructing both planar mazes and three-dimensional realizations of woven mazes. The system generates representative SMT-LIB examples and demonstrates concrete maze constructions.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully creates maze structures from input patterns using SMT encoding, extending beyond the original conference paper to provide more comprehensive examples and documentation of how synthesized paths become actual maze constructions in both 2D and 3D forms.

title: Spectral Origins of the Self-Correction Blind Spot in Autoregressive Generation
summary: Large autoregressive language models exhibit a self-correction blind spot: they reliably fix identical errors when attributed to an external source yet fail to fix the same errors in their own outputs. Prior work has documented this phenomenon empirically, through controlled error injection, error-depth decompositions, RL-based verifier-corrector training, and intrinsic self-verification, but offers no formal model of why generating a token suppresses the ability to detect its error, no quantitative activation condition for correction markers, and no convergence guarantee for reinforcement-learning-based self-correction. We close these gaps with SPARC, a spectral-algebraic theory of self-correction in autoregressive generation. We define the error-propagation operator as the product of per-step attention Jacobians on the residual stream and prove that the blind spot arises if and only if the spectral radius of this operator is at least one. We derive a sharp activation threshold, given as a function of the spectral radius, that a correction marker must exceed, recovering the 89.3\% blind-spot reduction observed with a simple ``Wait'' marker. We further prove that RL-based verifier-corrector training converges at a rate proportional to the squared coupling strength over the square root of the number of samples if and only if the verifier-corrector coupling matrix has spectral norm below one, and that this criterion is invariant across residual-stream autoregressive modalities, unifying text LLMs and autoregressive image and video generation. Experiments across four backbones and a visual autoregressive probe validate every theorem, with spectral predictions matching measured blind-spot rates within 3.2\% RMSE.

AI.tldr: SPARC provides a spectral-algebraic theory explaining self-correction blind spots in autoregressive models, showing they arise when error-propagation operators have spectral radius ≥1, and proving convergence conditions for RL-based correction training.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models have a self-correction blind spot: they can fix identical errors from external sources but fail to fix the same errors in their own outputs. Prior empirical work documented this phenomenon but lacked formal models, quantitative activation conditions, or convergence guarantees for RL-based self-correction methods.
AI.method: Developed SPARC (spectral-algebraic theory of self-correction) that defines an error-propagation operator as the product of per-step attention Jacobians on the residual stream. Proved mathematical conditions for blind spots using spectral analysis of these operators, derived activation thresholds for correction markers, and established convergence guarantees for RL-based verifier-corrector training.
AI.result: Proved that blind spots arise if and only if the spectral radius of the error-propagation operator is at least one. Derived a sharp activation threshold for correction markers (recovering the 89.3% blind-spot reduction with a "Wait" marker). Proved convergence rate for RL-based training depends on coupling matrix spectral norm being below one. Validated theorems across four backbones with spectral predictions matching measured blind-spot rates within 3.2% RMSE.
AI.conclusion: SPARC provides a unified spectral-algebraic theory that explains self-correction blind spots in autoregressive generation, offers quantitative predictions for correction effectiveness, and establishes mathematical guarantees for RL-based self-correction training across text, image, and video generation modalities.

title: Length Penalties Make Chain-of-Thought Less Monitorable
summary: Length-penalized reinforcement learning can shorten chain-of-thought reasoning while hiding an influence that drives the model's answer. In our experiments, training with length penalties does not stop misleading hints from steering models, even though the models' chains of thought mention the hint much less often. A token-accuracy evaluation would count these runs as successful because they use fewer reasoning tokens with little accuracy loss; it would miss whether the remaining trace still shows what drove the answer. We train Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B variants with different target chain lengths, then evaluate them with biasing-hint interventions on held-out MMLU-Pro-R and four transfer benchmarks. Compression sharply cuts reasoning tokens, preserves most multiple-choice accuracy, and leaves hint influence near baseline. At the strongest target, lower-bound faithfulness falls to 63.1% of baseline for Qwen3-14B and 69.4% for Qwen3-4B; the raw rate at which a monitor catches hint use falls from 69% to 49% and from 60% to 48%. To separate length from content, we randomly delete sentences from uncompressed baseline chains until the remaining text matches the compressed length. Even after this length matching, compressed chains disclose the hint 7-35 percentage points less often than baseline chains that we shorten at random, for both Qwen3 sizes and all five evaluation distributions. Compression therefore does more than shorten reasoning, preferentially removing the cues a monitor needs to see what influenced the answer. Together, these results reveal a compression-monitorability frontier in which cheaper reasoning can preserve answers while making the influences behind them harder to detect.

AI.tldr: Length-penalized reinforcement learning compresses chain-of-thought reasoning, reducing token usage while preserving answer accuracy but significantly reducing transparency - making it harder to detect what influenced the model's answers.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate how length-penalized training affects the transparency and monitorability of chain-of-thought reasoning. While compressing reasoning saves tokens and maintains accuracy, it may hide the actual influences that drive model answers, creating a trade-off between efficiency and explainability.
AI.method: The authors trained Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B variants with different target chain lengths using length-penalized reinforcement learning. They then evaluated these compressed models on MMLU-Pro-R and four transfer benchmarks using biasing-hint interventions. To isolate length from content effects, they randomly deleted sentences from uncompressed baseline chains to match compressed lengths for comparison.
AI.result: Compression dramatically reduces reasoning tokens while preserving most multiple-choice accuracy, but hint influence remains near baseline levels. Lower-bound faithfulness fell to 63.1% of baseline for Qwen3-14B and 69.4% for Qwen3-4B. Compressed chains disclose hints 7-35 percentage points less often than length-matched baseline chains, showing compression preferentially removes monitoring cues.
AI.conclusion: There exists a compression-monitorability frontier where cheaper reasoning preserves answers but makes underlying influences harder to detect. Length penalties don't stop misleading hints from steering models; they just make the influence less visible in the reasoning trace, creating a transparency-efficiency tradeoff.

title: RUBRIC: Realism--Utility Balanced Ranking for Imbalanced Classification
summary:
Class imbalance poses a fundamental challenge in risk-sensitive applications such as fraud detection and medical diagnosis, where minority-class samples are scarce yet critical for accurate classification. Existing oversampling methods generate synthetic samples to rebalance class distributions; however, they often produce large numbers of low-quality candidates that distort decision boundaries or introduce artifacts, leading to overfitting and degraded generalization.
  In this work, we introduce RUBRIC, a generator-agnostic filtering framework that formulates synthetic sample selection as a quality-over-quantity optimization problem. RUBRIC ranks candidates using a realism-utility trade-off: realism is quantified by a learned discriminator that distinguishes real samples from synthetic samples, while utility captures proximity to the decision boundary through a concave margin-based scoring function. We show that, under mild regularity conditions, the proposed filtering strategy monotonically tightens the generalization bound for margin-based classifiers by jointly reducing distribution shift and suppressing near-negative tail contributions.
  Through extensive experiments on credit-card fraud detection and other imbalanced benchmarks, we demonstrate that RUBRIC improves F1-macro and recall while maintaining comparable ROC-AUC across several generators. We also provide explicit lambda-sensitivity analysis to show how users can recover AUPRC when ranking quality is prioritized.

AI.tldr: RUBRIC is a generator-agnostic filtering framework that selects high-quality synthetic samples for imbalanced datasets by balancing realism and utility, improving classification performance in risk-sensitive applications.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance is a fundamental challenge in risk-sensitive applications like fraud detection and medical diagnosis, where minority-class samples are scarce but critical. Existing oversampling methods often produce low-quality synthetic samples that distort decision boundaries, cause overfitting, and degrade generalization performance.
AI.method: RUBRIC formulates synthetic sample selection as a quality-over-quantity optimization problem. It ranks candidates using a realism-utility trade-off: realism is quantified by a learned discriminator distinguishing real from synthetic samples, while utility captures proximity to decision boundaries through a concave margin-based scoring function. The framework is generator-agnostic and works with various oversampling methods.
AI.result: Experimental results on credit-card fraud detection and other imbalanced benchmarks show RUBRIC improves F1-macro and recall while maintaining comparable ROC-AUC across several generators. The framework also provides lambda-sensitivity analysis for recovering AUPRC when prioritizing ranking quality.
AI.conclusion: RUBRIC addresses the quality deficiency in oversampling methods by introducing a principled filtering framework that selects synthetic samples based on both realism and utility. The approach theoretically tightens generalization bounds and practically improves performance in imbalanced classification tasks, particularly in risk-sensitive domains.

title: PHITSBench: an execution-scored benchmark for AI-assisted PHITS radiation-transport input generation using natural language
summary: We introduce PHITSBench, an execution-scored benchmark for the Monte Carlo Particle and Heavy Ion Transport code System (PHITS). PHITSBench comprises 282 transport-scorable tasks spanning three common workflow categories: parameter editing (Edit), syntax repair (Repair ), and complete simulation generation from natural-language descriptions (Reproduce). Each task is evaluated using a Composite Metric Score that combines execution success with agreement between generated and reference transport observables. Using PHITSBench, we evaluate five GPT-5.4-based configurations ranging from zero-shot prompting to knowledge-augmented and agentic workflows. Without domain-specific knowledge, the model performs well on editing and repair tasks (95% and 70% success, respectively) but fails to generate correct simulations from scratch (0% success on the Reproduce track). A structured, machine-readable PHITS knowledge catalog, supplied alongside the user manual, raises single-shot Reproduce-task success to 57%. Agentic execution provides a further improvement to 66-73%, but at increased computational cost. Failure analysis shows that the remaining errors are dominated by incorrect selection and configuration of physical observables rather than syntax generation. These results suggest that future progress in AI-assisted radiation-transport modeling will depend as much on machine-readable knowledge bases, curated domain-training datasets, and execution-grounded evaluation environments as on advances in foundation models themselves.

AI.tldr: PHITSBench: A benchmark for evaluating AI systems on Monte Carlo particle transport code tasks, showing that while AI performs well on editing/repair tasks (95%/70% success), it struggles with generating simulations from scratch (0% success) without domain knowledge.
AI.motivation: There's a need to evaluate AI systems' capabilities in assisting with radiation-transport modeling workflows, particularly for the PHITS Monte Carlo code system. Current evaluation methods may not adequately assess real-world performance on tasks like simulation generation, editing, and repair.
AI.method: Created PHITSBench with 282 transport-scorable tasks across three workflow categories: parameter editing, syntax repair, and simulation generation from natural-language descriptions. Used a Composite Metric Score combining execution success and observable agreement. Evaluated five GPT-5.4-based configurations ranging from zero-shot to knowledge-augmented and agentic workflows.
AI.result: Without domain knowledge: 95% success on editing, 70% on repair, but 0% on generation tasks. With structured knowledge catalog: generation success increased to 57%. Agentic execution further improved to 66-73% but with higher computational cost. Main failures were due to incorrect observable selection/configuration rather than syntax generation.
AI.conclusion: Future progress in AI-assisted radiation-transport modeling depends as much on machine-readable knowledge bases, curated domain-training datasets, and execution-grounded evaluation environments as on foundation model advances.

title: Estimation, Prediction, and Assortment Optimization for Markov Chain Choice Models with Panel Data
summary: We propose a framework for the Markov chain (MC) choice model with panel data, including parameter estimation, personalized choice prediction, and personalized assortment optimization. In contrast to the traditional setting, which assumes that each transaction is independently drawn from a random utility model, our framework accounts for dependencies among transactions for the same customer in historical data, captured by partial-ordering preference information. To the best of our knowledge, our framework initiates the study of choice modeling with panel data under MC. As our primary result, we propose novel expectation-maximization (EM) algorithms for MC parameter estimation by incorporating partial-ordering-based customer preference information. On synthetic datasets and the sushi dataset, our EM algorithms outperform the traditional EM algorithm of Simsek and Topaloglu (Operations Research, 66, 2018) and multinomial-logit-based partial-order benchmarks adapted from Jagabathula and Vulcano (Management Science, 64, 2018). As our secondary contribution, we present hardness and computational results for conditional choice prediction and assortment optimization problems. These results complement our estimation framework and clarify the computational landscape of conditional choice and assortment optimization, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: A framework for Markov chain choice modeling with panel data that incorporates customer-specific transaction dependencies using partial-order preferences, with EM algorithms for estimation and computational analysis of conditional prediction/optimization problems.
AI.motivation: Traditional choice models assume independent transactions per customer, but real customer histories show dependencies. Panel data captures these temporal dependencies and partial-ordering preferences, which existing Markov chain choice models don't incorporate.
AI.method: Proposed novel EM algorithms for MC parameter estimation using partial-ordering customer preference information. Also analyzed computational complexity of conditional choice prediction and personalized assortment optimization problems.
AI.result: EM algorithms outperform traditional EM (Simsek & Topaloglu, 2018) and multinomial-logit benchmarks (Jagabathula & Vulcano, 2018) on synthetic and sushi datasets. Established computational hardness results for conditional problems.
AI.conclusion: First framework for MC choice modeling with panel data using partial-order preferences. EM algorithms provide better estimation, and computational analysis clarifies the landscape of personalized choice prediction and optimization.

title: Semantic Drift and the Stability of Operator Control in Reasoning-Class Decision Support Systems
summary: The article investigates the fundamental problem of ensuring the stability of operator control and preserving goal-targeting in hybrid human-machine decision support systems (DSS) of a new generation. Based on a two-month continuous longitudinal experiment on the joint design of a monograph-format textual array, the latent phenomenon of semantic context drift in large language models of deep logical reasoning (Reasoning LLMs) is verified and described. A mathematical model of interaction in the human-machine interface is proposed, and an original metric is introduced - the operator control stability coefficient, which takes into account the non-linear contextual pressure of hidden reasoning chains. Within the paradigm of the cognitome theory, a critical point of control functions inversion is captured. Engineering recommendations are formulated for implementing dynamic relational arbitration loops based on a modified hierarchical similarity model.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates stability and goal preservation in human-machine DSS, identifies semantic context drift in Reasoning LLMs through longitudinal experiments, and proposes control stability metrics with engineering recommendations.
AI.motivation: To address the fundamental problem of ensuring operator control stability and preserving goal-targeting in next-generation hybrid human-machine decision support systems, particularly due to emerging issues with large language models in reasoning contexts.
AI.method: Two-month continuous longitudinal experiment on joint monograph design, verification of semantic context drift phenomenon in Reasoning LLMs, development of mathematical interaction model in human-machine interface, and introduction of operator control stability coefficient metric.
AI.result: Identified and described latent phenomenon of semantic context drift in Reasoning LLMs, proposed mathematical model of human-machine interaction, introduced control stability coefficient metric, captured critical point of control functions inversion within cognitome theory, and formulated engineering recommendations for dynamic relational arbitration loops.
AI.conclusion: The research successfully identifies and characterizes semantic context drift in Reasoning LLMs, provides theoretical and mathematical frameworks for understanding human-machine control stability, and offers practical engineering solutions for maintaining control integrity in next-generation DSS through dynamic relational arbitration mechanisms.

title: Learning Predictive Ambiguity Sets for Decision-Focused Distributionally Robust Optimization
summary: Predict-then-optimize systems usually compress uncertainty into a point forecast and then solve a downstream optimization problem as if the forecast were reliable. Distributionally robust optimization (DRO) offers protection against misspecification, but the ambiguity set is often centered at historical samples and uses a fixed radius. We propose \emph{learned predictive ambiguity sets} (LPAS): a deep contextual model outputs a finite nominal scenario distribution, a state-dependent Wasserstein radius, and optionally an anisotropic ground metric. These outputs define a contextual ambiguity set that feeds a DRO decision layer. The radius is trained by a combination of conditional quantile calibration, size regularization, and downstream decision loss, so that robustness is adaptive rather than globally fixed. We derive the finite dual form used by the decision layer, present a staged training algorithm, and evaluate the method on distributionally robust portfolio optimization with 20 S&P 500 constituents from 2018--2026. The proposed method substantially improves over equal-weight, predict-then-optimize, and historical Wasserstein DRO baselines, achieving 26.28% annualized return, Sharpe ratio 1.30, final wealth 1.61, and lower tail loss than a deep fixed-radius DRO baseline while using a smaller average radius. The results show that learned ambiguity radii can recover most of the performance of strong fixed-radius DRO while reducing unnecessary conservatism and improving regime adaptivity.

AI.tldr: A deep learning approach that learns contextual ambiguity sets for distributionally robust optimization, including adaptive radii and anisotropic metrics, achieving better portfolio performance than baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional predict-then-optimize systems compress uncertainty into unreliable point forecasts, while standard DRO uses fixed-radius ambiguity sets centered at historical samples, lacking adaptivity to varying uncertainty regimes.
AI.method: Proposes Learned Predictive Ambiguity Sets (LPAS): a deep contextual model outputs a nominal scenario distribution, state-dependent Wasserstein radius, and optionally anisotropic ground metric, defining a contextual ambiguity set feeding a DRO decision layer. Trained via conditional quantile calibration, size regularization, and downstream decision loss.
AI.result: Applied to distributionally robust portfolio optimization with 20 S&P 500 constituents (2018-2026), achieving 26.28% annualized return, Sharpe ratio 1.30, final wealth 1.61, with lower tail loss than fixed-radius DRO baselines while using smaller average radius.
AI.conclusion: Learned ambiguity radii recover most of fixed-radius DRO performance while reducing unnecessary conservatism and improving regime adaptivity, demonstrating the value of context-aware robustness in decision-making under uncertainty.

title: A Strong Balanced-Softmax Classifier-Retraining Baseline for Long-Tailed Recognition
summary: Long-tailed recognition methods often modify losses, margins, or representations to reduce the dominance of frequent classes. We ask whether, after Balanced Softmax training, the remaining tail error can be reduced by retraining only the classifier. We evaluate BS-cRT, a two-stage procedure that trains a backbone and cosine classifier with Balanced Softmax, freezes the backbone, and updates only the classifier on balanced episodic batches. The second stage keeps the empirical-prior Balanced Softmax objective and uses raw cosine logits at inference. Across CIFAR-100-LT, CIFAR-10-LT, ImageNet-LT, and Places-LT, this classifier-only step consistently improves Few-shot accuracy over the matched Balanced Softmax checkpoint. At imbalance factor 100, Few-shot gains are +5.15 points on CIFAR-100-LT and +5.83 on CIFAR-10-LT; on ImageNet-LT and Places-LT, gains are +6.92 and +9.78 points, respectively, with a Top-1/Few-shot trade-off on ImageNet-LT. We also analyze Counterfactual Boundary Risk Minimization (CBRM), a boundary-probe extension using prototype-based features near decision boundaries. CBRM identifies two failure modes: scaled-logit cosine margins destabilize training, and corrected hardest-negative probes remain head-class anchored. The results support BS-cRT as a practical classifier-side baseline and indicate that boundary supervision must account for class frequency.

AI.tldr: Two-stage approach for long-tailed recognition: first train backbone with Balanced Softmax, then freeze backbone and retrain only classifier on balanced batches, achieving significant few-shot accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing long-tailed recognition methods modify losses, margins, or representations, but even after Balanced Softmax training, there's remaining tail error. The paper investigates whether classifier-only retraining can further reduce this error.
AI.method: BS-cRT: a two-stage procedure. Stage 1: Train backbone and cosine classifier with Balanced Softmax. Stage 2: Freeze backbone, update only classifier on balanced episodic batches while keeping Balanced Softmax objective, using raw cosine logits at inference. Also analyzes CBRM as boundary-probe extension.
AI.result: Classifier-only retraining consistently improves Few-shot accuracy across multiple datasets: +5.15 points on CIFAR-100-LT (IF=100), +5.83 on CIFAR-10-LT, +6.92 on ImageNet-LT, and +9.78 on Places-LT, with Top-1/Few-shot trade-off on ImageNet-LT.
AI.conclusion: BS-cRT provides a practical classifier-side baseline for long-tailed recognition, showing that classifier-only updates after Balanced Softmax training can significantly boost few-shot performance. Boundary supervision methods need to account for class frequency, as demonstrated by CBRM analysis showing head-class anchoring issues.

title: Exploring Agentic Workflows for Generating High Quality Math Visual Aids
comment: 13 pages, 9 figures. Exploratory course project on agentic workflows for generating and evaluating K 12 mathematical diagrams
summary: Mathematical diagrams play a crucial role in K 12 education, both as problem components and as scaffolding for student comprehension. However, current AI tools, including Large Language Models (LLMs), struggle to reliably generate accurate and pedagogically sound visual diagrams, even when provided with detailed descriptions. A significant gap therefore remains in the reliable generation of diagrams for middle school mathematics. To address this, we introduce an agentic workflow that enables LLM agents to evaluate the quality of generated visuals and use this feedback to iteratively improve their outputs. This self improvement loop aims to enhance the accuracy and educational appropriateness of AI generated diagrams. Our research investigates two questions. First, can LLMs accurately generate quality assurance questions for a visual aid given specific criteria for visual quality? Second, given valid quality assurance questions, can Vision Language Models effectively evaluate generated K 12 visual aids and use the resulting feedback to improve them iteratively? We conduct an exploratory evaluation of our agentic workflow and identify key areas for improvement, including stronger spatial reasoning and more comprehensive coverage of diagram features in the generated quality assurance questions. Our results provide preliminary evidence that this approach can improve the reliability and educational value of AI generated mathematical diagrams.

AI.tldr: An agentic workflow enabling LLM agents to evaluate and iteratively improve AI-generated mathematical diagrams for K-12 education through self-improvement feedback loops.
AI.motivation: Current AI tools, including LLMs, struggle to generate accurate and pedagogically sound visual diagrams for K-12 mathematics education, creating a significant gap in reliable diagram generation for middle school math despite detailed descriptions being available.
AI.method: Introduces an agentic workflow where LLM agents generate quality assurance questions based on visual quality criteria, then Vision Language Models evaluate generated diagrams and use feedback for iterative improvement in a self-improvement loop.
AI.result: Preliminary evidence shows the approach can improve reliability and educational value of AI-generated mathematical diagrams. Exploratory evaluation identifies key improvement areas: stronger spatial reasoning and more comprehensive coverage of diagram features in quality assurance questions.
AI.conclusion: The agentic workflow shows promise for enhancing accuracy and educational appropriateness of AI-generated K-12 mathematical diagrams, though further improvements in spatial reasoning and comprehensive feature coverage are needed for more effective quality assurance.

title: From Direction to Magnitude: How Multimodal Instruction-Tuning Reorganizes the Geometric Encoding of Identity-Specifying Prompts in Transformer Hidden States
comment: 16 pages, 8 tables. Working draft v0.3. Uses Ollivier-Ricci curvature and edge-wise Wasserstein-1 as primary geometric statistic; four models (Gemma-4-E4B, Gemma-4-E4B-it, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct)
summary: We investigate whether identity-specifying system prompts produce statistically distinguishable geometric fingerprints in the hidden-state trajectories of four open-weight transformer language models spanning four post-training regimes: no training (Gemma-4-E4B base), multimodal RLHF (Gemma-4-E4B-it), RL distillation (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B), and SFT (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct). Three prompt conditions (an identity-specifying axis prompt, a length-matched generic-assistant prompt, and a 26-token vanilla baseline) are compared via five geometric metrics, principally the 1-Wasserstein distance between edge-wise distributions of Ollivier-Ricci curvature on k-NN trajectory graphs. Claims rest on trajectory-level permutation tests with multiple geometric controls (teacher-forced content controls, temporal-chain vs k-NN topology, ABT-projected k-NN, angular vs Euclidean graph construction, B=5000 permutations on borderline statistics). The central finding is a qualitative reorganization of identity encoding across the instruction-tuning boundary: in the base model the fingerprint is direction-coded (separation 0.034, p=0.002 under angular k-NN); in the multimodal instruction-tuned model it migrates into the magnitude (angular separation collapses to p=0.439 while Euclidean survives at p=0.042, and the mean norm of the first generated state inverts its length-ordering, being lowest for the identity prompt). This direction-to-magnitude reorganization is specific to the multimodal instruction-tuning regime, absent under RL distillation and SFT. A teacher-forced control attributes ~30% of the free-running cosine signal to prompt-driven effects. We position W_1 on edge-wise Ollivier-Ricci distributions on k-NN trajectory graphs as a methodological contribution of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Transformers develop geometric fingerprints from identity prompts, with a direction-to-magnitude reorganization occurring specifically under multimodal instruction tuning but not other post-training methods.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether identity-specifying system prompts produce statistically distinguishable geometric fingerprints in transformer hidden-state trajectories, and how these fingerprints differ across various post-training regimes (base model, multimodal RLHF, RL distillation, and SFT).
AI.method: Analyzed four transformer models with different post-training using three prompt conditions (identity-specifying, generic-assistant, vanilla baseline). Compared hidden-state trajectories using five geometric metrics, primarily 1-Wasserstein distance between edge-wise distributions of Ollivier-Ricci curvature on k-NN trajectory graphs. Used trajectory-level permutation tests with multiple geometric controls (B=5000 permutations).
AI.result: Found a qualitative reorganization of identity encoding across the instruction-tuning boundary: base model encodes identity in direction (separation 0.034, p=0.002), but multimodal instruction-tuned model encodes it in magnitude (angular separation collapses to p=0.439 while Euclidean survives at p=0.042). This direction-to-magnitude reorganization is specific to multimodal instruction-tuning and absent under RL distillation and SFT. Teacher-forced control shows ~30% of free-running cosine signal from prompt effects.
AI.conclusion: Identity prompts create statistically distinguishable geometric fingerprints that reorganize from direction to magnitude coding specifically under multimodal instruction tuning. The methodological approach using 1-Wasserstein distance on edge-wise Ollivier-Ricci distributions offers a powerful tool for analyzing transformer hidden states.

title: TopoExplore: Topological Discrimination for Archive-Based Exploration
summary: Archive-based exploration methods such as Go-Explore select which visited state to return to using visitation rarity, and frontier methods return to the boundary of the unknown; neither asks whether the unexplored region behind a boundary is enterable at all. Exploration is not just about finding reward - it is about collecting a structurally complete experience for downstream learning and planning. We introduce TopoExplore, which augments Go-Explore cell selection with a periodic topological pass: enclosed unexplored regions (voids) of the visited-set occupancy grid are detected by flood fill (the H1 classes of its cubical complex), and a decaying selection bonus is placed only on their strict entrances (gap or door cells), so sealed regions are never targeted and entered regions retire. On a controlled 18-environment MiniGrid suite (15 seeds, frozen hyperparameters) TopoExplore attains a 1.52x geometric-mean speedup in median steps-to-first-entry over its exact Go-Explore ablation, versus 1.37x for a frontier baseline; frontier exploration degrades when sealed decoy structure appears (0.83-1.48x on decoy environments vs. 1.65-2.11x for TopoExplore), while TopoExplore holds its largest win on hard multi-interaction doors (10.9x). We report an honest negative on Montezuma's Revenge - without wall knowledge, unreachable occupancy artifacts capture the bonus and performance degrades as it grows, isolating the wall-aware entrance test as the load-bearing component - and a preliminary positive on HM3D scanned buildings, where the speedup over Go-Explore tracks scene difficulty (r=0.69) even as frontier selection dominates blanket coverage. The evidence supports a deliberately scoped claim: topology-aware selection pays off where enclosed structure must be discriminated, and remains competitive at open coverage, where frontier methods are strongest, despite not being tuned for that regime.

AI.tldr: TopoExplore improves exploration by focusing only on accessible regions, using topological analysis to identify enterable areas rather than all boundaries.
AI.motivation: Current exploration methods don't distinguish between accessible and inaccessible unexplored regions - they either use visitation rarity (Go-Explore) or target all boundaries (frontier methods), wasting effort on sealed areas that can't actually be entered.
AI.method: TopoExplore augments Go-Explore with periodic topological analysis: uses flood fill to detect enclosed unexplored regions (voids), then places decaying selection bonuses only on their strict entrances (gap/door cells). Sealed regions are never targeted, and entered regions retire naturally.
AI.result: On MiniGrid: 1.52x geometric-mean speedup in median steps-to-first-entry over Go-Explore ablation. Better than frontier baseline (1.37x), especially in environments with sealed decoy structures. Hard multi-interaction doors: 10.9x improvement. Mixed results on Montezuma's Revenge (negative without wall knowledge), positive on HM3D scanned buildings where speedup tracks scene difficulty (r=0.69).
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware selection pays off where enclosed structure must be discriminated, and remains competitive at open coverage despite not being tuned for that regime. The wall-aware entrance test is crucial - performance degrades without it when unreachable artifacts capture the bonus.

title: Nonlinear Axiomatic Attribution for Cooperative Games
summary: The Shapley value is a widely used concept in attribution problems, as it uniquely satisfies the axioms of linearity, consistency, equal treatment, and efficiency. Often, the inclusion AUC metric is used to evaluate the quality of player rankings, in order to identify positively participating players. However, it can be established that the Shapley value is not always reliable for this purpose. The core issue lies in its linearity: the Shapley value acts as a linear operator with an excessively large null space, which is likely to contain non-negligible perturbations that remain indistinguishable to the operator. To address this limitation, we explore the design of nonlinear axiomatic attribution methods. Inspired by the least core, which is a popular nonlinear substitute for the Shapley value, we introduce a class of nonlinear attribution methods that retain the remaining necessary axioms. Each method yields a contribution vector that is the unique optimal solution to a minimization problem, which aims to approximate utility functions as faithfully as possible. In terms of the inclusion AUC metric, our experiments demonstrate the potential effectiveness of these methods compared to Shapley value variants that relax only the efficiency axiom. Our code is available at https://github.com/watml/nonlinear-axiom.

AI.tldr: Shapley value's linearity limits ranking reliability; nonlinear attribution methods inspired by least core address this by optimizing utility approximation.
AI.motivation: The Shapley value, though uniquely satisfying key axioms, is unreliable for identifying positively participating players via inclusion AUC ranking due to its linear nature creating a large null space where perturbations remain indistinguishable.
AI.method: Proposes nonlinear axiomatic attribution methods inspired by least core (a nonlinear Shapley substitute) that preserve necessary axioms while solving minimization problems to best approximate utility functions.
AI.result: Experiments show these nonlinear methods outperform Shapley value variants (relaxing only efficiency axiom) on inclusion AUC metric for identifying positively participating players.
AI.conclusion: Nonlinear attribution methods address Shapley value's linearity limitations and show promise for more reliable player ranking via inclusion AUC, with open-sourced implementation available.

title: Serving the Long Tail: Training-Free LLM Candidate Generation for Vacation Rental Marketplaces
comment: Accepted at TSMO 2026 workshop, co-located with KDD 2026; 9 pages
summary: Vacation rental marketplaces face a structural imbalance on the supply side: a small fraction of properties receive most user interactions, while the long tail of new, niche, and seasonal listings generates too little behavioral signal for collaborative filtering to serve effectively. At Vrbo, item-based k-nearest neighbors (IBKNN) is a core candidate generation channel, but leaves tens of thousands of properties with no candidates and produces weak neighborhoods for sparsely interacted ones. We present a training-free, LLM-based candidate generation pipeline that complements IBKNN using static property metadata alone. An off-the-shelf LLM synthesizes diverse semantic queries per property, a pre-trained text encoder embeds them, and an approximate nearest-neighbor index retrieves candidates from an 11.7M-property catalog. A Union fusion strategy merges these with IBKNN while preserving the behavioral channel's ordering, guaranteeing no degradation on well-served properties, and a downstream learning-to-rank model re-scores the fused pool. Evaluated on 1.6M focal properties, the system extends candidate coverage to tens of thousands of properties IBKNN cannot reach, delivers its largest gains on the long-tail segment where behavioral methods are weakest, and matches or beats IBKNN at every K on shared properties. A downstream learning-to-rank stage further lifts the fused pool, yielding a complete candidate generation and re-ranking stack that serves the long tail without regressing well-served properties. We additionally show that Union fusion collapses the recall gap between a 3B open-weights LLM and frontier API-based models from 27-46% to under 1%, supporting self-hosted small-model deployment at marketplace catalog scale.

AI.tldr: LLM-based candidate generation system complements traditional collaborative filtering to serve vacation rental long-tail properties using semantic queries from static metadata
AI.motivation: Vacation rental marketplaces have structural supply imbalance where small fraction of properties get most interactions, leaving long-tail of new/niche/seasonal listings with insufficient behavioral data for effective collaborative filtering
AI.method: Training-free LLM pipeline: off-the-shelf LLM generates diverse semantic queries from property metadata, text encoder embeds them, approximate nearest-neighbor search retrieves candidates from 11.7M property catalog, Union fusion merges with existing IBKNN while preserving ordering
AI.result: System extends candidate coverage to tens of thousands of properties IBKNN couldn't reach, delivers largest gains on long-tail where behavioral methods are weakest, matches/beats IBKNN on shared properties, and Union fusion reduces recall gap between small and large models to under 1%
AI.conclusion: Complete candidate generation and re-ranking stack serves long-tail properties without regressing well-served properties, enables self-hosted small-model deployment at marketplace scale

authors: Jose Luis Lima de Jesus Silva
title: A Symbolic Neural CPU for Quantization-Simulated Writeback and Interpretable Program Execution
summary: Neural networks can learn algorithmic input-output mappings, but trusting a learned executor requires more than a correct final answer because the state transitions that produce it are usually hidden. To make those transitions visible, we introduce a trace-supervised symbolic neural CPU, a factorized learned execution architecture that combines recurrent control, an explicit operation router over a fixed differentiable arithmetic-logic unit bank, destination-masked register writeback, complete trajectory supervision and matched fixed-point replay. The model exposes the selected operation, source and destination registers, register trajectory, memory signals and writeback semantics at every step. On the principal 16-wide benchmark, the non-quantized executor reproduces reference execution exactly, while the eight-bit quantization-simulated executor preserves the symbolic operation path through programs of 1,000 instructions. When the same execution is evaluated against a matched fixed-point replay, the residual numerical drift disappears, showing that it comes from a mismatch between continuous and low-precision reference semantics rather than from execution failure. We compare recurrent, Transformer, temporal-convolution, temporal graph-inspired and state-space controllers, and the ablations show that operation-gate supervision is necessary for an inspectable execution path. Hidden-opcode memory-pressure tasks expose the remaining limits in delayed state use and temporal binding. We also extend the interface with ValueMemory, hybrid adaptive leaky integrate-and-fire controllers, candidate-constrained symbolic control trained through behaviour cloning and actor-critic reinforcement learning, and an RV32I base-integer semantic bridge. Together, these results establish a trace-verifiable framework for interpretable, low-precision and controllable neural execution.

AI.tldr: A neural CPU architecture with symbolic trace supervision that exposes internal execution states for interpretability and verification.
AI.motivation: Traditional neural networks learn algorithmic mappings but hide internal state transitions, making them untrustworthy. We need interpretable executors where all computation steps are visible and verifiable.
AI.method: Trace-supervised symbolic neural CPU combining recurrent control, explicit operation router over fixed ALU bank, destination-masked register writeback, complete trajectory supervision, and matched fixed-point replay. Includes ValueMemory, LIF controllers, and RL-trained symbolic control.
AI.result: Non-quantized executor reproduces reference execution exactly; 8-bit quantized executor preserves symbolic operation path through 1000 instructions. Residual drift disappears with matched fixed-point replay. Various controller types compared, with operation-gate supervision essential for inspectable execution.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a trace-verifiable framework for interpretable, low-precision, and controllable neural execution, with extensions for memory-intensive tasks and semantic bridging to RV32I instruction set.

title: Nonparametric Bayesian Inverse Reinforcement Learning with Data-Parallel Gibbs Sampling
summary: Inverse Reinforcement Learning recovers reward functions from expert demonstrations, but standard formulations assume that all demonstrations come from a single expert. When demonstrations are pooled from multiple experts with distinct preferences, parametric methods recover an averaged reward that fits no individual expert well. We implement Nonparametric Bayesian Inverse Reinforcement Learning with a Dirichlet Process prior over reward functions, allowing the number of latent reward types to be inferred jointly with the rewards themselves. Inference uses a collapsed Gibbs sampler combining a Chinese Restaurant Process update for cluster assignments with a Metropolis-Hastings update for reward weights, and soft value iteration as the inner planning routine. We evaluate on a 10x10 ObjectWorld grid with two and three ground-truth reward types. The serial sampler recovers K=2 with Adjusted Rand Index of 1.000, substantially outperforming a Maximum Entropy IRL baseline (ARI=0.000). Extension to K=3 shows that the sampler correctly identifies the number of clusters in all runs; assignment ARI of 0.48-0.58 reflects behavioral overlap between expert types that persists across grid instantiations, revealing that reliable K=3 evaluation on ObjectWorld requires controlled object placement rather than random seeding. We further parallelize the sampler across CPU cores using Ray on HPC hardware, achieving a peak speedup of 4.79x at 8 workers, and characterize a throughput-versus-accuracy tradeoff arising from the consensus merge heuristic used during state aggregation. Code and a containerized environment are available at https://github.com/dasashreeya/np_bayes_irl.

AI.tldr: Nonparametric Bayesian IRL using Dirichlet Process prior to handle multiple experts with distinct preferences, outperforming standard IRL baselines and enabling parallel computation.
AI.motivation: Standard IRL assumes demonstrations come from a single expert, but when pooling from multiple experts with distinct preferences, parametric methods recover only an averaged reward that fits no individual expert well.
AI.method: Nonparametric Bayesian IRL with Dirichlet Process prior over reward functions to infer number of latent reward types jointly with rewards; inference uses collapsed Gibbs sampler with Chinese Restaurant Process for cluster assignments and Metropolis-Hastings for reward weights, with soft value iteration as planning routine.
AI.result: Successfully recovers K=2 with perfect Adjusted Rand Index (1.000), substantially outperforming Maximum Entropy IRL baseline (ARI=0.000). For K=3, correctly identifies number of clusters but assignment ARI is lower (0.48-0.58) due to behavioral overlap. Parallel implementation achieves 4.79x speedup with 8 workers.
AI.conclusion: Nonparametric Bayesian approach effectively handles multiple expert preferences in IRL, with parallel implementation showing practical scalability, though evaluation on complex domains requires careful experimental design due to behavioral overlap challenges.

title: AgentAbstain: Do LLM Agents Know When Not to Act?
summary: Agent systems based on large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for autonomous tasks, yet existing evaluations mostly focus on task success rather than whether agents know when to abstain. This gap poses real risks: under ambiguity, conflicting constraints, or tool failures, agents may execute unintended and irreversible actions. To close this gap, we present the first systematic evaluation framework for agentic abstention: the calibrated ability of tool-using LLM agents to recognize when not to act. At its core, AgentAbstain is a paired-task benchmark built on an agent-native taxonomy of 8 abstention scenarios across pre-execution reasoning and runtime discovery. It contains 263 paired tasks across 42 executable sandbox environments, where each pair consists of a should-act task and a should-abstain variant produced through a controlled perturbation to the instruction, tool, or environment state. To scale this paired design and resist data contamination, we propose AbstainGen, a fully automated pipeline that synthesizes sandbox environments and generates paired tasks end-to-end, validated by deterministic replay and semantic LLM judges; fresh task instances can be regenerated on demand, and three independent annotators rate 94-98% of sampled tasks as well-designed. Across 17 frontier LLMs in 4 agent harnesses, the best agent (Gemini 3.1 Pro) achieves only 59.5% paired accuracy (correct on both the act and abstain sides of each paired task). More importantly, abstention capability is largely independent of general task-solving capability, indicating that scaling task-solving alone will not close this gap. We further identify failure modes such as post-hoc abstention, in which agents execute irreversible actions before recognizing abstention triggers. Our code and dataset are open-sourced at agentabstain.github.io.

AI.tldr: AgentAbstain: First systematic framework evaluating LLM agents' ability to know when not to act in ambiguous situations, showing even top agents fail 40% of paired abstention tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent evaluations focus only on task success, ignoring critical abstention capability. This creates real risks where agents may execute unintended irreversible actions under ambiguity, conflicting constraints, or tool failures.
AI.method: Introduced AgentAbstain benchmark with paired-task design: should-act vs should-abstain tasks across 8 abstention scenarios. Used AbstainGen automated pipeline to synthesize 263 paired tasks in 42 sandbox environments, validated by deterministic replay and LLM judges.
AI.result: Even best agent (Gemini 3.1 Pro) achieved only 59.5% paired accuracy. Abstention capability is independent of general task-solving ability. Identified failure modes like post-hoc abstention where agents act before recognizing triggers.
AI.conclusion: Scaling task-solving alone won't fix abstention gap. Systematic evaluation and improvement of agent abstention capability is crucial for safe deployment of autonomous LLM agents in real-world applications.

title: Remembering Distinct Items, Not Tokens: A Learnable Dirichlet-Process Cache Between State-Space Models and Attention
comment: 16 pages, 9 figures. Companion paper on event-log applications forthcoming
summary: Fixed-state sequence models compress an unbounded past into a bounded state, which caps their associative recall at roughly the state dimension; attention escapes the cap by keeping a key-value entry for every token, at quadratic compute and a cache that grows with the sequence. We study the middle ground: a sparse cache that allocates a slot only when an input is novel, so its size tracks the number of distinct items rather than the number of tokens. The allocation rule is the DP-means clustering rule, the small-variance limit of a Dirichlet-process mixture, used not as latent-variable inference but as the key-value memory operator for a deep recurrent backbone. We develop it in two forms, a static cache with a fixed concentration and a surprise-adaptive variant whose concentration follows the recent novelty rate. On a controlled associative-recall benchmark with redundancy we show that the cache matches full-attention recall while storing only the distinct items, that it dominates a fixed-budget eviction cache on the recall-versus-size frontier, and that on a state-space backbone it answers both a recall query and a long-range aggregate at the lowest memory of any model tested. The allocation is learnable end to end: a two-parameter novelty-threshold gate trained on the task loss alone recovers the rule exactly, whereas an over-parameterized gate fails, so the operative ingredient is the inductive bias rather than capacity. The evidence is a family of controlled mechanism studies at modest scale, with the distinct-items property confirmed on four real streams (recommendation, systems logs, clinical events, and insurance claims); a real-backbone, real-corpus language validation is pursued in a companion study.

AI.tldr: A novel sparse cache architecture using DP-means clustering allocation for key-value memory in sequence models, achieving associative recall while storing only distinct items rather than all tokens.
AI.motivation: Fixed-state sequence models have limited associative recall capacity (capped by state dimension), while attention models suffer from quadratic compute and growing cache sizes. Need a middle ground that efficiently handles sequences with redundancy.
AI.method: Uses DP-means clustering rule (small-variance limit of Dirichlet-process mixture) as key-value memory operator for deep recurrent backbone. Two variants: static cache with fixed concentration and surprise-adaptive variant with concentration following recent novelty rate. Novelty allocation is learnable end-to-end via a two-parameter novelty-threshold gate.
AI.result: Matches full-attention recall while storing only distinct items; dominates fixed-budget eviction cache on recall-versus-size frontier; achieves both recall queries and long-range aggregates with lowest memory of tested models. Two-parameter gate trained on task loss recovers allocation rule exactly while over-parameterized gates fail, showing inductive bias is key.
