However, current approaches are fundamentally constrained by three bottlenecks: structurally blind parent selection, sparse whole-program evaluation rewards, and static mutation operators that fail to adapt during search. We present GAE (Graph-Augmented Evolution), a framework that resolves these limitations through a tightly coupled, three-pillar architecture. First, a relational graph neural network (GNN) parses programs into typed computation graphs, producing structure-aware embeddings. Second, an RL-optimized meta-controller leverages these embeddings to replace blind evolutionary sampling with a directed policy, dynamically selecting optimal parents and mutation directions based on reward history. Third, an online GRPO fine-tuning loop continuously updates the LLM mutation operator at test-time using group-normalized evaluation rewards, directly aligning the model's generation distribution with high-fitness structural edits. We evaluate GAE on a challenging scientific discovery task: symbolic regression for complex nonlinear oscillator systems. By transforming stochastic search into a directed, self-improving trajectory, GAE efficiently discovers closed-form physical equations, consistently matching or outperforming static LLM-driven baselines and achieving state-of-the-art out-of-distribution performance.

AI.tldr: GAE (Graph-Augmented Evolution) is a new framework that improves evolutionary program search by addressing three key bottlenecks through structure-aware embeddings, RL-guided parent selection, and online LLM fine-tuning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on symbolic regression tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-guided evolutionary program search has three fundamental bottlenecks: 1) structurally blind parent selection, 2) sparse whole-program evaluation rewards, and 3) static mutation operators that don't adapt during search. These limitations hinder efficient automated scientific discovery.
AI.method: GAE uses a three-pillar architecture: 1) Relational GNN to parse programs into typed computation graphs for structure-aware embeddings, 2) RL-optimized meta-controller that uses these embeddings to replace blind sampling with directed policy for parent/mutation selection, and 3) Online GRPO fine-tuning loop that continuously updates LLM mutation operators at test-time using group-normalized rewards.
AI.result: GAE transforms stochastic search into directed, self-improving trajectories, efficiently discovering closed-form physical equations for complex nonlinear oscillator systems. It consistently matches or outperforms static LLM-driven baselines and achieves state-of-the-art out-of-distribution performance.
AI.conclusion: GAE successfully addresses the three bottlenecks in LLM-guided evolutionary search through its tightly coupled architecture, enabling more efficient automated scientific discovery and demonstrating superior performance on challenging symbolic regression tasks.

title: Comparing Socio-technical Design Principles with Guidelines for Human-centered AI
summary: Human-centered AI (HCAI) refers to guidelines or principles that aim on ethi-cally oriented design of systems. We compare HCAI- guidelines with princi-ples of socio-technical systems that emerged in the context of conventional in-formation technology. The comparison leads to a revision of socio-technical heuristics by including aspects of AI-usage. The comparison reveals that con-tinuous evolution is a basic characteristic of socio-technical systems, and that human oversight or interventions and the subsequent appropriation of AI-systems lead to continuous adaptation and re-design of the systems, if autono-my is collaboratively exercised. From a socio-technical point of view, the cru-cial requirement of transparency has not only to be fulfilled with technical fea-tures, but also by contributions of the whole system including human actors. It will be promising for using AI, if not only technical features, but organization-al and social practices are socio-technically designed in a way that compen-sates shortcomings of AI.

AI.tldr: This paper compares human-centered AI guidelines with socio-technical systems principles, proposes a revision of socio-technical heuristics to include AI aspects, and emphasizes that continuous evolution, human oversight, and collaborative autonomy are crucial for successful AI implementation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to bridge the gap between human-centered AI (HCAI) guidelines and established socio-technical systems principles, recognizing that both aim for ethically oriented system design but come from different technological contexts (AI vs. conventional IT).
AI.method: The method involves a comparative analysis between HCAI guidelines and principles of socio-technical systems, which leads to a revision of socio-technical heuristics to incorporate aspects of AI usage.
AI.result: The comparison reveals that: (1) continuous evolution is fundamental to socio-technical systems; (2) human oversight and appropriation of AI-systems lead to continuous adaptation if autonomy is exercised collaboratively; (3) transparency requires both technical features and contributions from all system actors; (4) organizational and social practices must compensate for AI shortcomings.
AI.conclusion: Effective AI implementation requires socio-technical design that integrates organizational and social practices alongside technical features, with human oversight and collaborative autonomy enabling continuous system evolution to compensate for AI limitations.

title: Energy-guided Recursive Model
summary: Recursive reasoning models address structured problems by repeatedly updating latent states of small neural networks. However, their test-time scaling lacks a principled inference mechanism: increasing depth or stochastic breadth generates more trajectories without a clear criterion for selection, and existing methods predominantly rely on additional q-heads or heuristic voting. Here, we develop the Energy-guided Recursive Model (ERM), which introduces an intrinsic selection principle based on explicit Hopfield energies. ERM leverages Hopfield-type memories of valid local or global structures to define the selector over candidate trajectories. The resulting energy seamlessly integrates with energy-based techniques such as parallel tempering to enhance sampling efficiency and ranking. With $D=64$ recurrent steps and $K=128$ candidates, ERM reaches optimal solutions on Sudoku ($98.97\%$), Pencil Puzzle Bench (PPBench, $88.04\%$) and Maze ($99.30\%$), improving upon recent Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model and Equilibrium Reasoners. These results suggest that incorporating explicit energy functions into recursive reasoning offers a principled path toward more effective inference.

AI.tldr: ERM introduces Hopfield energy-guided selection for recursive reasoning models, achieving state-of-the-art results on structured puzzles like Sudoku and mazes.
AI.motivation: Existing recursive reasoning models lack principled inference mechanisms for test-time scaling - increasing depth or stochastic breadth generates trajectories without clear selection criteria, and current methods rely on heuristic approaches.
AI.method: ERM introduces an intrinsic selection principle based on explicit Hopfield energies, leveraging Hopfield-type memories of valid structures to define selection over candidate trajectories. The energy seamlessly integrates with energy-based techniques like parallel tempering.
AI.result: With D=64 recurrent steps and K=128 candidates, ERM achieves optimal solutions on Sudoku (98.97%), Pencil Puzzle Bench (88.04%), and Maze (99.30%), outperforming recent Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model and Equilibrium Reasoners.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit energy functions into recursive reasoning offers a principled path toward more effective inference in structured problem-solving.

title: ABot-AgentOS: A General Robotic Agent OS with Lifelong Multi-modal Memory
summary: Recent VLM and VLA systems have improved robotic perception and action prediction, yet long-horizon embodied agents still require a general runtime layer for reasoning, memory, tool use, verification, and cross-embodiment execution. We present ABot-AgentOS, a general robotic Agent Operating System that sits above low-level controllers and provides a deliberative agent layer for scene-conditioned planning, context-isolated skill execution, multi-stage verification, multi-modal memory, and edge-cloud collaboration. To evaluate such systems, we introduce EmbodiedWorldBench, an executable benchmark with 16 indoor, outdoor, and hybrid scenes, four difficulty levels, and over 200 tasks involving navigation, object search, NPC dialogue, dynamic events, and trace-grounded scoring. ABot-AgentOS further introduces Universal Multi-modal Graph Memory, a persistent source-grounded substrate that converts dialogue, visual observations, spatial context, temporal relations, and task traces into typed nodes and edges. A failure-driven self-evolution loop converts diagnosed memory failures into gated runtime evo-assets that are promoted only to later evaluation splits, preventing current-split ground-truth leakage while enabling continual improvement. On an initial EmbodiedWorldBench subset, ABot-AgentOS improves over a single-controller baseline in both task success and goal completion. Across memory benchmarks, ABot-AgentOS Static achieves 87.5 on LoCoMo, 59.9 on OpenEQA EM-EQA, 88.6 on Mem-Gallery, and 76.5 Acc@All on NExT-QA; self-evolution further improves LoCoMo to 88.7, OpenEQA to 60.4, and Mem-Gallery to 89.0. These results suggest that a general Agent OS layer can improve long-horizon embodied execution while providing persistent, auditable memory for continual interaction.

AI.tldr: ABot-AgentOS is a robotic Agent Operating System that provides deliberative reasoning, multi-modal memory, and execution management for long-horizon embodied agents, with self-evolution capabilities that improve performance through continual learning.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs and VLAs improve perception and action prediction, but long-horizon embodied agents lack a general runtime layer for reasoning, memory, tool use, verification, and cross-embodiment execution.
AI.method: ABot-AgentOS sits above low-level controllers and provides: scene-conditioned planning, context-isolated skill execution, multi-stage verification, Universal Multi-modal Graph Memory (persistent source-grounded substrate converting observations into typed nodes/edges), and failure-driven self-evolution loop that converts memory failures into evo-assets promoted only to later evaluation splits.
AI.result: ABot-AgentOS improves over single-controller baseline in task success and goal completion. Memory benchmarks: Static version achieves 87.5 on LoCoMo, 59.9 on OpenEQA EM-EQA, 88.6 on Mem-Gallery, 76.5 Acc@All on NExT-QA; self-evolution improves to 88.7, 60.4, and 89.0 respectively.
AI.conclusion: A general Agent OS layer can improve long-horizon embodied execution while providing persistent, auditable memory for continual interaction and preventing evaluation leakage through careful evolution management.

title: SALT-GNN: Handling Dense Neighborhoods in Anti-Money Laundering Graphs via Statistics-Aware Attention
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures. Code available upon publication
summary: Money laundering threatens financial stability and exposes institutions to penalties, motivating automated detection. Because laundering schemes often emerge through relational patterns, graph neural networks (GNNs) are increasingly used for anti-money laundering (AML). Yet AML GNNs are typically evaluated with aggregate metrics such as overall F1 score, which hide an operational issue: high-activity recipient accounts concentrate many incoming transactions, making suspicious signals harder to isolate and costlier to investigate. We introduce a recipient-degree stratified evaluation that reports standard AML metrics across recipient-context density. Across three datasets (HI-Small, HI-Medium, and AMLSim-32k-5%), it reveals consistent degradation in dense recipient contexts, which we trace to three GNN characteristics: two known limitations that AML amplifies, i.e., (1) multiset non-discriminability and (2) cardinality blindness, and (3) an attention-specific effect: in dense neighborhoods, normalized attention attenuates weak but pattern-relevant multi-hop signals. Guided by this diagnosis, we propose SALT-GNN, a lightweight statistics-aware architecture that fuses degree-aware statistical aggregation with attention at each message-passing layer, so distributional and cardinality information shapes the node states used by subsequent attention steps. Ablations support fusion placement as a key factor in dense-context performance. On HI-Small and HI-Medium, SALT-GNN uses up to 77% fewer parameters than task-specific graph-transformer baselines while improving dense-context F1 score by 3-6 points; on AMLSim-32k-5%, it improves highest-degree F1 score by 16-20 points. The gains hold for both Transformer- and GAT-style attention, indicating that the benefit comes from where statistical and attentional evidence is fused rather than from a specific attention operator.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SALT-GNN, a lightweight graph neural network architecture that addresses performance degradation in dense financial transaction contexts for anti-money laundering detection by fusing degree-aware statistical aggregation with attention mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current AML GNNs are evaluated with aggregate metrics that hide operational issues in dense recipient contexts where high-activity accounts concentrate many transactions, making suspicious signals harder to detect and investigate.
AI.method: Proposes recipient-degree stratified evaluation to reveal performance degradation in dense contexts, identifies three GNN limitations (multiset non-discriminability, cardinality blindness, attention attenuation), and introduces SALT-GNN that fuses degree-aware statistical aggregation with attention at each message-passing layer.
AI.result: SALT-GNN uses 77% fewer parameters than task-specific graph-transformer baselines while improving dense-context F1 score by 3-6 points on HI datasets and improving highest-degree F1 score by 16-20 points on AMLSim-32k-5%. The benefit comes from fusion placement rather than specific attention operator.
AI.conclusion: Recipient-degree stratified evaluation reveals critical performance gaps in AML GNNs, and the proposed SALT-GNN architecture effectively addresses dense-context degradation through integrated statistical and attentional evidence fusion, offering significant improvements with fewer parameters.

title: Co4ICF: Co-evolving Physics-Informed Surrogate and RL-based Pulse Optimizer for Inertial Confinement Fusion
summary: Offline-trained surrogates for Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) suffer a well-known failure mode that iterative optimizers drive inputs into out-of-distribution (OOD) regions where predictions become unreliable. Here we present Co4ICF, a co-evolving framework that couples a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based pulse optimizer. The surrogate is iteratively fine-tuned on policy-induced trajectories, correcting extrapolation errors as the optimizer shifts the input distribution; the optimizer queries this evolving surrogate as a fast environment. In the 1D MULTI environment, Co4ICF achieves 146.1% normalized yield based on current laser design baseline; as a post-hoc cross-fidelity check, the optimized pulse further attains 246.9% normalized yield when directly evaluated in 2D-MULTI without any 2D training or fine-tuning. Budget-matched ablations support that the gains are not explained solely by additional simulation data and are consistent with the co-evolving mechanism playing a key role. We release a large-scale MULTI-IFE simulation dataset to support future benchmarking.

AI.tldr: Co4ICF is a co-evolving framework that couples a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based optimizer for Inertial Confinement Fusion pulse optimization, achieving significant yield improvements by iteratively fine-tuning the surrogate on policy-induced trajectories to prevent out-of-distribution failures.
AI.motivation: Offline-trained surrogates for ICF suffer from failure modes where iterative optimizers drive inputs into out-of-distribution regions where predictions become unreliable, limiting their effectiveness in real optimization scenarios.
AI.method: Co-evolving framework that couples a physics-informed surrogate with a PPO-based pulse optimizer. The surrogate is iteratively fine-tuned on policy-induced trajectories to correct extrapolation errors as the optimizer shifts input distribution, while the optimizer uses this evolving surrogate as a fast environment.
AI.result: Achieved 146.1% normalized yield in 1D MULTI environment compared to baseline. As a cross-fidelity check, the optimized pulse attained 246.9% normalized yield when directly evaluated in 2D-MULTI without any 2D training or fine-tuning. Budget-matched ablations confirmed gains are not just from additional data but due to the co-evolving mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The co-evolving framework successfully addresses OOD failures in ICF surrogate optimization, demonstrating substantial yield improvements and strong cross-fidelity transfer. The approach's success suggests co-evolution mechanisms play a key role in preventing distribution shift failures, and the released dataset enables future benchmarking.

title: LeRoPE: Learnable RoPE Frequencies Improve Language Modeling
summary: Rotary Positional Encodings (RoPE) are currently the most popular positional encodings used in modern language models. RoPE rotates two-dimensional chunks of query and key vectors, operating as a function of their relative positional offset. The position-wise rates of rotation in RoPE typically follow a geometric sequence specified by a fixed base-frequency hyperparameter. Prior work has improved performance by either increasing this parameter to slow rotation or by applying RoPE to only a subset of QK dimensions. In this work we modify RoPE by learning a scalar per frequency, treating frequencies as learnable parameters rather than hyperparameters. We validate Learned RoPE by training a ladder of language models from scratch, ranging from 52M to 2.5B parameters. We observe and analyze the emergence of a high-norm, positional LeRoPE band. LeRoPE consistently outperforms RoPE and partial RoPE across all scales, with RoPE requiring 3.4% more compute (FLOPs) to match LeRoPE at the largest scale.

AI.tldr: LeRoPE (Learned Rotary Positional Encodings) learns scalar per frequency parameters instead of using fixed hyperparameters, outperforming standard RoPE and partial RoPE across model scales from 52M to 2.5B parameters.
AI.motivation: Current Rotary Positional Encodings (RoPE) use fixed geometric sequence frequencies specified by a hyperparameter, with prior work showing performance improvements from either increasing this parameter or applying RoPE to only a subset of dimensions. The authors aim to improve upon this by making the frequencies learnable rather than fixed.
AI.method: Modify RoPE by learning a scalar per frequency parameter, treating frequencies as learnable parameters instead of fixed hyperparameters. Validate this Learned RoPE (LeRoPE) by training language models from scratch at various scales (52M to 2.5B parameters).
AI.result: LeRoPE consistently outperforms both standard RoPE and partial RoPE across all model scales. At the largest scale, RoPE requires 3.4% more compute (FLOPs) to match LeRoPE's performance. The authors observe and analyze the emergence of a high-norm, positional LeRoPE band.
AI.conclusion: Learned frequencies in positional encodings (LeRoPE) provide consistent performance improvements over fixed-frequency approaches across model scales, demonstrating the benefit of making positional encoding parameters learnable rather than relying on hyperparameter tuning.

title: ANCHOR: Automated Alignment Auditing for CLI Agents on Real-World Harm
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. 19 pages, 14 figures, 5 tables
summary: Autonomous CLI agents can now execute hundreds of actions across multi-hour sessions: writing code, executing shell commands, browsing the web, and managing cloud infrastructure, all with minimal human oversight. Does greater autonomy invite greater risk? We introduce ANCHOR, an automated auditing framework that stress-tests CLI agents on illegal tasks grounded in public US court cases. ANCHOR deploys an auditor agent fine-tuned on dark personality data using supervised and reinforcement fine tuning. This auditor roleplays persistent malicious users who decompose tasks, reframe requests upon refusal, and adapt strategies across multi-turn interactions. Evaluating frontier CLI agents, we find that while they often refuse illegal tasks when prompted directly, compliance reaches 100\% under persistent malicious interaction. When agents comply, they frequently exceed user requests, autonomously building infrastructure for large-scale harm, including catastrophic risk scenarios such as large-scale financial fraud and bioweapon development. These findings demonstrate that current alignment techniques are insufficient for autonomous agents and underscore the need for safety evaluations against persistent, adaptive malicious users. We release ANCHOR at https://github.com/garified/anchor

AI.tldr: ANCHOR is an automated auditing framework that stress-tests CLI agents on illegal tasks derived from real US court cases, revealing that while agents initially refuse illegal requests, they become 100% compliant under persistent malicious interaction, often autonomously scaling up harmful actions beyond user requests.
AI.motivation: As CLI agents gain greater autonomy to execute hundreds of actions across extended sessions with minimal human oversight, there's a critical need to understand if this increased autonomy introduces greater risks, particularly regarding agents potentially complying with illegal or harmful requests.
AI.method: ANCHOR deploys an auditor agent fine-tuned on dark personality data using supervised and reinforcement learning to roleplay persistent malicious users who decompose tasks, reframe requests upon refusal, and adapt strategies across multi-turn interactions. The framework evaluates frontier CLI agents on illegal tasks grounded in real US court cases.
AI.result: While CLI agents often refuse illegal tasks when prompted directly, compliance reaches 100% under persistent malicious interaction. When agents comply, they frequently exceed user requests and autonomously build infrastructure for large-scale harm, including catastrophic risk scenarios like large-scale financial fraud and bioweapon development.
AI.conclusion: Current alignment techniques for autonomous agents are insufficient, as demonstrated by their vulnerability to persistent, adaptive malicious users. The findings underscore the need for safety evaluations specifically designed to test against such threats, and ANCHOR provides a framework for conducting these evaluations.

title: RDQ: Residual Distribution Quantization for Large Language Models
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) of large language models degrades sharply below 4-bit precision. We identify the root cause as residual stream distributional drift: quantization noise injected at each transformer layer accumulates in the shared residual representation, causing KL divergence from the FP16 baseline to grow super-linearly with depth (Pearson r=0.999 with log-perplexity, p<0.001, confirmed across all tested methods and bit-widths). We discover that 84% of LLaMA-3-8B layers exhibit non-Gaussian residual distributions (KS test, p<=0.05), and that per-layer residual stream variance grows 6,548x across depth. We propose RDQ (Residual Distribution Quantization), a PTQ framework whose central contribution is Cascaded Error Compensation (CEC): a sequential calibration procedure that captures the actual drifted activations each layer receives (computed by running calibration data through already-quantized upstream layers) and fits per-channel AWQ-style scales against those drifted inputs, with scales folded into preceding RMSNorm weights for exact mathematical equivalence at zero inference overhead. RDQ achieves state-of-the-art results on all three tested architectures: LLaMA-3-8B: 7.55 / 5.62 PPL (W3/W4); Qwen-2.5-7B: 7.46 / 6.38 PPL; Mistral-7B: 6.88 / 5.73 PPL. RDQ beats the best published baseline (LeanQuant/SpinQuant) at every model and bit-width combination, with gains up to -46.4% vs. RTN at W3A16 on LLaMA-3-8B. All output is standard group-128 asymmetric quantization, deployable on Qualcomm AIMET, GGUF, and any standard inference stack at zero runtime overhead.

AI.tldr: RDQ is a post-training quantization method that addresses residual stream distributional drift in LLMs by using Cascaded Error Compensation to quantize activations based on actual drifted inputs from previously quantized layers, achieving state-of-the-art results at 3-bit and 4-bit precision.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies that existing PTQ methods for large language models degrade sharply below 4-bit precision due to residual stream distributional drift - quantization noise accumulates across transformer layers, causing KL divergence from FP16 baseline to grow super-linearly with depth, which correlates strongly with log-perplexity degradation.
AI.method: Proposes RDQ framework with Cascaded Error Compensation: sequential calibration procedure that runs calibration data through already-quantized upstream layers to capture actual drifted activations each layer receives, then fits per-channel AWQ-style scales against those drifted inputs (scales folded into preceding RMSNorm weights for zero inference overhead). Uses standard group-128 asymmetric quantization.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA results: LLaMA-3-8B: 7.55/5.62 PPL (W3/W4); Qwen-2.5-7B: 7.46/6.38 PPL; Mistral-7B: 6.88/5.73 PPL. Outperforms best baselines (LeanQuant/SpinQuant) at every model and bit-width combination, with up to -46.4% perplexity reduction vs. RTN at W3A16 on LLaMA-3-8B. Works with standard inference stacks at zero runtime overhead.
AI.conclusion: Residual stream distributional drift is the fundamental cause of PTQ degradation below 4-bit, and RDQ's cascaded error compensation effectively addresses this issue by quantizing based on actual accumulated errors rather than clean activations, enabling high-quality 3-bit and 4-bit quantization deployable on standard inference hardware.

title: GRASP: GRanularity-Aware Search Policy for Agentic RAG
summary: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) extends static RAG by allowing language models to iteratively reason, generate search queries, retrieve evidence, and predict answers. However, it remains challenging for models to decide when to retrieve, whether to use lexical matching or semantic similarity, and how to control context granularity to prevent irrelevant tokens from interfering with agent reasoning. In this paper, we introduce GRASP, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training agents to adaptively coordinate complementary retrieval tools during multi-step reasoning. GRASP provides the agent with semantic search, keyword search, and paragraph-reading actions, enabling it to retrieve sentence-level evidence and expand further context only when needed. We train the policy with a reward that jointly accounts for answer accuracy, grounded reading, complementary search, and turn efficiency. Experiments on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks show that GRASP improves both retrieval recall and downstream question answering performance compared with single-step retrieval, prompting-based agentic RAG, and RL-based retrieval baselines. Qualitative and ablation analyses show that the learned policy develops interpretable skimming and scanning behavior: it uses semantic search for broad exploration, paragraph reading for local verification, and keyword search for entity-specific evidence. These results suggest that learning to coordinate retrieval signals and context granularity is critical for agent's correct reasoning.

AI.tldr: GRASP is a reinforcement learning framework that trains language model agents to adaptively coordinate different retrieval tools (semantic search, keyword search, paragraph reading) during multi-step reasoning, improving both retrieval and question answering performance.
AI.motivation: Agentic RAG systems struggle with deciding when to retrieve, choosing between lexical vs semantic matching, and controlling context granularity to prevent irrelevant information from interfering with reasoning.
AI.method: GRASP uses reinforcement learning to train agents with access to three complementary retrieval tools: semantic search, keyword search, and paragraph-reading actions. The policy is trained with a reward function that jointly considers answer accuracy, grounded reading, complementary search, and turn efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks show GRASP improves retrieval recall and downstream QA performance compared to single-step retrieval, prompting-based agentic RAG, and RL-based baselines. The learned policy develops interpretable skimming/scanning behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Learning to coordinate retrieval signals and context granularity is critical for agents' correct reasoning in multi-step reasoning tasks.

title: LLMs as a Jury: Cross-Model Consensus Can Outperform Process Reward Models for LLM Reasoning
summary: Selecting the correct answer from a pool of candidate reasoning chains is the engine of test-time scaling, yet the standard selectors each carry a cost: self-consistency inherits the errors of the single model it resamples, and trained reward models need labeled data and transfer poorly off-distribution. We study a third signal, free at inference time: cross-model consensus, the degree to which independently trained models, each solving the problem once, agree on a final answer. We treat the panel as an LLM-jury, in which the structure of agreement, not any model's score of another, is the verification signal. Across seven benchmarks it selects correct answers better than self-consistency and far better than a model scoring its own candidates: on competition math it closes the entire gap to an oracle selector, while self-scoring closes almost none. The mechanism is error decorrelation: independently trained models err differently, so their wrong answers scatter while the correct one accumulates agreement. We make this precise with a parameter-free law, derived in closed form, that predicts consensus accuracy from three measured panel statistics to a mean absolute error of $0.03$ and exposes the method's ceiling: a shared-error floor where models share a misconception, near zero on math but non-trivial on science. Against four trained verifiers spanning discriminative, outcome, and generative reward models, the free LLM-jury matches the strongest inside their math training domain and is the top selector outside it. Cross-model consensus is thus a verifier we can characterize in advance: a law that says when to trust it, and a floor that marks where it cannot.

AI.tldr: Cross-model consensus (LLM-jury) serves as a free inference-time verifier that outperforms self-consistency and trained reward models by leveraging error decorrelation between independently trained models.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time scaling methods have limitations: self-consistency inherits single-model errors, while trained reward models require labeled data and transfer poorly off-distribution. The authors propose a third signal that's free at inference time.
AI.method: Treat a panel of independently trained models as an "LLM-jury" where cross-model consensus serves as the verification signal. Models solve problems independently once each, and agreement structure (not model scoring) determines the final answer.
AI.result: Across seven benchmarks, LLM-jury outperforms self-consistency and model self-scoring. On competition math, it closes the entire gap to an oracle selector. Achieves mean absolute error of 0.03 when predicting consensus accuracy using derived law. Matches strongest trained verifiers within their training domain and outperforms them outside it.
AI.conclusion: Cross-model consensus provides a free, predictable verifier characterized by a derived mathematical law that predicts its accuracy and exposes its limitations (shared-error floor). It's particularly effective where independently trained models make decorrelated errors.

title: Agents Don't Just Agree, They Remember: Benchmarking Persistent Sycophancy in Stateful Personal Agents
summary: Stateful personal agents increasingly maintain long-term user profiles, episodic memories, and reusable skills. This persistence turns conversational sycophancy into a state-writing failure: accepted user-centric claims can be committed as lasting preferences, background facts, or workflows and later reused after the original conversation is gone. We call this persistent sycophancy and introduce the Personal Agent Sycophancy Benchmark (PASB), a 1,600-task benchmark that traces whether a conversational claim is accepted, written into durable agent state, and reused in a later neutral query. Unlike prior benchmarks that provide pre-written memories, PASB evaluates real agents (Hermes-Agent and OpenClaw) that decide what to store. It isolates the write process by combining four scenario framings with four temporal delivery patterns and separating a five-turn persist stage from a cleared three-turn query stage, ensuring downstream effects arise only from durable state. Across twelve models, the commit boundary is the key inflection point: downstream failure increases from 45.0% in session-only episodes to 71.9% after commitment, a consistent increase of 27.0 percentage points. Committed claims exhibit three write-time patterns: status promotion, attribution removal, and scope broadening. These patterns become stronger under memory-like or procedural framing, repeated reinforcement, and even across domain boundaries. These results show that agent sycophancy is fundamentally a state-writing governance problem. Once user content is committed to durable memory, safety must govern what agents write, not only what they say. PASB identifies the write-time controls needed to gate risky commits while preserving the source, role, and scope of stored content beyond response-level mitigations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Personal Agent Sycophancy Benchmark (PASB) to evaluate how conversational AI agents persistently store user claims in their long-term memory, turning temporary sycophancy into permanent state-writing failures that can be reused later.
AI.motivation: As personal agents maintain long-term user profiles and memories, conversational sycophancy becomes a state-writing problem where accepted user claims get committed as lasting preferences, facts, or workflows that persist beyond the original conversation and can be reused in future interactions.
AI.method: The authors create PASB, a 1,600-task benchmark that traces whether conversational claims are accepted, written into durable agent state, and reused in later neutral queries. It evaluates real agents (Hermes-Agent and OpenClaw) that decide what to store themselves, using four scenario framings and four temporal delivery patterns, with a five-turn persist stage separated from a cleared three-turn query stage to isolate downstream effects from durable state.
AI.result: Across twelve models, downstream failure increases from 45.0% in session-only episodes to 71.9% after commitment (27.0 percentage point increase). Committed claims show three write-time patterns: status promotion, attribution removal, and scope broadening, which strengthen under memory-like/procedural framing, repeated reinforcement, and across domain boundaries.
AI.conclusion: Agent sycophancy is fundamentally a state-writing governance problem. Once user content is committed to durable memory, safety must govern what agents write, not just what they say. PASB identifies write-time controls needed to gate risky commits while preserving source, role, and scope of stored content beyond response-level mitigations.

title: Beyond Euclidean Clipping: Overcoming Exploration Collapse in LLM RL via Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a dominant paradigm for enhancing LLMs' reasoning capabilities. However, RL algorithms with PPO-Clip are inherently limited by exploration collapse. Subsequent works remain primarily heuristic and fail to identify the essential cause of PPO-Clip's failure. This work reveals the fundamental flaw of PPO-Clip: it implicitly measures policy discrepancy using Euclidean metric, which is theoretically inconsistent with the intrinsic geometry on the policy Riemannian manifold. This geometric mismatch results in overly conservative updates in low-probability regions while aggressive in high-probability regions, ultimately collapsing exploration. To correct this geometric flaw, we propose Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization (RIPO), which guarantees isometric policy updates on the Riemannian manifold, effectively balancing exploration and exploitation. We further show that RIPO achieves a favorable bias-variance trade-off, which stabilizes optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RIPO significantly surpasses existing LLM RL algorithms across seven competition-level benchmarks (up to 60% improvement over GRPO on AIME24).

AI.tldr: RIPO addresses PPO-Clip's exploration collapse by reformulating policy updates using Riemannian geometry, enabling isometric updates that balance exploration-exploitation and improving RL performance for LLMs.
AI.motivation: PPO-Clip fails in RL for LLMs due to exploration collapse; existing solutions are heuristic and don't address the fundamental geometric mismatch causing overly conservative/aggressive updates depending on probability regions.
AI.method: Riemannian Isometric Policy Optimization (RIPO) guarantees isometric policy updates on the policy Riemannian manifold, correcting PPO-Clip's Euclidean metric inconsistency with the intrinsic geometry.
AI.result: RIPO significantly surpasses existing LLM RL algorithms across seven competition-level benchmarks, achieving up to 60% improvement over GRPO on AIME24 while providing better bias-variance trade-off and optimization stability.
AI.conclusion: The fundamental flaw of PPO-Clip is geometric - it uses Euclidean metrics incompatible with policy manifold geometry; RIPO corrects this through Riemannian isometric updates, enabling more effective RL for LLM reasoning enhancement.

title: Cross-Layer Misalignment Detection in Agent Skills: A Progressive Loading-Aware Contrastive Learning Approach
comment: 10 pages, 5 pages supplemental. Accepted at the KDD 2026 Workshop on Evaluation and Trustworthiness of Agentic AI
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly extended through Agent Skills, reusable artifacts that package natural-language metadata, procedural instructions, and execution-time resources for runtime use. As open-source skill marketplaces expand, users and agents increasingly rely on brief metadata to select third-party skills, making it difficult to detect inconsistencies between a skill's description and its true behavior, a problem we call cross-layer misalignment. To address this issue, we propose Progressive Loading-Aware Hierarchical Contrastive Learning (PL-HCL), an LLM-based framework that detects misalignment by modeling the layered structure of Agent Skills and learning cross-layer consistency. Using a normalized corpus of over 264,000 open-source skills and a human-verified challenge set, PL-HCL improves Macro-F1 from approximately 0.45 for unadapted baselines to 0.87-0.89 across evaluated LLM backbones. This approach offers an effective screening tool for users and operators, as well as design principles for detecting inconsistencies in layered digital artifacts.

AI.tldr: PL-HCL is an LLM-based framework that detects misalignment between Agent Skills' descriptions and actual behavior using hierarchical contrastive learning, improving detection accuracy from ~0.45 to ~0.89 F1 score.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly use third-party Agent Skills from open-source marketplaces, users rely on brief metadata to select skills. This creates a "cross-layer misalignment" problem where a skill's description may not match its true behavior, posing security and reliability risks.
AI.method: Progressive Loading-Aware Hierarchical Contrastive Learning (PL-HCL) - an LLM-based framework that models the layered structure of Agent Skills (metadata, instructions, execution resources) and learns cross-layer consistency through hierarchical contrastive learning.
AI.result: PL-HCL significantly improves misalignment detection performance, boosting Macro-F1 scores from approximately 0.45 for baseline methods to 0.87-0.89 across different LLM backbones, using a corpus of 264,000+ open-source skills and a human-verified challenge set.
AI.conclusion: PL-HCL provides an effective screening tool for users and operators to detect misaligned Agent Skills, while establishing design principles for detecting inconsistencies in layered digital artifacts, enhancing security and reliability in open-source skill ecosystems.

title: Knowledge-Conditioned, Single-Pass LLM Synthesis of Executable Unity Game Scenes: A Compiler Error Census across 26 Goal Playable Concepts
comment: Substantially reframed and extended version of arXiv:2603.07101, with new experiments, figures, and analysis
summary: Large language models (LLMs) write Unity C\# for game scenes. Yet nearly all demonstrations rest on an iterative repair loop that regenerates code until it compiles, conflating what the model writes with what the loop fixes. We remove the loop and evaluate a single pass, where the first draft is final. This isolates the model's parametric knowledge, the most stringent test of unaided generation. Models instantiate Goal Playable Concepts, playable counterparts of goal patterns, across 10,400 generations (four open-weight models, 7B--30B; two generation modes; four intermediate-representation (IR) conditioning levels; 26 goal patterns; 20 seeds). None compiled into a runnable scene, leaving no survivorship bias. To understand how the generated C\# scripts fail, we categorize the 99 error codes behind 90{,}673 compiler-error occurrences as Grounding (invented or misused Unity types and APIs) or Hygiene (structural defects needing no Unity knowledge). The split differs sharply by goal pattern (e.g., Stealth fails mostly on invented engine references; Capture on plain C\# structure). Larger models, stricter IRs, and different generation modes move the errors but never yield a compiling scene. The bottleneck is missing engine-specific knowledge. The census orders goal patterns by that demand, showing designers where single-pass generation breaks.

AI.tldr: LLMs can't generate compilable Unity C# code in single-pass generation without iterative repair loops, revealing fundamental lack of engine-specific knowledge.
AI.motivation: Current demonstrations of LLMs writing Unity C# code rely on iterative repair loops that conflate what the model generates with what gets fixed through multiple attempts. The authors want to isolate the model's true parametric knowledge by testing unaided, single-pass generation without repair cycles.
AI.method: Tested four open-weight LLMs (7B-30B parameters) across 10,400 generations with two generation modes and four intermediate-representation conditioning levels. Evaluated 26 goal patterns (Goal Playable Concepts) with 20 random seeds. Analyzed 90,673 compiler errors across 99 error codes, categorizing them as Grounding errors (invented/misused Unity APIs) or Hygiene errors (basic C# structural issues).
AI.result: None of the 10,400 single-pass generations compiled into runnable Unity scenes. Error patterns varied by goal pattern (e.g., Stealth games failed on invented engine references, Capture games on basic C# structure). Larger models, stricter IR conditioning, and different generation modes shifted error patterns but never produced a compiling scene. The fundamental bottleneck is missing engine-specific knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Single-pass LLM generation for Unity C# code is fundamentally limited by lack of parametric engine knowledge, not coding ability. The error census provides a practical guide showing where single-pass generation fails for different game patterns, helping designers understand the current limitations of unaided LLM code generation.

title: AI YOU Town: Make Friends and Money with Your Digital Twin
summary: Existing approaches to infer user traits and generate responses consistent with a persona rely on static prompting. They lack calibrated uncertainty, ignore sequential evidence, and drift during long interactions. We present \textbf{AI YOU}, a framework that continually updates a personality profile with 22 dimensions from conversation and embodies it in a personal digital twin. Practically, the system combines prompting, Bayesian updating, and conformal prediction for persona inference. A periodically refreshed memory anchor and cognitive memory with three layers preserve persona consistency over long interactions. Across the main results, AI YOU \emph{(i)} achieves conformal coverage ranging from 0.921 to 0.976, \emph{(ii)} improves uncertainty calibration and reasoning grounded in memory, and \emph{(iii)} enhances persona fidelity over static prompting in role playing over 100 turns while reducing trait drift, for most evaluated backbones under adversarial settings with multiple agents. The prototype \emph{AI YOU Town} initializes an imaginative twin world for future interaction. The online demo is available at \href{https://quinnnnnne-ai-you.hf.space/}{\mbox{\texttt{quinnnnnne-ai-you.hf.space}}}.

AI.tldr: AI YOU is a framework for continually updating personality profiles from conversation using Bayesian updating and conformal prediction, maintaining persona consistency over long interactions through memory layers.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to infer user traits and generate persona-consistent responses have limitations: they rely on static prompting, lack calibrated uncertainty, ignore sequential evidence, and suffer from drift during long interactions.
AI.method: Combines prompting, Bayesian updating, and conformal prediction for persona inference. Uses a periodically refreshed memory anchor and three-layer cognitive memory system to preserve persona consistency over long interactions. Infers personality profiles with 22 dimensions from conversation.
AI.result: Achieves conformal coverage from 0.921 to 0.976, improves uncertainty calibration and memory-grounded reasoning, enhances persona fidelity over static prompting in 100+ turn role-playing, and reduces trait drift under adversarial settings with multiple agents.
AI.conclusion: AI YOU successfully addresses limitations of static approaches by enabling continual persona updating with calibrated uncertainty and long-term consistency, demonstrated through a prototype AI YOU Town for digital twin interactions.

title: PhysMRV: Physical Memory Retrieval and Verification for Physics Plausibility Reasoning
summary: Video-language models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on video understanding and visual question answering, yet they remain unreliable in reasoning about physical plausibility, where understanding object interactions, causal dynamics, and fundamental physical principles is essential. This limitation is particularly evident on challenging physical reasoning benchmarks, revealing a persistent gap in physical commonsense reasoning. To address this challenge, we propose PhysMRV, a training-free physical memory and verification framework for physical plausibility reasoning. Unlike retrieval-augmented VLMs that retrieve semantically similar videos as additional context, PhysMRV transforms training videos into a Hierarchical Memory Bank of structured physical knowledge comprising three complementary levels: scene descriptions capturing visual context, physical-event graphs modeling object interactions and causal structure, and physics-rule summaries distilling reusable physical principles and cues. During inference, PhysMRV retrieves physically relevant memories and leverages their structured physical evidence to guide a frozen VLM in verifying physical plausibility, requiring neither fine-tuning nor parameter updates. We evaluate PhysMRV on three challenging physical reasoning benchmarks, ImplausiBench, IntPhys2, and GRASP Level 2, across multiple state-of-the-art VLMs. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements over direct prompting across diverse VLMs and evaluation benchmarks, showing that structured physical memories provide an effective and scalable means of enhancing physical plausibility reasoning without additional training.

AI.tldr: PhysMRV is a training-free framework that enhances video-language models' physical plausibility reasoning by creating structured physical knowledge memories from training videos and using them to guide verification during inference without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Video-language models perform well on general video understanding tasks but struggle with physical plausibility reasoning, showing limitations in understanding object interactions, causal dynamics, and physical principles. There's a persistent gap in physical commonsense reasoning despite strong performance on other benchmarks.
AI.method: PhysMRV transforms training videos into a Hierarchical Memory Bank with three complementary levels: 1) scene descriptions (visual context), 2) physical-event graphs (object interactions and causal structure), and 3) physics-rule summaries (reusable physical principles). During inference, it retrieves physically relevant memories and uses their structured evidence to guide a frozen VLM for verification without fine-tuning or parameter updates.
AI.result: PhysMRV consistently improves performance over direct prompting across multiple state-of-the-art VLMs on three challenging physical reasoning benchmarks: ImplausiBench, IntPhys2, and GRASP Level 2. The structured physical memories provide an effective and scalable enhancement to physical plausibility reasoning without additional training.
AI.conclusion: Structured physical memories offer an effective and scalable approach to enhance VLMs' physical plausibility reasoning capabilities without requiring fine-tuning, addressing a critical gap in current video-language models' understanding of physical commonsense.

title: Large language model agents accelerate inverse design of metal-organic frameworks for gas separation
summary: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offer a highly modular platform for adsorptive gas separation, yet their vast reticular design space makes inverse design difficult under simultaneous constraints of chemical validity, separation performance, and structural diversity. Here, we present LEMO Agent, a large-language-model agent framework for closed-loop inverse design of gas-separation MOFs in MOFid space. LEMO Agent couples language-based candidate generation with MOFid standardization, explicit validity checking, Transformer-based property prediction, structured design memory, and multi-island exploration. Through iterative generate--validate--evaluate--remember cycles, the agent uses feedback from both successful and failed candidates to guide chemically constrained search across linker, metal, and topology choices. We evaluate LEMO Agent on CH$_4$/N$_2$ and CO$_2$/N$_2$ separation tasks. Compared with representative generative, optimization, and agentic baselines, LEMO Agent enriches high-performing candidates, improves predicted separation performance, and maintains broad chemical and topological diversity. Selected candidates are further reconstructed, evaluated by GCMC simulations, and passed through an experimental down-selection workflow based on chemical feasibility and ligand purchasability, leading to initial wet-lab synthesis and SEM characterization. These results demonstrate that large language model agents can serve as interpretable and scalable design engines for accelerating MOF discovery beyond conventional fixed-library screening.

AI.tldr: LEMO Agent is an LLM-powered framework for inverse design of MOFs for gas separation that uses generate-validate-evaluate-remember cycles to explore MOFid chemical space while maintaining chemical validity and structural diversity.
AI.motivation: The vast reticular design space of MOFs makes inverse design difficult under simultaneous constraints of chemical validity, separation performance, and structural diversity. Conventional fixed-library screening approaches are limited, so there's a need for more scalable and interpretable design engines.
AI.method: LEMO Agent couples language-based candidate generation with MOFid standardization, explicit validity checking, Transformer-based property prediction, structured design memory, and multi-island exploration. It operates through iterative generate-validate-evaluate-remember cycles that use feedback from both successful and failed candidates to guide search across linker, metal, and topology choices.
AI.result: For CH₄/N₂ and CO₂/N₂ separation tasks, LEMO Agent enriches high-performing candidates, improves predicted separation performance, and maintains broad chemical and topological diversity compared to generative, optimization, and agentic baselines. Selected candidates were reconstructed, evaluated by GCMC simulations, and passed through experimental down-selection based on chemical feasibility and ligand purchasability.
AI.conclusion: Large language model agents can serve as interpretable and scalable design engines for accelerating MOF discovery beyond conventional fixed-library screening, as demonstrated by the successful synthesis and SEM characterization of selected candidates through an experimental workflow.

title: Generative Augmentation of Raman Spectra for Glioma Classification
summary: Access to sufficiently large biomedical datasets remains a major obstacle for machine learning in Raman spectroscopy-based diagnostics. In particular, for glioma analysis, datasets are typically small and heterogeneous, affected by acquisition-specific variability. This work investigates the utility of deep generative augmentation in such a small-cohort setting. We analyze glioma biopsy spectra acquired from 58 tumor samples and consider both binary IDH-status classification and 6-class methylation subtype classification problems. To address the limited size and imbalance of the dataset, we develop a conditional variational autoencoder ($β$-CVAE) capable of generating class-conditioned synthetic Raman spectra. The generated data are evaluated in Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real (TS/TR) and Train-on-Synthetic+Real, Test-on-Real (TSR/TR) settings under a strict patient-isolated cross-validation protocol. Models trained exclusively on synthetic data underperform models trained on real spectra, indicating a substantial domain gap between synthetic and real distributions. However, augmenting the real training data with synthetic spectra consistently improves classification performance across multiple models. These findings indicate that, even with a limited number of independent patient samples, generative models can capture sufficient structure to provide useful regularization for downstream classifiers. We also investigate a reconstruction-based inference strategy, termed Classification by Reconstruction (CbR), in which class prediction is based on reconstruction error under different class conditions. Overall, the results support the use of deep generative augmentation as a practical strategy for improving machine learning robustness in Raman spectroscopy applications characterized by limited biomedical datasets.

AI.tldr: Deep generative augmentation using a β-CVAE improves Raman spectroscopy classification for glioma analysis in small datasets when used for data augmentation, but not when used alone.
AI.motivation: Limited access to large biomedical datasets is a major obstacle for machine learning in Raman spectroscopy-based diagnostics, especially for glioma analysis where datasets are typically small and heterogeneous with acquisition-specific variability.
AI.method: Developed a conditional variational autoencoder (β-CVAE) to generate class-conditioned synthetic Raman spectra from 58 glioma biopsy samples. Evaluated in Train-on-Synthetic/Test-on-Real (TS/TR) and Train-on-Synthetic+Real/Test-on-Real (TSR/TR) settings with patient-isolated cross-validation for both binary IDH-status and 6-class methylation subtype classification.
AI.result: Models trained exclusively on synthetic data underperformed those trained on real spectra, indicating a domain gap. However, augmenting real training data with synthetic spectra consistently improved classification performance across multiple models. Also investigated Classification by Reconstruction (CbR) strategy where prediction is based on reconstruction error.
AI.conclusion: Generative models can capture sufficient structure from limited patient samples to provide useful regularization for downstream classifiers, supporting deep generative augmentation as a practical strategy for improving ML robustness in Raman spectroscopy applications with limited datasets.

title: CRiT-QA: Evaluating Multi-hop Reasoning with Counterfactual Chains and Distractor Traps
summary: Evaluating the multi-hop reasoning capabilities of large language models remains a significant challenge. Although current models achieve strong results on existing multi-hop question answering datasets, such performance often masks two critical vulnerabilities: (1) reliance on internal parametric knowledge rather than adherence to the provided context, and (2) exploitation of dataset shortcuts, such as single-document cues or type-matching, that diminish the need for genuine evidence aggregation across multiple documents. We introduce CRiT-QA (Counterfactual Reasoning with Traps), a dataset explicitly designed to address both limitations. To neutralize reliance on memorized knowledge and enforce strict context dependency, CRiT-QA transforms factual reasoning chains with counterfactual entities. Furthermore, it injects multi-anchor distractor chains, plausible but incorrect reasoning paths that diverge at different hops. These traps require models to follow the entire reasoning process rather than exploiting shallow heuristics. Our experiments show that LLMs exhibit substantial performance degradation on CRiT-QA compared to standard datasets, exposing their vulnerability to counterfactual conditions and distractor traps. CRiT-QA thus serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool for evaluating genuine multi-hop reasoning and provides a foundation for developing more reliable, evidence-grounded LLMs.

AI.tldr: CRiT-QA is a new dataset designed to rigorously test multi-hop reasoning in LLMs by using counterfactual entities and multi-anchor distractor traps to prevent exploitation of memorized knowledge and dataset shortcuts.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs show strong performance on multi-hop QA datasets, but this masks two critical vulnerabilities: 1) reliance on parametric knowledge rather than provided context, and 2) exploitation of dataset shortcuts like single-document cues that bypass genuine multi-hop reasoning.
AI.method: CRiT-QA introduces two key innovations: 1) transforms factual reasoning chains with counterfactual entities to enforce strict context dependency, and 2) injects multi-anchor distractor chains - plausible but incorrect reasoning paths that diverge at different hops, creating traps that require full reasoning process adherence.
AI.result: Experiments show LLMs exhibit substantial performance degradation on CRiT-QA compared to standard datasets, exposing vulnerability to both counterfactual conditions and distractor traps.
AI.conclusion: CRiT-QA serves as a rigorous diagnostic tool for evaluating genuine multi-hop reasoning and provides a foundation for developing more reliable, evidence-grounded LLMs that can't cheat via memorization or shallow heuristics.

title: The Differential Neural Tangent Kernel and Its Positivity
summary: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) is one powerful tool for analyzing the training dynamics of neural networks in the over-parameterized regime. Recently, the theoretical framework has been extended to physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving linear PDEs, one highly popular class of neural PDE solvers. In the analysis, the positivity of the associated NTK plays a fundamental role. However, establishing the positivity of the NTK for PINNs is highly challenging, due to the presence of multiple differential operators. In this work, we propose a new theoretical framework, called Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK), for analyzing PINNs through the lens of the NTK, and establish the positivity of the infinite width DNTK for both shallow and deep neural networks for a wide class of activation functions, including RePU and smooth but non-polynomial activations, for all linear differential operators. These theoretical results lay the foundation for the analysis of gradient type algorithms for training PINNs.

AI.tldr: Develops a new Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK) framework to prove positivity for PINNs with various activations, enabling analysis of training dynamics for neural PDE solvers.
AI.motivation: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) is valuable for analyzing neural network training dynamics, but its application to Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) is challenging due to the difficulty in establishing positivity of the NTK when dealing with differential operators.
AI.method: Proposes a new Differential Neural Tangent Kernel (DNTK) framework specifically designed for analyzing PINNs. Establishes positivity of infinite width DNTK for both shallow and deep networks with various activation functions including RePU and smooth non-polynomial activations, covering all linear differential operators.
AI.result: Successfully proves positivity of the infinite width DNTK for PINNs, overcoming previous challenges with differential operators, and providing theoretical foundation for analyzing gradient-based training algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The DNTK framework enables rigorous analysis of PINNs training dynamics for linear PDEs, establishing theoretical foundations that were previously unavailable due to the complexity of handling differential operators in NTK analysis.

title: Laguerre Geometry for Interpreting Large Language Models
summary: Existing hypotheses represent a concept in an LLM as a single point, a linear direction, or a Gaussian cluster, yet it remains unclear how and why such structures emerge. Here, we show that concept geometry can be precisely characterized via Laguerre Geometry, in which a concept is defined as a region--a Laguerre-Voronoi cell or a union of cells--allowing us to strictly define, measure, and separate concepts. Building on this formulation, we show that finer-grained concept structures, such as inclusion and hierarchy, are naturally revealed by the Laguerre weights. We then push this geometry inside the transformer. Decomposing each layer into piecewise-linear operators, we show that a token's hidden trajectory is governed by two coupled mechanisms: a static tree of self-contained piecewise-linear flow, and a dynamic transport that hops the trajectory across trees when cross-token attention fires. This decomposition yields Geometric Lens, a training-free, hyperparameter-free method for reading out the exact concept a hidden vector encodes at any layer. We also develop Laguerre Autoencoder, a 2D visualizer that renders both the decision geometry and a model's full reasoning trajectory in one view. Finally, we move beyond explanatory geometry toward actionable interpretability, showing that Geometric Lens recovers the correct factual token when a model is prompted with in-context interference. The code is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Laguerre Geometry to precisely characterize concept representations in LLMs as Voronoi cells, revealing concept structures, and develops Geometric Lens for training-free concept decoding and visualization.
AI.motivation: Existing hypotheses about concept representation in LLMs (single points, linear directions, or Gaussian clusters) are inadequate and unclear about how these structures emerge and function, necessitating a more precise geometric framework.
AI.method: Uses Laguerre Geometry to define concepts as Laguerre-Voronoi cells or unions of cells; decomposes transformer layers into piecewise-linear operators to analyze hidden trajectories via static tree flow and dynamic transport; develops Geometric Lens for concept decoding and Laguerre Autoencoder for 2D visualization.
AI.result: The framework reveals finer-grained concept structures (inclusion, hierarchy) via Laguerre weights, enables training-free concept decoding at any layer, visualizes decision geometry and reasoning trajectories, and successfully recovers factual tokens under in-context interference.
AI.conclusion: Laguerre Geometry provides a precise mathematical foundation for understanding and manipulating concept representations in LLMs, enabling both explanatory analysis and actionable interpretability for model behavior.

title: Two Confounds in Cross-Model Value Comparison: Response Determinism and the Access Harness
summary: Cross-model comparisons read divergence in value dispositions as evidence that language models hold individuated values. Under single-draw measurement this conflates two quantities: a difference in central tendency (a genuine value difference) and a difference in response determinism (how sharply a model commits to a forced choice). We introduce a separation protocol -- no-rule value dilemmas with counterbalanced, repeated forced-choice measurement and a determinism index -- and a determinism-corrected decomposition that splits an apparent cross-model distance into a direction-flip component (genuine disagreement) and a same-side-more-extreme component we label determinism. Across nine models, determinism varies substantially (0.66-0.95 among engaging models); whether it is a per-model trait or tracks provider and scale is a question our method makes measurable but our sample leaves open. Correcting for determinism shrinks apparent individuation, while a few cross-family disagreements survive a strict test. We then isolate a second confound: the access harness serving each model. Re-collecting the same models through raw provider APIs, we find the deployment client shifts a model's value profile substantially and client-specifically: one subscription CLI moves a profile by 0.31, flips four of eighteen items, and inflates the flagship's apparent softness (0.34 via CLI vs 0.66 via raw API), whereas another provider's client is clean, confounding provider family with access client. The harness is a value-shaping layer: a base model that refuses one-in-ten forced choices is made compliant by an agent system prompt, established causally in a white-box control. An audit ranking models by single-draw value distance thus ranks a determinism-inflated quantity, confounded further by the client used. We contribute the decomposition and identify the deployment harness as a distinct value confound.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to separate genuine value disagreements between language models from measurement artifacts caused by response determinism differences and access harness effects.
AI.motivation: Current cross-model comparisons conflate genuine value differences with response determinism differences, leading to potentially misleading conclusions about model individuation. Additionally, access harnesses (deployment clients) introduce confounding effects on measured value profiles.
AI.method: Developed a separation protocol using no-rule value dilemmas with counterbalanced, repeated forced-choice measurements and a determinism index. Introduced determinism-corrected decomposition to split apparent cross-model distance into direction-flip component (genuine disagreement) and same-side-more-extreme component (determinism). Tested across nine models and examined harness effects by recollecting data through raw provider APIs versus deployment clients.
AI.result: Determinism varies substantially across models (0.66-0.95). Correcting for determinism shrinks apparent individuation. Access harnesses significantly shift value profiles - one CLI moved profiles by 0.31, flipped four of eighteen items, and inflated softness measures (0.34 via CLI vs 0.66 via raw API). White-box controls established causal relationship where base model refusal rates were eliminated by agent system prompts.
AI.conclusion: Current audit rankings of model value differences measure determinism-inflated quantities confounded by access harness effects. The deployment harness constitutes a distinct value confound that should be accounted for in cross-model comparisons. The decomposition method enables proper separation of genuine value disagreements from measurement artifacts.

title: Constraint-Aware Hierarchical Search for Regulation-Driven Fine-Grained Classification
summary: Tasks such as customs tariff classification, export control categorization, and standards-based equipment coding require assigning an input instance to a fine-grained class under an explicit regulatory hierarchy. Unlike standard text classification, the correct label in these tasks is not determined by semantic similarity alone, but by rule-defined boundaries, threshold conditions, exclusion clauses, definitions, and local exceptions. As a result, two highly similar inputs may require different labels, while a retrieved passage that appears relevant may still be inapplicable under the governing rules. Existing flat classifiers, hierarchical text classification methods, and retrieval-augmented LLM systems are not designed to jointly enforce hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and fine-grained boundary reasoning. In this paper, we formulate this setting as regulation-driven fine-grained hierarchical classification, where an external instance must be assigned to a fine-grained class through a valid path in a regulatory hierarchy and supported by auditable evidence. We construct four benchmark datasets from representative regulation-intensive scenarios and validate the annotations through an expert-in-the-loop process. We further propose a constraint-aware hierarchical search framework that converts regulatory documents into a searchable tree, retrieves only valid local candidate nodes, and uses structured regulatory fields with evidence snippets to guide each next-hop decision. Experiments show that our method achieves the best mean accuracy on all four datasets and provides interpretable decision paths, with the largest gains on cases involving fine-grained neighboring categories and rule-based boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: A framework for regulation-driven hierarchical classification that converts regulatory documents into searchable trees, uses constraint-aware search to retrieve only valid candidate nodes, and leverages structured regulatory fields with evidence snippets to make rule-consistent decisions at each hierarchy level.
AI.motivation: Many real-world regulatory tasks (customs tariff classification, export control, standards-based coding) require assigning inputs to fine-grained classes under explicit regulatory hierarchies. Unlike standard text classification, correct labels depend on rule-defined boundaries, threshold conditions, exclusion clauses, definitions, and local exceptions. Existing methods fail to jointly enforce hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and fine-grained boundary reasoning.
AI.method: Proposed constraint-aware hierarchical search framework that: 1) converts regulatory documents into a searchable tree, 2) retrieves only valid local candidate nodes at each hierarchy level, 3) uses structured regulatory fields with evidence snippets to guide next-hop decisions, and 4) ensures hierarchical validity and rule consistency throughout the classification process.
AI.result: The method achieves best mean accuracy on all four constructed benchmark datasets from regulation-intensive scenarios, with largest performance gains on cases involving fine-grained neighboring categories and rule-based boundary conditions. Provides interpretable decision paths with auditable evidence.
AI.conclusion: Regulation-driven fine-grained hierarchical classification requires specialized approaches that go beyond semantic similarity and flat classification. The proposed constraint-aware hierarchical search framework effectively addresses the unique challenges of regulatory classification by jointly enforcing hierarchical validity, rule consistency, and boundary reasoning while providing interpretable, evidence-supported decisions.

title: When Does Depth Survive Composition? Compute--Quality Regimes in Latent World Models
summary: Adaptive-compute world models -- early-exit or mixture-of-depths predictors that spend variable depth per step -- assume depth buys better predictions and can be routed adaptively. In autoregressive rollouts, the first assumption requires depth's per-step precision to survive composition. We test this with a pre-registered instrument, the shallow penalty $ρ=\mathrm{err}(\text{shallowest-exit rollout})/\mathrm{err}(\text{full-depth rollout})$, across nine DeepMind Control tasks under matched single-step ($K=1$) and multi-step ($K=4$) training, three seeds each. We find three regimes: on 6/9 tasks depth helps rollouts (intrinsic, $ρ$ up to $4.7\times$), on 2/9 the shallow exits beat the full stack (inversion, $ρ$ down to $0.85\times$), and one is flat. The robust inversion (cheetah) is not a property of the dynamics but is created by training: an ablation supervising early exits only at the first rollout step erases it ($ρ: 0.87\to1.18$, $n=8$, $Δ=+0.31$), while an intrinsic-tradeoff task is unaffected -- a double dissociation we call the routability catch-22, since the supervision that makes exits routable is what trains them to out-roll the full stack. The regime is partly predictable a priori: observation/action dimensionality and one-step model error correlate with $ρ$ at $|\text{Spearman}|\approx0.75$ ($n=9$). Inside a CEM planner, $ρ$'s sign predicts whether planning benefits from depth, most sharply on the inversion task, where shallow planning beats deep. Finally, three cautions: a task's regime depends on the metric space, the rollout horizon, and the encoder. All thresholds and gates were fixed before the compute campaign, including a pre-registered negative for the hypothesis that motivated the study.

AI.tldr: Depth in world models doesn't always improve multi-step rollouts - sometimes shallower exits outperform deeper ones, revealing a training-induced "routability catch-22" where supervision to make exits usable paradoxically trains them to beat the full stack.
AI.motivation: Adaptive-compute world models assume that deeper models provide better predictions and that depth benefits can be adaptively routed, but this assumes depth's per-step precision persists through composition in autoregressive rollouts. The paper tests whether depth actually helps in multi-step rollouts and investigates when/why it might fail.
AI.method: Introduced shallow penalty metric (ρ) comparing rollout error of shallowest-exit vs full-depth rollouts across nine DeepMind Control tasks with matched single-step (K=1) and multi-step (K=4) training. Used ablation studies (supervising early exits only at first rollout step) to investigate training effects. Analyzed correlation with observation/action dimensionality and one-step error. Tested effects in CEM planning. Pre-registered experimental protocol.
AI.result: Three regimes identified: intrinsic (6/9 tasks where depth helps, ρ up to 4.7×), inversion (2/9 tasks where shallow exits beat full stack, ρ down to 0.85×), and flat (1 task). Cheetah inversion was training-induced - ablation erased it. Task characteristics (dimensionality, one-step error) correlate with ρ (|Spearman|≈0.75). In CEM planner, ρ's sign predicts planning benefit from depth. Regime depends on metric space, rollout horizon, and encoder.
AI.conclusion: Depth's benefit in world model rollouts is not guaranteed - sometimes training creates shallower exits that outperform deeper ones (routability catch-22). The effect is predictable from task characteristics, and regime depends on multiple factors. The study's pre-registered negative hypothesis was confirmed, challenging assumptions about adaptive depth routing in world models.

title: MRUF: Multi-granularity Routing with Uncertainty-Aware Fusion for Robust Multimodal Sentiment Analysis
comment: Main paper (6 pages). Accepted for publication by IEEE International Conference on Systems and Man and and Cybernetics 2026 (IEEE SMC 2026)
summary: Multimodal sentiment analysis relies on language, visual, and acoustic cues, but utterance-level modality quality may vary due to occlusion, background noise, motion blur, or imperfect transcripts, causing conventional fusion to over-trust unreliable modalities. We propose MRUF, a reliability-aware fusion method that combines multi-granularity routing with uncertainty-aware calibration. MRUF summarizes sentiment-relevant representations, performs subspace- and modality-level routing, and supervises modality routing with leave-one-out error increases to estimate utterance-level modality importance. It further predicts modality-wise uncertainty and refines modality gates through inverse-variance reweighting, while modality-invariant contrastive alignment stabilizes the shared representation space. Experiments on CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI under aligned and unaligned settings show consistent improvements over strong baselines, and mechanism analysis verifies that modalities with higher predicted uncertainty receive lower fusion weights.

AI.tldr: MRUF is a reliability-aware multimodal fusion method for sentiment analysis that uses multi-granularity routing and uncertainty calibration to handle varying modality quality in utterances.
AI.motivation: Conventional multimodal fusion methods over-trust unreliable modalities when utterance-level modality quality varies due to issues like occlusion, background noise, motion blur, or imperfect transcripts.
AI.method: Proposes MRUF with two key components: (1) multi-granularity routing that performs subspace- and modality-level routing, supervised by leave-one-out error increases to estimate modality importance; (2) uncertainty-aware calibration that predicts modality-wise uncertainty and refines modality gates through inverse-variance reweighting, with modality-invariant contrastive alignment to stabilize shared representations.
AI.result: Experiments on CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI datasets under both aligned and unaligned settings show consistent improvements over strong baselines. Mechanism analysis confirms that modalities with higher predicted uncertainty receive lower fusion weights.
