Experiments on CRAG, ConflictQA, and MuSiQue show that ERAG remains competitive with the strongest matched baseline on standard question answering while improving behavior under conflict. On the CRAG ambiguous subset, hallucination decreases from 45.3% for Corrective RAG to a human-calibrated estimate of 34.8%, conflict resolution increases from 35.2% to 51.2%, and expected calibration error improves to 0.122. These results suggest that evidential modeling is a practical mechanism for trustworthy information processing in foundation-model-based retrieval systems.

AI.tldr: ERAG is an uncertainty-aware RAG framework that models retrieved chunks as probabilistic evidence, uses Dempster-Shafer fusion to preserve conflicts as epistemic uncertainty, and routes responses based on fused uncertainty scores to improve handling of conflicting information.
AI.motivation: Most RAG pipelines treat retrieved passages as deterministic and mutually consistent, but in real open information environments, retrieved sources often disagree due to temporal drift, source error, ambiguity, or genuine uncertainty. Current approaches either ignore or normalize away these conflicts.
AI.method: 1. Lightweight evaluator extracts candidate claims and maps chunk-level support to Dirichlet evidence. 2. Conflict-preserving Dempster-Shafer fusion rule transfers unresolved disagreement into epistemic uncertainty instead of normalizing it away. 3. Generator is routed to direct answering, conflict-aware answering, or abstention based on fused uncertainty score.
AI.result: Experiments on CRAG, ConflictQA, and MuSiQue show ERAG remains competitive with strongest baselines on standard QA while improving conflict handling. On CRAG ambiguous subset: hallucination decreases from 45.3% to 34.8%, conflict resolution increases from 35.2% to 51.2%, expected calibration error improves to 0.122.
AI.conclusion: Evidential modeling provides a practical mechanism for trustworthy information processing in foundation-model-based retrieval systems by properly handling uncertainty and conflicts rather than ignoring them.

title: SETA: Scaling Environments for Terminal Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly shifting toward agents that solve tasks through diverse interfaces, including web and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). Among these, the terminal command line provides a text-based, general-purpose interface, covering tasks from system operations to data science and machine learning. However, scaling terminal-agent training remains challenging, as it requires diverse and coherent task instructions, executable environments, and reliable verification, while lacking naturally grounded supervision data. In this work, we propose SETA, a scalable framework for generating verifiable terminal environments for reinforcement learning (RL). The framework consists of two pipelines sharing a unified verification mechanism: SETA-Synth converts diverse sources into standardized RL environments, and SETA-Evol further expands from existing environments with adaptive control of difficulty and diversity. Together, we construct and release SETA-Env, the largest open-source verifiable terminal RL dataset to date, containing over 4,500 environments. We evaluate our dataset by training Qwen3-8B with GRPO on SETA-Env, achieving 12% pass rate on Terminal-Bench 2.0, the best reported result for an RL-trained model at the 8B scale. We further observe gains on DeepSeek-V4-Flash under the same terminal agent harness, with pass@1 on Terminal-Bench 2.0 improving from 40% to 43% and pass@5 improving from 54% to 58%. These results demonstrate that SETA- Env provides high-quality training environments for terminal agents and serves as a valuable resource for advancing research on terminal-based agent learning.

AI.tldr: SETA is a framework that generates verifiable terminal environments for RL training of terminal agents, creating the largest open-source dataset (4,500+ environments) that improves terminal agent performance on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Training terminal agents is challenging due to the need for diverse task instructions, executable environments, reliable verification, and lack of naturally grounded supervision data.
AI.method: Two pipelines with unified verification: SETA-Synth converts diverse sources into standardized RL environments, and SETA-Evol expands from existing environments with adaptive difficulty and diversity control.
AI.result: Created SETA-Env dataset with 4,500+ environments. Trained Qwen3-8B with GRPO achieving 12% pass rate on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (best RL-trained model at 8B scale). Improved DeepSeek-V4-Flash from 40% to 43% (pass@1) and 54% to 58% (pass@5).
AI.conclusion: SETA-Env provides high-quality training environments for terminal agents and serves as a valuable resource for advancing terminal-based agent learning research.

title: Learning from Noise: Effective-Rank Collapse and Out-of-Distribution Rejection in Restricted Boltzmann Machines
summary: Restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs) represent data by shaping an energy landscape over visible and hidden configurations, but their discriminative use is fragile under out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs: samples outside the training distribution can be absorbed into one of the learned class basins rather than rejected. Here, we analyze this failure mode through the spectrum of the induced visible--visible interaction $J=WW^{T}$, where \(W\) is the visible--hidden weight matrix. Relative to a Marchenko--Pastur random-matrix reference, conventional training spreads spectral weight into many weak, bulk-compatible directions, increasing the effective rank of $J$. When auxiliary random binary images are assigned to a rejection label during training, the learned interaction undergoes effective-rank collapse: weak bulk-like modes are depleted, spectral weight concentrates into fewer dominant eigendirections, and the effective rank of $J$ approaches that of the empirical data covariance matrix. The resulting RBM rejects structured OOD image datasets while preserving MNIST classification accuracy, showing that random auxiliary exposure can reshape both the interaction spectrum and the free-energy landscape of an energy-based classifier.

AI.tldr: Training RBMs with random auxiliary images assigned to a rejection class causes effective-rank collapse of the interaction matrix, enabling out-of-distribution rejection while maintaining classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Restricted Boltzmann Machines have fragile discriminative performance under out-of-distribution inputs, as OOD samples can be incorrectly classified rather than rejected. The authors aim to understand this failure mode through spectral analysis and develop a method to improve OOD rejection capabilities.
AI.method: Analyze RBMs through the spectrum of visible-visible interaction matrix J=WW^T. Train RBMs with auxiliary random binary images assigned to a rejection label. Compare spectral properties to Marchenko-Pastur random-matrix reference, examining effective rank changes.
AI.result: Conventional training spreads spectral weight into many weak directions, increasing effective rank. With random auxiliary exposure during training, the interaction undergoes effective-rank collapse: spectral weight concentrates into fewer dominant eigendirections, and effective rank approaches that of empirical data covariance. This enables structured OOD image dataset rejection while preserving MNIST classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Random auxiliary exposure during training can reshape both the interaction spectrum and free-energy landscape of energy-based classifiers, transforming RBMs from fragile discriminative models into robust classifiers with effective out-of-distribution rejection capabilities.

title: Incremental Transformer for Surrogate-Based Inverse Design of Geopolymer Mixtures
summary: Small-data inverse design is challenging in engineering informatics when observations are heterogeneous, mixed-type, and constrained by physical relations among design variables. This work proposes a topology-aware surrogate framework guided by an Incremental Transformer (INCRT) for physics-constrained inverse design, applied to geopolymer mixture design. The method integrates intrinsic-dimensionality analysis, mixed-variable design-space representation, tabular surrogate prediction, INCRT-based manifold rationalisation, and constrained inverse optimisation. Using a public benchmark of fly-ash and slag-based geopolymer concrete mixtures with compressive-strength and carbon-emission targets, the high-dimensional design space proves strongly redundant, organising around fewer effective mixture regimes. Compressive strength requires nonlinear tabular surrogates, while carbon emission is largely determined by composition and well recovered by regularised linear models. INCRT thus acts not as a replacement for tabular predictors but as a rationalisation layer providing prototype regimes and a manifold-support score for inverse design. Three strategies are compared: unconstrained surrogate optimisation, physics-constrained optimisation, and topology-aware physics-constrained optimisation. Unconstrained optimisation can match target strength but may yield physically invalid or off-manifold candidates; physics-only constraints do not always ensure data support. The topology-aware strategy yields candidates balancing target compliance, carbon reduction, physical admissibility, and proximity to the learned feasible manifold. The framework aims not to replace experimental validation but to support screening of credible candidate mixtures from small, mixed, physically constrained engineering datasets.

AI.tldr: A topology-aware surrogate framework using Incremental Transformer (INCRT) for physics-constrained inverse design of geopolymer mixtures, addressing challenges of small, heterogeneous, mixed-type datasets with physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Small-data inverse design is challenging when observations are heterogeneous, mixed-type, and constrained by physical relations among design variables, particularly in engineering informatics applications like geopolymer mixture design.
AI.method: Integrates intrinsic-dimensionality analysis, mixed-variable design-space representation, tabular surrogate prediction, INCRT-based manifold rationalisation, and constrained inverse optimization. Three strategies compared: unconstrained surrogate optimization, physics-constrained optimization, and topology-aware physics-constrained optimization.
AI.result: High-dimensional design space is strongly redundant, organizing around fewer effective mixture regimes. Compressive strength requires nonlinear tabular surrogates, while carbon emission is well recovered by regularized linear models. INCRT serves as rationalization layer providing prototype regimes and manifold-support score. Topology-aware strategy yields candidates balancing target compliance, carbon reduction, physical admissibility, and proximity to learned feasible manifold.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports screening credible candidate mixtures from small, mixed, physically constrained engineering datasets, aiming not to replace experimental validation but to guide efficient design exploration while respecting physical constraints and data topology.

title: Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering
summary: Activation steering offers a lightweight alternative to fine-tuning for controlling large language models at inference time. While many existing methods implicitly optimize a log-density-ratio objective between desired and undesired activation distributions, they do so heuristically rather than deriving it from a principled optimization problem. Moreover, these methods produce query-independent steering directions that can degrade performance on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs. We introduce \textsc{Cobras} (Conditional Optimal Bridge for Riemannian Activation Steering), which addresses both limitations by casting activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge on the residual-stream hypersphere. This formulation yields, to our knowledge, the first principled derivation of the log-density-ratio steering objective from a well-posed optimization problem. Solving the bridge via entropic optimal transport and extracting the probability flow ODE recovers the widely used density-ratio gradient as a special case when the Sinkhorn potentials are uniform. Crucially, the Schrödinger potentials are evaluated at the current activation, making the resulting steering direction inherently query-adaptive. Empirically, across four models and three alignment axes (helpfulness, truthfulness, and detoxification), \textsc{Cobras} consistently outperforms prior activation steering baselines while avoiding the OOD degradation commonly observed in existing methods. The code can be found at https://github.com/arshandalili/cobras.

AI.tldr: COBRAS introduces a novel activation steering method that formulates the problem as a Schrödinger Bridge on the residual-stream hypersphere, providing the first principled derivation of log-density-ratio steering and achieving query-adaptive steering that avoids OOD degradation.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods heuristically optimize log-density-ratio objectives without principled derivation, and they use query-independent steering directions that degrade performance on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution inputs.
AI.method: COBRAS casts activation steering as a Schrödinger Bridge problem on the residual-stream hypersphere, solves it via entropic optimal transport, extracts the probability flow ODE, and recovers the density-ratio gradient as a special case when Sinkhorn potentials are uniform.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across four models and three alignment axes (helpfulness, truthfulness, detoxification) shows COBRAS consistently outperforms prior activation steering baselines while avoiding OOD degradation seen in existing methods.
AI.conclusion: COBRAS provides a principled, query-adaptive activation steering method that addresses limitations of existing approaches and demonstrates superior performance across multiple models and alignment tasks.

title: Learning Linear Temporal Specifications from Demonstrations with Uncertainty
comment: This paper has been accepted by the ACC2026 (American Control Conference)
summary: Learning temporal logic specifications from system demonstrations is essential for tasks such as formal verification and controller synthesis, especially in safety-critical domains. Existing approaches typically assume demonstrations are correct or only affected by misclassification errors. In practice, however, system traces are often uncertain or incomplete due to sensor faults, measurement errors, or data loss. We present a framework for learning minimal Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formulas from demonstrations with uncertainty. Our approach models uncertainty via Hamming distance to generate possible estimates around each observed trace, which are grouped with constraints requiring that at least one trace per group is consistent with the learned formula. Our problem is then reduced to an equivalent Pseudo-Boolean Optimization. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art LTL learning approaches and show that it recovers specifications that more closely align with ground-truth formulas under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: A framework for learning Linear Temporal Logic formulas from uncertain/incomplete system demonstrations using Hamming distance modeling and Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing LTL learning approaches assume demonstrations are correct or only have misclassification errors, but in practice, system traces are often uncertain/incomplete due to sensor faults, measurement errors, or data loss, which is especially problematic in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: Models uncertainty via Hamming distance to generate possible estimates around each observed trace, groups them with constraints requiring at least one trace per group is consistent with learned formula, then reduces the problem to equivalent Pseudo-Boolean Optimization.
AI.result: The method recovers specifications that more closely align with ground-truth formulas under uncertainty compared to state-of-the-art LTL learning approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the practical challenge of learning LTL specifications from uncertain system demonstrations, making formal verification and controller synthesis more robust in safety-critical applications with imperfect data.

title: LLM-PDESR: Robust PDE Discovery via Subdomain Weighted Residuals and LLM-Guided Symbolic Hypothesis Generation
summary: Discovering governing partial differential equations (PDEs) from noisy observational data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning. Traditional symbolic regression (SR) methods often struggle to identify accurate equations within vast combinatorial search spaces, largely due to their inability to incorporate essential domain-specific prior knowledge. Furthermore, reliance on pointwise evaluations and discrete finite differences inherently amplifies high-frequency noise, creating deceptive fitness landscapes that derail the optimization process. To resolve these bottlenecks, we propose LLM-PDESR, a framework that integrates the structural hypothesis generation of Large Language Models (LLMs) with a mathematically rigorous evaluation environment. By employing C^4-continuous quintic splines for robust differentiation and subdomain weighted residuals as natural low-pass filters, our approach effectively mitigates the fitness landscape distortion that plagues existing methods. A Pareto-driven feedback loop then enables the LLM to iteratively refine candidate equations, balancing predictive accuracy with structural parsimony. We evaluate LLM-PDESR on 23 canonical PDEs and five structurally novel equations (including a multivariate system) specifically designed to preclude dataset memorization and test true discovery capabilities. Demonstrating real-world applicability, the framework successfully extracts a consistent structural skeleton for an interpretable 1D dynamical surrogate (1D-CACE) directly from noisy ERA5 reanalysis data. Extensive experiments and out-of-distribution testing confirm that LLM-PDESR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methodologies in structural recovery, noise resilience, and the avoidance of spurious complexity and equation bloat.

AI.tldr: LLM-PDESR integrates LLMs with robust PDE discovery methods using spline-based differentiation and subdomain residuals to overcome noise and search space challenges.
AI.motivation: Traditional symbolic regression methods struggle with noisy data and vast search spaces in PDE discovery, failing to incorporate domain knowledge and suffering from noise amplification via finite differences.
AI.method: Uses LLMs for structural hypothesis generation combined with C⁴-continuous quintic splines for differentiation and subdomain weighted residuals as noise filters, with Pareto-driven feedback for iterative refinement.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on 23 canonical PDEs and 5 novel equations, successfully extracts interpretable surrogate from noisy reanalysis data, and demonstrates superior structural recovery and noise resilience.
AI.conclusion: LLM-PDESR effectively integrates LLMs with mathematical rigor to overcome traditional PDE discovery limitations, enabling robust equation discovery from noisy observational data.

title: SVR-R1: Bootstrapping Multi-modal Reasoning with Self-verification in Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce Self-Verified Reasoner (SVR-R1), a multi-turn RL framework that turns a model's own verification into a learning signal for multimodal reasoning. For each query, the model proposes an answer using the same weights, and issues a binary self-verdict (Yes/No). A 'No' triggers a second-chance rethink; a 'Yes,' or a turn cap, finalizes the output for computing the outcome-based reward. SVR-R1 is implemented with GRPO and an asynchronous multi-turn rollout framework and needs no external supervision or auxiliary critics. We evaluate SVR-R1 on vision-language reasoning benchmarks and show that it improves accuracy by a large margin over strong standard GRPO baselines. Training dynamics show decreasing reliance on verification-fewer verification turns, yet higher test accuracy-indicating that the gap between verification and generation narrows as the policy internalizes self-correction and chooses the most confident answer via our framework. SVR-R1 bridges the less explored intersection of inference-time self-refinement and RL training for VLMs, offering a simple yet effective recipe for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning. We will open-source \textbf{SVR-R1} to facilitate future research in VLMs.

AI.tldr: SVR-R1 is a multi-turn RL framework that uses a model's own binary self-verification (Yes/No) as learning signal for multimodal reasoning, reducing reliance on verification while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between inference-time self-refinement and RL training for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), enabling models to learn from their own verification without external supervision.
AI.method: Multi-turn RL framework where model proposes answer, issues binary self-verdict (Yes/No). 'No' triggers rethink; 'Yes' or turn cap finalizes output for outcome-based reward. Implemented with GRPO and asynchronous multi-turn rollout, no external supervision or auxiliary critics needed.
AI.result: Significant accuracy improvements over strong GRPO baselines on vision-language reasoning benchmarks. Training dynamics show decreasing verification reliance (fewer turns) yet higher test accuracy, indicating policy internalizes self-correction and selects most confident answers.
AI.conclusion: SVR-R1 offers simple yet effective approach for bootstrapping multimodal reasoning by turning self-verification into learning signal, bridging inference-time self-refinement with RL training for VLMs. Will be open-sourced to facilitate future research.

title: Learning from Local Walks on Dynamic Graphs with Bandit Feedback
summary: We study stochastic multi-armed bandits on dynamic graphs, where arms correspond to the vertices of a network with time-varying edges. In this setting, the learner is restricted to local movement, selecting only its current node or an immediate neighbor at each round. This constraint decouples best-arm identification from exploitation: even after the optimal arm is identified, the learner may remain unable to reach it through the evolving topology. We identify a process-agnostic structural condition, based on sliding-window mixing, that ensures the graph's intrinsic walk remains stable for both exploration and navigation. Under this regime, we analyze a family of local explore-then-commit algorithms and establish sublinear expected regret. Our framework includes a reward-aware strategy, for which we prove a worst-case safety theorem and a separate performance gain theorem.

AI.tldr: The paper studies multi-armed bandits on dynamic graphs where arms are vertices and the learner can only move locally (current node or neighbors), separating identification from exploitation even when optimal arm is found.
AI.motivation: Existing bandit algorithms assume immediate access to any arm, but real-world systems (like networks with mobility constraints) restrict movement to local neighbors only, creating a gap between identifying the best arm and actually reaching it due to time-varying connectivity.
AI.method: Proposes a framework with process-agnostic structural condition based on sliding-window mixing that ensures graph's intrinsic walk remains stable. Analyzes local explore-then-commit algorithms and includes a reward-aware strategy with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Establishes sublinear expected regret under the proposed stability regime. Proves both a worst-case safety theorem and a separate performance gain theorem for the reward-aware strategy, providing formal guarantees for dynamic graph bandits.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical foundation for multi-armed bandits on dynamic graphs with local movement constraints, showing that stable graph mixing enables effective learning despite the decoupling of identification and exploitation.

title: From Checker to Forecaster: Code-Owned Evaluation of Model-Generated Strategic Routes Under Delayed Ground Truth
summary: Many evaluations of model outputs rely either on contracts checkable at evaluation time or on feedback that arrives within the operating loop. We study the complementary setting in which ground truth is delayed, censored, or private, so deterministic code cannot check correctness at scoring time and must instead issue a code-owned provisional forecast. RouteCast instantiates this regime for model-generated typed strategic routes: models propose candidate routes and structured factors; point-in-time evidence, reference classes, and deterministic transformations produce a provisional forecast-ranking; later outcomes evaluate the forecast. In a retrospective venture pilot on 21 binary-outcome cases (6 positive, 15 negative), the whole-packet RouteCast score showed preliminary retrospective discrimination (AUC 0.756, 95% CI [0.471,0.980]), while a blind LLM judge reached AUC 0.678 [0.419,0.897] and an identity-exposed LLM judge reached AUC 0.761 [0.515,0.944], consistent with recognition- or outcome-related leakage risk. A preregistered decomposition ablation on the same binary subset found that converting the identical inputs into typed staged routes was indistinguishable from the whole-packet score (Delta AUC = -0.144, 95% CI [-0.471,0.176]) and from a deterministic heuristic (Delta AUC = -0.089, 95% CI [-0.412,0.278]). The pilot establishes an auditable feasibility result and exposes failure modes; it does not establish prospective calibration, causal decision improvement, route-decomposition advantage, or cross-domain validity.

AI.tldr: RouteCast is a system for evaluating model outputs when ground truth is delayed, censored, or private, using provisional forecasts that are later validated against actual outcomes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address evaluation challenges where ground truth is unavailable at scoring time (due to delay, censorship, or privacy constraints), making standard deterministic checks impossible.
AI.method: RouteCast uses models to propose candidate routes and structured factors, then combines point-in-time evidence, reference classes, and deterministic transformations to produce provisional forecast rankings that are later evaluated against actual outcomes.
AI.result: In a retrospective pilot on 21 binary cases, RouteCast achieved AUC 0.756, comparable to an identity-exposed LLM judge (AUC 0.761). Route decomposition showed no significant advantage over simpler approaches.
AI.conclusion: RouteCast demonstrates feasibility for auditable evaluation in delayed-truth scenarios but has limitations: no proven prospective calibration, causal decision improvement, route-decomposition advantage, or cross-domain validity established.

title: MemDecay: Region-Aware KV Cache Eviction for Efficient LLM Agent Inference
summary:
Large language model (LLM) agents accumulate heterogeneous context, including system instructions, plans, user turns, retrieved documents, tool outputs, and intermediate reasoning, whose key-value (KV) cache can become a major memory bottleneck. Existing eviction policies generally apply the same attention- or recency-based rule to every token, ignoring semantic structure already available to the agent orchestrator.
  We introduce MemDecay, a training-free, region-aware KV-cache eviction policy. MemDecay assigns tokens region-specific base priorities and decay rates, refreshes retention scores when tokens receive attention, and evicts the lowest-scoring pages under a fixed cache budget while allowing critical regions to be pinned. We also provide a procedure for calibrating decay rates from measured attention lifetimes.
  We evaluate MemDecay at approximately 450 and 1,700 token contexts using Qwen2.5-1.5B and 3B. Across all settings, attention lifetimes differ by an order of magnitude across regions: system-token half-lives range from 148 to 189 decoding steps, compared with 14 to 16 for scratchpad tokens. Pinning preserves system-region facts at full-cache accuracy in every setting, while no baseline preserves more than 13 of 24. Region-aware retention remains effective as context grows, whereas recency-based retention collapses. Accumulated-attention retention performs better on unpinned content, however, and ablations identify attention-score normalization as the main limitation of the current formulation. These results establish semantic prompt structure as a robust signal for KV-cache management while clarifying how it should be combined with attention-based importance.

AI.tldr: MemDecay: A training-free, region-aware KV-cache eviction policy that uses semantic prompt structure to prioritize different content types with region-specific decay rates.
AI.motivation: LLM agents accumulate heterogeneous context (instructions, plans, user turns, documents, tool outputs, reasoning) whose KV cache becomes a major memory bottleneck. Existing eviction policies apply uniform rules to all tokens, ignoring available semantic structure.
AI.method: MemDecay assigns region-specific base priorities and decay rates to tokens based on their semantic category (system tokens, scratchpad tokens, etc.), refreshes retention scores when tokens receive attention, and evicts lowest-scoring pages under a fixed cache budget while allowing critical regions to be pinned. Includes calibration procedure for decay rates from measured attention lifetimes.
AI.result: Attention lifetimes differ by an order of magnitude across regions (system tokens: 148-189 half-lives vs scratchpad tokens: 14-16). Pinning preserves system-region facts at full-cache accuracy in every setting, while no baseline preserves more than 13 of 24. Region-aware retention remains effective as context grows, whereas recency-based retention collapses. Attention-based importance works better for unpinned content but has normalization limitations.
AI.conclusion: Semantic prompt structure is a robust signal for KV-cache management, but should be combined with attention-based importance. The work establishes region-aware approaches as effective while identifying attention-score normalization as a key limitation to address.

title: QwenPaw-Data: Bridging Facts, Methodology, and Execution for Autonomous Enterprise Data Analytics
summary: Enterprise data analysis is emerging as a distinct frontier for autonomous agents. Compared with general-purpose interaction and software engineering, it operates in an open, ambiguous, and continuously evolving environment. These characteristics call for a data-agent architecture that treats semantics, methodology, execution, and evolution as first-class system concerns. To this end, we introduce QwenPaw-Data, an agentic data system designed for enterprise intelligent data analysis. QwenPaw-Data consolidates heterogeneous assets from warehouses, dashboards, documents, interaction logs, and historical tasks into reusable, governable, and evolvable analysis assets, then turns natural-language requests into end-to-end analytical workflows spanning data understanding, retrieval, analysis, report generation, and decision support. Its architecture decomposes the problem into three collaborative subsystems: DataBridge provides trustworthy semantic grounding through interconnected metadata, knowledge, and trace graphs; Skill-Hub codifies expert analytical methodology into reusable and verifiable skills; and Host materializes these evidence and method assets into controllable, artifact-centric runtime execution. Across these subsystems, semantics, methods, traces, and feedback are continuously deposited back into the system, forming a self-evolving asset flywheel. Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world industrial BI workloads show that QwenPaw-Data improves both verifiable data access capability and higher-level analytical quality, offering a practical foundation for reliable, traceable, and continuously improving enterprise data agents.

AI.tldr: QwenPaw-Data is an agentic data system for enterprise intelligent data analysis that consolidates heterogeneous assets into reusable analytical workflows through three collaborative subsystems with a self-evolving feedback loop.
AI.motivation: Enterprise data analysis operates in open, ambiguous, and continuously evolving environments that differ from general-purpose interaction and software engineering. This requires a new architecture treating semantics, methodology, execution, and evolution as first-class concerns for reliable enterprise data agents.
AI.method: Introduces a three-subsystem architecture: 1) DataBridge for semantic grounding through interconnected metadata/knowledge/trace graphs, 2) Skill-Hub for codifying expert analytical methodology into reusable skills, and 3) Host for controllable, artifact-centric runtime execution. The system has a self-evolving asset flywheel where semantics, methods, traces, and feedback are continuously deposited back.
AI.result: Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world industrial BI workloads show improved verifiable data access capability and higher-level analytical quality. The system provides practical foundation for reliable, traceable, and continuously improving enterprise data agents.
AI.conclusion: QwenPaw-Data offers a comprehensive solution for enterprise data analysis by treating semantics, methodology, execution, and evolution as first-class concerns, creating a practical foundation for autonomous data agents that can operate reliably in complex enterprise environments.

title: Sharp Concentration Bounds for Bundle-Valued Statistics on Manifolds
summary: Many geometric statistics and manifold learning pipelines routinely produce observations -- such as tangent vectors or local frames -- whose natural home is a varying family of fibers attached to different points of a base manifold, rather than a single shared vector space. Forming empirical averages requires transporting these observations to a common reference fiber, thereby introducing curvature- and holonomy-driven effects that are absent from classical concentration theory. We develop a non-asymptotic concentration theory for such transported empirical means, deriving finite-sample, dimension-free Hoeffding- and Bernstein-type bounds via sharp Hilbert-space inequalities. When shortest paths to the reference point are non-unique, transport becomes path-dependent and introduces a deterministic holonomy bias; we isolate and quantify this bias through bundle curvature and loop geometry, with sharp closed-form formulas for the tangent bundle of a round sphere. The resulting bias-variance decomposition separates the stochastic fluctuation decaying at the classical $n^{-1/2}$ rate in sample size $n$, from a curvature-driven error floor that no amount of additional data can eliminate; minimax lower bounds confirm both terms are unavoidable. We further establish a robust median-of-means estimator achieving optimal rates under heavy tails and the central limit theorem in the reference fiber. Controlled experiments on the sphere validate all theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: A concentration theory for empirical means of geometric data transported along a manifold, accounting for curvature/holonomy effects and deriving bias-variance decomposition with dimension-free bounds.
AI.motivation: Geometric statistics and manifold learning produce observations (tangent vectors, local frames) that live in varying fibers attached to different points of a base manifold, not in a single vector space. Transporting these to a common reference fiber introduces curvature- and holonomy-driven effects missing from classical concentration theory.
AI.method: Develop non-asymptotic concentration theory for transported empirical means using sharp Hilbert-space inequalities to derive finite-sample, dimension-free Hoeffding- and Bernstein-type bounds. When shortest paths are non-unique, transport becomes path-dependent and introduces holonomy bias; quantify this bias through bundle curvature and loop geometry. Also establish robust median-of-means estimator for heavy tails and central limit theorem.
AI.result: Bias-variance decomposition separates stochastic fluctuation decaying at classical n^{-1/2} rate from curvature-driven error floor that persists regardless of sample size. Minimax lower bounds confirm both terms are unavoidable. Sharp closed-form formulas for tangent bundle of round sphere. Experiments on sphere validate theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a complete statistical theory for geometric data transport on manifolds, quantifying unavoidable curvature effects and establishing optimal estimators that account for both stochastic variability and deterministic geometric bias.

title: AdvNav: Behavior-Guided Black-Box Adversarial Attacks on Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Despite progress in Embodied AI, Vision-and-Language Navigation systems remain vulnerable to adversarial visual disturbances. Most existing methods rely on white-box access to target model gradients, which is often unrealistic for real-world deployed systems and computationally exhaustive due to recursive backpropagation for optimization, limiting their applicability. While previous black-box methods predominantly target single-step, instantaneous decision tasks, they struggle to handle the task complexities and temporal dependencies. This highlights the need for a gradient-free attack method that can effectively disrupt the multistep sequential perception-action loop using only observable inputs and outputs. Therefore, we propose AdvNav, a behavior-guided black-box adversarial attack framework that disturbs an agent's first-person views during navigation. To construct an informative surrogate objective for effective optimization guidance in gradient-free search under the black-box setting, we design a dual-granularity behavior-based feedback, aggregating a trajectory-level performance score representing overall navigation degradation, an action-level reward score considering the potential decision risk, and a deviation indicator, all of which are extracted from the agent's self-output behaviors. This feedback guides a hybrid optimization strategy that heuristically tunes perturbation strength via adaptive updates and evolves noise spatial structure genetically, to iteratively discover the most disruptive noise configuration. Evaluated against Transformer-based HAMT and LLM-based MapGPT with two types of backbones on R2R dataset, AdvNav achieves 49.70/65.96/87.30% Attack Success Rate. The result demonstrates the effectiveness and generality of AdvNav, reveals critical perception vulnerabilities and offers insights for the design of future resilient VLN models.

AI.tldr: AdvNav is a black-box adversarial attack framework that disturbs first-person views in Vision-and-Language Navigation using behavior-guided feedback and hybrid optimization, achieving high attack success rates without requiring model gradients.
AI.motivation: Existing adversarial attack methods for Vision-and-Language Navigation systems rely on white-box access to model gradients, which is unrealistic for deployed systems. Black-box methods struggle with the temporal dependencies and complexity of multistep sequential navigation tasks. There's a need for gradient-free attacks that can disrupt the perception-action loop using only observable inputs/outputs.
AI.method: AdvNav uses a dual-granularity behavior-based feedback system: trajectory-level performance score for overall navigation degradation, action-level reward score for decision risk, and deviation indicator. This guides a hybrid optimization strategy with adaptive perturbation strength updates and genetic evolution of noise spatial structure to iteratively find disruptive noise configurations.
AI.result: AdvNav achieves 49.70%, 65.96%, and 87.30% Attack Success Rate when evaluated against Transformer-based HAMT and LLM-based MapGPT models with different backbones on the R2R dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effectiveness and generality, reveals critical perception vulnerabilities in VLN systems, and provides insights for designing more resilient future models.

title: AutoNorm: Understanding Adaptive Normalization in Transformers through Differentiable Gating
summary: Normalization is a critical component for stabilizing Transformer training, yet the choice between static strategies such as Layer Normalization (LN) and adaptive alternatives remains largely task-dependent. In this paper, we investigate a key optimization challenge in differentiable normalization gating. Our experiments show that, on relatively stationary vision tasks, the high gradient variance introduced by Gumbel-Softmax gating can hinder convergence of the routing mechanism, causing learned gates to underperform simple random selection. In contrast, on non-stationary language modeling and classification tasks, sustained gating diversity enables the model to learn more effective layer-wise normalization policies. Motivated by these observations, we propose AutoNorm-S (Stabilized), a training strategy that mitigates optimization instability through a gate-freezing schedule. AutoNorm-S achieves competitive or improved performance across multiple benchmarks, outperforming adaptive normalization baselines on NLP datasets, including PTB and SST-2, while remaining competitive on standard vision benchmarks. These results suggest that decoupling normalization selection from optimization noise provides a practical and principled approach for adaptive normalization in Transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: AutoNorm-S introduces a gate-freezing schedule to stabilize differentiable normalization gating in Transformers, addressing optimization challenges where gradient variance hinders convergence on stationary vision tasks while leveraging sustained gating diversity on non-stationary NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: The choice between static normalization (like LayerNorm) and adaptive normalization is task-dependent. Differentiable normalization gating suffers from high gradient variance in Gumbel-Softmax gating, which can cause learned gates to underperform random selection on stationary vision tasks, while on non-stationary NLP tasks, sustained gating diversity enables learning effective normalization policies.
AI.method: Proposes AutoNorm-S (Stabilized), a training strategy that mitigates optimization instability through a gate-freezing schedule. This approach decouples normalization selection from optimization noise to provide stable adaptive normalization.
AI.result: AutoNorm-S achieves competitive or improved performance across multiple benchmarks, outperforming adaptive normalization baselines on NLP datasets (PTB and SST-2) while remaining competitive on standard vision benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling normalization selection from optimization noise provides a practical and principled approach for adaptive normalization in Transformer architectures, addressing the key optimization challenges in differentiable normalization gating.

title: Are LLMs Ready for Scientific Discovery? A Capability-Oriented Benchmark for AI Scientists
summary: Existing benchmarks for scientific data analysis evaluate LLMs primarily on code execution or workflow completion, overlooking that scientific analysis serves to support distinct types of scientific claims: hypothesis exploration, statistical inference, mechanistic explanation, each with different assumptions and validity criteria. We introduce SDABench, a benchmark that reorganizes evaluation around six capabilities (descriptive, exploratory, inferential, predictive, causal, and mechanistic) across five domains (Biology, Chemistry, Environment, Geography, Physics). SDABench comprises 527 real-data instances (SDA-Real) and 6000 synthetic instances (SDA-Synth), each in both multiple-choice and open-ended formats, constructed through an automated pipeline. Evaluating 15 representative LLMs, we find that models handle descriptive analysis well but degrade sharply on tasks requiring assumption selection, latent-process modeling, or mechanistic reasoning. SDABench further provides a five-stage error analysis framework that locates where LLMs fail: more advanced models more reliably identify the relevant scope and variables, but still struggle to select appropriate analytical procedures, model variable relationships, and draw valid conclusions.

AI.tldr: SDABench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs on scientific data analysis, focusing on six analysis capabilities across five scientific domains, revealing that while models handle basic descriptive tasks well, they struggle with advanced reasoning tasks like assumption selection and mechanistic analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for scientific data analysis overlook that scientific analysis serves different types of scientific claims (hypothesis exploration, statistical inference, mechanistic explanation) with different validity criteria. Current benchmarks mainly evaluate code execution or workflow completion rather than core scientific reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Created SDABench benchmark with 527 real-data instances (SDA-Real) and 6000 synthetic instances (SDA-Synth) across five domains (Biology, Chemistry, Environment, Geography, Physics). Organized evaluation around six analysis capabilities: descriptive, exploratory, inferential, predictive, causal, and mechanistic. Used both multiple-choice and open-ended formats with an automated construction pipeline. Evaluated 15 representative LLMs and developed a five-stage error analysis framework to identify failure points.
AI.result: Models perform well on descriptive analysis but degrade sharply on tasks requiring assumption selection, latent-process modeling, or mechanistic reasoning. More advanced models better identify relevant scope and variables but still struggle with selecting appropriate analytical procedures, modeling variable relationships, and drawing valid conclusions.
AI.conclusion: SDABench provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating LLMs on scientific data analysis by focusing on core scientific reasoning capabilities rather than just code execution. The benchmark reveals fundamental limitations in LLMs' ability to handle advanced scientific reasoning tasks, highlighting areas for future improvement in scientific AI systems.

title: M+Adam: Low-Precision Training via Additive-Multiplicative Optimization
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 45 pages
summary: Training with quantized weights can reduce costs but often results in degraded accuracy, especially when optimization is carried out in low precision, without storing high-precision copies. We identify a key failure mode: under low precision, standard optimizers can get stuck and not make progress, especially at large weight magnitudes due to coarse mantissa resolution. To overcome this, multiplicative updates have been previously proposed, in place of additive updates in standard optimizers. While successful under extremely low precision, such as under the logarithmic number system, they suffer from failures near zero and across sign changes. The failure modes of additive and multiplicative updates are therefore complementary. To exploit this, we propose M+Adam, which combines both update types: additive steps handle sign changes and small magnitudes, while multiplicative steps ensure progress at large magnitudes when additive updates are zeroed out under rounding. We prove monotone descent for M+Adam under standard smoothness assumptions. Across LLaMA-style pretraining with 60M-1B models, 1x-8x Chinchilla budgets, and using only BF16, FP8, and FP4 master weights, M+Adam consistently improves low-precision training.

AI.tldr: M+Adam: A new optimizer combining additive and multiplicative updates to enable effective low-precision training without accuracy degradation, maintaining performance even with BF16, FP8, and FP4 precision.
AI.motivation: Training with quantized weights reduces computational costs but often degrades accuracy, especially when using low precision without storing high-precision copies. Standard optimizers fail at large weight magnitudes due to coarse mantissa resolution, while multiplicative updates fail near zero and during sign changes.
AI.method: Proposes M+Adam which combines both additive and multiplicative updates: additive steps handle sign changes and small magnitudes, while multiplicative steps ensure progress at large magnitudes when additive updates would be zeroed out due to rounding. This hybrid approach exploits the complementary failure modes of both update types.
AI.result: Theoretical proof of monotone descent under standard smoothness assumptions. Empirical results show consistent improvement in low-precision training across LLaMA-style models (60M-1B parameters) with various Chinchilla budgets, using only BF16, FP8, and FP4 precision without accuracy degradation.
AI.conclusion: M+Adam successfully addresses the complementary failure modes of additive and multiplicative updates in low-precision training, enabling cost-effective quantization while maintaining model performance across various scales and precision levels.

title: NVAITC AI Scientist: A Governed End-to-End Research System -- A Hypertension GWAS Case Study
summary: Agentic research systems are emerging as a new paradigm for coordinating scientific workflows beyond isolated model inference, code generation, or statistical analysis. However, deployment in institutional biomedical environments requires governed mechanisms for research planning, data access, workflow orchestration, evidence tracking, reproducibility, and human oversight. We present NVAITC AI Scientist (NAIS), a governed end-to-end agentic research system designed to support domain-general scientific workflows while keeping protected data within institutional privacy boundaries. NAIS integrates proposal review, execution planning, governed computational routing, reproducible workflow orchestration, evidence generation, and scientist-in-the-loop oversight. We validate NAIS in a real-world hypertension genome-wide association study (GWAS) using hospital-linked genotype and electronic health record (EHR) data from 286,422 individuals under an aggregate-only data policy. The agent planned cohort extraction, orchestrated GWAS execution, generated quality-control summaries, and drafted publication-oriented outputs. Human-AI review identified phenotype discrepancies and enabled iterative refinement of the hypertension definition. After reconciliation, the agent-orchestrated GWAS reproduced established hypertension loci, including FGF5, ATP2B1, CNNM2, FTO, and GRB14, with the strongest signal at FGF5 reaching $-\log_{10}(p) \sim 70$. As a secondary demonstration, NAIS also supported a drug-induced liver injury prediction workflow, achieving a multimodal graph neural network AUC of 0.842. These results demonstrate that governed agentic research systems can support scalable AI-assisted biomedical discovery while producing outputs comparable to expert-led workflows.

AI.tldr: NAIS is a governed agentic research system that coordinates end-to-end scientific workflows while maintaining data privacy boundaries, validated through hypertension GWAS and drug-induced liver injury prediction with expert-comparable results.
AI.motivation: Agentic research systems need governed mechanisms for deployment in institutional biomedical environments that require research planning, data access controls, workflow orchestration, evidence tracking, reproducibility, and human oversight while keeping protected data within privacy boundaries.
AI.method: NAIS integrates proposal review, execution planning, governed computational routing, reproducible workflow orchestration, evidence generation, and scientist-in-the-loop oversight. It coordinates scientific workflows while enforcing institutional data policies (aggregate-only in this case).
AI.result: Validated on real-world hypertension GWAS with 286,422 individuals: reproduced established loci (FGF5, ATP2B1, CNNM2, FTO, GRB14) with FGF5 reaching -log10(p) ~70. Also achieved 0.842 AUC for drug-induced liver injury prediction using multimodal graph neural networks.
AI.conclusion: Governed agentic research systems like NAIS can support scalable AI-assisted biomedical discovery while producing outputs comparable to expert-led workflows and maintaining necessary governance and privacy controls.

title: modelDNA: Calibrated Lineage Verification and Merge Decomposition from Sampled Weight Fingerprints
summary: The lineage graph of open-weight language models is self-reported: Hugging Face's base_model metadata field is optional and unverified, and over 60% of Hub models document no parentage at all. Methods for detecting lineage from weights exist in the research literature, but each ships as paper code tied to one signal and one experiment; when a provenance dispute breaks, the analysis is redone by hand. This report describes modelDNA, a tool that fingerprints a model from roughly 100-300 MB of ranged HTTP reads (instead of a full 15 GB download for a 7B model), compares the fingerprint against a reference database of foundation models across four published signal families, and returns one of eight verdict classes with a calibrated probability, preferring honest abstention to confident error. On a benchmark of 15 real Hub models with org-documented parentage, judged against 8 candidate bases (13 positives, 107 hard negatives), the system achieves AUROC 1.0, zero false positives at its reporting threshold, and 13/13 correct top-1 parent attribution. The report's second contribution is merge decomposition. Every mainstream weight-merging method is (near-)linear per tensor, and fingerprint sample positions are deterministic functions of tensor identity, so a merged model's fingerprint is the same linear combination of its parents' fingerprints. Mixture weights can therefore be recovered from fingerprints alone by sum-to-one constrained least squares. Against merges with published mergekit configurations as ground truth, the method recovers a slerp merge's layer-interpolation curves at r = 0.999 and a dare_ties merge's mixture weights to within 0.011 of the published values, without downloading any weights beyond the fingerprints. All fingerprints, benchmarks, and the inferred lineage graph of 55 models are public and reproducible offline.

AI.tldr: modelDNA is a tool for fingerprinting language models using partial HTTP downloads, detecting parentage with high accuracy, and decomposing merged models to recover mixture weights from fingerprints alone.
AI.motivation: Current lineage tracking for open-weight models is unreliable: Hugging Face's metadata is optional/unverified, most models don't document parentage, and existing detection methods require full downloads and manual analysis during provenance disputes.
AI.method: Creates fingerprints from ~100-300MB of ranged HTTP reads (vs full ~15GB download for 7B models), compares against reference database using four signal families, and returns calibrated probability verdicts. For merged models, exploits linearity of weight merging to recover mixture weights via constrained least squares on fingerprints.
AI.result: Achieves AUROC 1.0, zero false positives, and 13/13 correct top-1 parent attribution on benchmark. For merge decomposition: recovers slerp merge layer-interpolation curves at r=0.999 and dare_ties mixture weights within 0.011 of ground truth.
AI.conclusion: modelDNA provides efficient, accurate lineage detection and merge decomposition without full weight downloads, enabling automated provenance analysis and transparent model lineage tracking.

title: OS-Pruner: Pruning Chains-of-Thought of Reasoning Models via Optimal Stopping
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks through Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. However, these models often exhibit "computational overthinking," generating redundant reasoning steps that increase latency and cost without improving accuracy. Recent studies suggest that CoT trajectories can be significantly pruned, yet existing methods often rely on forcing a static thinking budget, heuristic filtering, sub-optimal early exit via classification, or expensive re-training. In this paper, we introduce OS-Pruner, a lightweight plug-in framework that formulates chain-of-thought pruning as an optimal stopping problem. Given a reasoning prefix, OS-Pruner learns whether further reasoning is worth its token cost by optimizing an explicit utility that trades off final-answer accuracy against generated length. Our novel formulation enables the model to dynamically assess the sufficient point of termination for a reasoning chain. OS-Pruner is designed to be lightweight during both training and inference, and to provide users with fine-grained control over the reasoning-effort vs. accuracy trade-off. On diverse reasoning benchmarks and base models, OS-Pruner achieves 20-60\% reduction in generation length with minimal accuracy sacrifice.

AI.tldr: OS-Pruner is a lightweight framework that treats CoT reasoning as an optimal stopping problem, dynamically pruning unnecessary reasoning steps to reduce latency and cost while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs with Chain-of-Thought prompting suffer from "computational overthinking" - generating redundant reasoning steps that increase latency and cost without improving accuracy. Existing pruning methods are suboptimal (static budgets, heuristic filtering, early exit classification, or require expensive retraining).
AI.method: OS-Pruner formulates CoT pruning as an optimal stopping problem, learning whether further reasoning is worth its token cost by optimizing an explicit utility function that trades off final-answer accuracy against generated length. It's designed as a lightweight plug-in framework for dynamic termination assessment.
AI.result: On diverse reasoning benchmarks and base models, OS-Pruner achieves 20-60% reduction in generation length with minimal accuracy sacrifice, providing fine-grained control over reasoning-effort vs. accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: OS-Pruner provides an effective, lightweight solution to computational overthinking in LLMs by framing reasoning chain optimization as an optimal stopping problem, enabling significant token reduction while maintaining accuracy.

title: Auditing Construct Overlap in Explainable Machine Learning: Evidence from Burnout-Depression Prediction Across Student Cohorts
summary: Explainable machine learning (XML) pipelines applied to composite mental health outcomes can produce apparently-robust, cross-population-stable risk hierarchies that are largely artefacts of how the outcome was constructed. We demonstrate this using an ElasticNet pipeline applied to 886 medical students at the University of Lausanne (primary cohort, 2022), validated across 2,580 longitudinal observations at three time points and 701 non-medical students from eight faculties; all three datasets share identical instruments. The pipeline produces a hierarchy in which trait anxiety and health satisfaction dominate wherever the outcome is measured, with Kendall $τ= 1.0$ for the top-two positions across all five evaluation sets and consistent transfer performance ($R^2$: 0.41-0.49). Two residualization experiments, which isolate shared variance between correlated variables via regression, reveal the mechanism: when trait anxiety (STAI-T) is residualized against the co-included depression subscale (CES-D, $r = 0.72$), model $R^2$ drops from 0.41 to 0.16 and STAI-T falls from rank 1 to rank 6; when burnout subscales are residualized against CES-D, $R^2$ collapses to 0.016. Prediction intervals average 35.4 units on a 0-100 scale (2.4 outcome standard deviations), independently ruling out individual-level deployment. The residualization protocol is the paper's transferable contribution: any XAI study combining correlated predictor and outcome constructs should apply this check before interpreting apparent stability as a finding.

AI.tldr: XML pipelines can create misleading risk hierarchies in mental health studies due to outcome construction artifacts; residualization experiments reveal these artifacts.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that explainable machine learning pipelines can produce apparently robust, cross-population-stable risk hierarchies that are actually artifacts of how composite mental health outcomes are constructed, rather than genuine findings.
AI.method: Applied ElasticNet pipeline to 886 medical students (primary cohort), validated across 2,580 longitudinal observations and 701 non-medical students from eight faculties. Conducted two residualization experiments: 1) residualizing trait anxiety (STAI-T) against correlated depression subscale (CES-D), 2) residualizing burnout subscales against CES-D.
AI.result: Pipeline produced hierarchy where trait anxiety and health satisfaction dominated across all datasets (Kendall τ=1.0). Residualization experiments revealed the artifact: when STAI-T was residualized against CES-D, model R² dropped from 0.41 to 0.16 and STAI-T fell from rank 1 to 6; when burnout subscales were residualized against CES-D, R² collapsed to 0.016. Prediction intervals were too wide (35.4 units) for individual-level deployment.
AI.conclusion: The apparent stability of risk hierarchies in XML studies can be artifacts of outcome construction when correlated predictors are used. The residualization protocol is a crucial check that should be applied before interpreting such findings as genuine.

title: A Formal Hierarchical Architecture for Agentic Orchestration with Stack-Based Execution and Lazy Discovery
summary: The rapid expansion of capabilities in Large Language Model (LLM) agents has exposed a critical architectural bottleneck: when agents are given access to a flat, monolithic registry of tools, the model must evaluate hundreds or thousands of options simultaneously. This leads to decision-space explosion, context window saturation, and degraded routing accuracy. To address these limitations, this paper presents a hierarchical, skill-based architecture for agentic orchestration. Capabilities are organized as a rooted tree where internal nodes make routing decisions and leaf nodes execute deterministic tasks. The runtime enforces a single-step execution loop governed by a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack, giving the agent a form of memory akin to a Pushdown Automaton, therefore enabling it to track nested execution contexts and resume deterministically from any depth. Capability discovery follows a manifest-driven, lazy-loading protocol: only the immediate children of the active node are loaded, so memory and prompt costs scale with the explored path rather than the global registry. By replacing global memory with localized stack frames, the architecture prevents outputs from one execution branch from leaking into another, establishing the isolation guarantees required for deployment in regulated enterprise environments. We also discuss UPI Help, an AI-powered digital payments support product, as a motivating production deployment context. We provide a mathematical formalization of the orchestration state, detailed algorithmic analysis of the execution loop, and controlled benchmarks comparing flat and hierarchical routing under increasing tool catalogs, multi-step workflow pressure, and visible schema-token exposure per LLM call.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical, skill-based architecture for LLM agents that organizes tools as a tree, uses LIFO stack execution, and lazy-loads capabilities to prevent decision-space explosion and context window saturation.
AI.motivation: Flat, monolithic tool registries in LLM agents cause decision-space explosion, context window saturation, and degraded routing accuracy when agents must evaluate hundreds/thousands of options simultaneously.
AI.method: Organizes capabilities as a rooted tree (internal nodes route, leaf nodes execute), uses LIFO stack for single-step execution loop, implements manifest-driven lazy-loading where only immediate children of active node are loaded, replacing global memory with localized stack frames.
AI.result: Enables nested execution context tracking, deterministic resumption from any depth, prevents output leakage between execution branches, provides isolation guarantees for regulated environments, and scales memory/prompt costs with explored path rather than global registry.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical architecture solves key limitations of flat tool registries, enables enterprise deployment with isolation guarantees, and is validated through UPI Help production deployment and controlled benchmarks comparing flat vs hierarchical routing.

title: Modernizing HEBO: a robust Bayesian optimization baseline for practical heteroskedastic and non-stationary problems
summary: Bayesian optimization is increasingly used to guide data-efficient experimentation in chemistry, materials science, and related laboratory settings, but its practical performance depends strongly on how well surrogate-model assumptions match the geometry and noise structure of the underlying objective. We introduce tidyHEBO, a robust Bayesian optimization model inspired by heteroskedastic evolutionary Bayesian optimization (HEBO) for single-objective, sequential optimization. tidyHEBO reconstructs the HEBO design philosophy in BoTorch and revises surrogate training, output-warping selection, acquisition function evaluation, and Pareto-front search. We benchmarked tidyHEBO on synthetic functions, Olympus emulators, fully experimental reaction-optimization datasets, needle-in-a-haystack (NIAH) materials problems, and Bayesmark hyperparameter optimization tasks. On these tasks tidyHEBO achieved competitive to superior performance and improvement in robustness across repeated optimization runs. We therefore propose tidyHEBO as a practical tool for sequential experimentations and a strong general-purpose benchmark for future Bayesian optimization research.

AI.tldr: tidyHEBO is a robust Bayesian optimization model that reconstructs and improves the HEBO design philosophy in BoTorch, showing competitive performance across diverse optimization tasks from synthetic functions to real experimental data.
AI.motivation: Bayesian optimization is widely used in chemistry and materials science for data-efficient experimentation, but its performance heavily depends on how well surrogate model assumptions match the objective's geometry and noise structure. There's a need for more robust optimization methods that work well across diverse experimental settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce tidyHEBO, which reconstructs the heteroskedastic evolutionary Bayesian optimization (HEBO) design philosophy in the BoTorch framework. The method revises several key components: surrogate training, output-warping selection, acquisition function evaluation, and Pareto-front search to improve robustness.
AI.result: tidyHEBO was benchmarked on synthetic functions, Olympus emulators, experimental reaction-optimization datasets, needle-in-a-haystack materials problems, and Bayesmark hyperparameter optimization tasks. It achieved competitive to superior performance with improved robustness across repeated optimization runs.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose tidyHEBO as both a practical tool for sequential experimentation and a strong general-purpose benchmark for future Bayesian optimization research, offering improved robustness and competitive performance across diverse optimization scenarios.

title: NextFund: A Unified Performance Tracking Platform for Agentic Portfolio Management
summary: Large language models (LLMs) based agents are beginning to participate in portfolio construction and market analysis, where decisions must be justified under evolving information and risk constraints. Current assessment practice, however, remains poorly aligned with this setting: many studies rely on static examinations or report only terminal portfolio returns, while the intermediate evidence, analyst judgments, and execution steps that produced those returns stay largely invisible. We introduce NextFund, an evaluation platform that makes financial-agent behavior observable under live market conditions. The platform couples time-consistent market access, coordinated multi-agent analysis, and persistent logging of the full decision path from observation to trade. Through an interactive Trading Arena, users can compare models across markets, inspect equity curves, and drill from leaderboard outcomes down to individual justifications. We present NextFund on Hong Kong, U.S., and China A-share equities, illustrating how inspectable decision histories enable fairer benchmarking and more actionable diagnosis. Our demo is available at https://paradoox.cn/nextfund/.

AI.tldr: NextFund is an evaluation platform that makes financial-agent behavior observable under live market conditions, enabling transparent benchmarking of LLM-based portfolio agents through inspectable decision histories rather than just terminal returns.
AI.motivation: Current assessment practices for LLM-based financial agents are poorly aligned with real-world portfolio construction and market analysis settings. Many studies rely on static examinations or only report terminal portfolio returns, making intermediate evidence, analyst judgments, and execution steps largely invisible despite the need for decisions to be justified under evolving information and risk constraints.
AI.method: Introduces NextFund, an evaluation platform that couples time-consistent market access, coordinated multi-agent analysis, and persistent logging of the full decision path from observation to trade. Features an interactive Trading Arena where users can compare models across markets, inspect equity curves, and drill from leaderboard outcomes down to individual justifications.
AI.result: Demonstrated NextFund on Hong Kong, U.S., and China A-share equities, showing how inspectable decision histories enable fairer benchmarking and more actionable diagnosis of financial-agent performance under live market conditions.
AI.conclusion: NextFund addresses the transparency gap in financial-agent evaluation by providing observable decision paths under live market conditions, moving beyond static assessments and terminal returns to enable comprehensive, fair benchmarking that reveals the intermediate reasoning and execution steps behind portfolio decisions.

title: From Self-Attention to Connection Laplacian: A Unified Operator View of Transformers
summary: Self-attention is a ubiquitous primitive in modern sequence models, yet its operator-level geometry is only partially understood. We view a token sequence as a vector field over the token-position graph and identify attention as a connection walk: messages are aggregated by a nonnegative walk matrix while being transported along each edge by a learned linear map. Within this framework, we prove that single-head attention (SHA) is exactly a connection propagation step with constant transport, and that multi-head attention (MHA) is exactly a single edge-dependent connection walk whose effective transport is an attention-gated mixture of headwise transports. We further clarify the conditions under which the corresponding generator reduces to a random-walk connection Laplacian, highlighting the roles of stochasticity, reversibility, and metric-compatible transports. Empirically, we find that trained Transformers across scales (from 124M to 8B) and structures (encoder/decoder) exhibit geometric structure consistent with our theory: effective attention graphs converge to stable geometric operators in deeper layers, learned transports self-organize into approximate scaled isometries, and both phenomena strengthen consistently with scale. Overall, the paper provides a precise connection-walk formalism that links self-attention to classical geometric operators, along with a set of operator-level tools for analyzing transformer models from a geometric perspective.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a geometric interpretation of self-attention by viewing token sequences as vector fields and identifying attention mechanisms as connection walks on graphs, linking them to classical geometric operators.
AI.motivation: While self-attention is fundamental to modern sequence models, its operator-level geometry remains only partially understood. The authors seek to provide a precise mathematical framework to understand the geometric structure of attention mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors view token sequences as vector fields over the token-position graph and identify attention as a connection walk, where messages are aggregated by a nonnegative walk matrix while being transported along edges by learned linear maps. They prove theoretical results about single-head and multi-head attention within this framework and analyze the conditions under which the generator reduces to a random-walk connection Laplacian.
AI.result: The paper shows that single-head attention is exactly a connection propagation step with constant transport, and multi-head attention is a single edge-dependent connection walk whose effective transport is an attention-gated mixture of headwise transports. Empirical analysis of trained Transformers across scales (124M to 8B parameters) reveals that effective attention graphs converge to stable geometric operators in deeper layers, learned transports self-organize into approximate scaled isometries, and both phenomena strengthen consistently with model scale.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a precise connection-walk formalism that links self-attention to classical geometric operators, offering operator-level tools for analyzing transformer models from a geometric perspective and revealing previously unrecognized geometric structure in trained attention mechanisms.

title: The Hidden Footprint: Making Storage a First-Class Metric for LLM Agent Evaluation
summary: LLM agent benchmarks measure task completion, reliability, and inference cost, but not the persistent data an agent run leaves on disk, including logs, context snapshots, checkpoints, and debug traces. We introduce AgentFootprint, a cross-framework benchmark of post-run agent storage footprint. Its serialization-aware metric suite measures total retention, channel composition, duplication, growth, compressibility, and conversation-history reconstructability. It addresses a measurement trap: naive byte-level measurement understates duplication by an order of magnitude because database paging and JSON escaping obscure repeated content. A fixed-trace control separates agent-generated logical volume from persistence-layer amplification: replaying the same trajectory through seven persisting frameworks yields a 6.7x spread. Under identical models, tools, and tasks, configurations with 100% accuracy differ by 15.7x in retained bytes, although their defaults support different recovery and audit capabilities. Three full-history configurations grow superlinearly on a repeated-observation stress task. Exported trajectories from 108 instance-normalized SWE-bench Verified submissions span three orders of magnitude per instance, with no detectable correlation with resolve rate. A content-addressed store reduces retention by 4.8x-32.7x while preserving every reconstructability score. These results establish persistent storage as a resource metric to report jointly with accuracy and reconstructability.

AI.tldr: AgentFootprint is a benchmark measuring the persistent storage footprint (logs, checkpoints, traces) left by LLM agents, revealing significant storage inefficiencies across frameworks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent benchmarks focus on task completion, reliability, and inference costs but ignore the persistent storage footprint agents leave behind (logs, context snapshots, checkpoints, debug traces), creating hidden costs and inefficiencies.
AI.method: Introduced AgentFootprint benchmark with serialization-aware metrics measuring total retention, channel composition, duplication, growth, compressibility, and conversation-history reconstructability. Used fixed-trace controls to separate agent-generated content from persistence-layer amplification, and tested across seven persisting frameworks.
AI.result: Found 6.7x storage spread across frameworks for identical trajectories; 15.7x difference in retained bytes among 100%-accuracy configurations; superlinear growth in full-history configurations; 4.8x-32.7x reduction using content-addressed stores while maintaining reconstructability; no correlation between storage use and task success rate.
AI.conclusion: Persistent storage should be reported as a resource metric alongside accuracy and cost, as framework choices significantly impact storage efficiency without necessarily improving task outcomes.

title: LayerNorm as Implicit Gain Control in Looped Transformers
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures, 13 tables. Code available at https://github.com/clojure-finance/mythjure/tree/paper-v1
summary: In pre-LayerNorm looped transformers, LayerNorm inside the recurrent block acts as an implicit gain controller: by coupling the block's local Lipschitz constant inversely to the activation scale, it renders the recurrence Jacobian non-normal -- asymptotically contractive at every verified fixed point even where its operator norm exceeds 1 -- so the true stability budget is the spectral margin, not an operator-norm bound. That margin depletes as the carry $ρ\to 1$, and a minority of initializations never converge to a fixed point at all, so the diagonal carry constraint $ρ(\bar{A}) < 1$ is necessary but not sufficient for convergence of the full recurrence. Training experiments across six tasks, including a controlled ablation, reveal that the linear carry is not the depth-memory mechanism: gradient descent routes memory through the block's more expressive nonlinear recurrence and leaves the stability-constrained carry at rest -- the carry's role is stabilization, not memory. We characterize the boundary of this claim: on tasks with axis-aligned per-channel structure, gradient descent does recruit the carry. All results are derived analytically and verified in a from-scratch, CPU-scale implementation; verification at larger scale is needed.

AI.tldr: LayerNorm in looped transformers acts as implicit gain control, enabling stable recurrence even when operator norms exceed 1, but the linear carry mechanism is not used for memory - it's just for stabilization, unless tasks have per-channel structure.
AI.motivation: To understand the actual role of LayerNorm in pre-LayerNorm looped transformers and determine whether the linear carry mechanism (with diagonal constraint) is the primary memory mechanism in these architectures.
AI.method: Analytical derivation combined with experimental verification on six tasks using a from-scratch, CPU-scale implementation. Includes controlled ablation studies to isolate mechanisms.
