AI.result: LayerNorm acts as implicit gain controller by coupling block's Lipschitz constant to activation scale, making recurrence Jacobian non-normal and contractive at fixed points even when operator norm > 1. The spectral margin (not operator norm) determines stability. Gradient descent routes memory through nonlinear recurrence, not through stability-constrained carry - carry's role is purely stabilization unless tasks have per-channel structure.
AI.conclusion: The linear carry constraint ρ(Ā) < 1 is necessary but not sufficient for convergence. The carry mechanism is not the depth-memory mechanism in most cases; it serves stabilization while gradient descent uses the more expressive nonlinear recurrence for memory. Boundary case: carry is recruited for axis-aligned per-channel structured tasks.

title: STAMP: Provenance-Guided Credit Assignment for Deep Search Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning for deep-search agents has largely focused on trajectory-level scoring -- outcome correctness, citation-aware rewards, and evidence coverage. Yet the actions that expose supporting documents receive no targeted credit, a gap we call the reward-credit mismatch. We propose STAMP, in which a reference-based verifier judges whether each cited document supports an entity or relation in a training-time evidence graph, and first-exposure attribution traces each supported citation back to the action that first surfaced it. This step credit is injected through sign-preserving advantage modulation, which redistributes advantage across steps without changing the trajectory-level reward or the relative ranking of trajectories within each group. On BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, and xbench-DS, STAMP improves the GRPO baseline by +2.0/+5.5/+3.0 points under matched SFT initialization, training data, and search tools, and composes with both outcome-only and citation-rubric base rewards. Component ablations confirm that the provenance-based credit signal and the sign-preserving advantage modulation each contribute to the gains.

AI.tldr: STAMP introduces step-level credit attribution for document exposure in deep-search agents, addressing reward-credit mismatch by using reference-based verification and first-exposure attribution.
AI.motivation: Current reinforcement learning for deep-search agents focuses on trajectory-level scoring (outcome correctness, citation-aware rewards, evidence coverage), but actions that expose supporting documents receive no targeted credit - a problem called the reward-credit mismatch.
AI.method: STAMP uses a reference-based verifier to judge whether each cited document supports an entity/relation in a training-time evidence graph, and first-exposure attribution traces each supported citation back to the action that first surfaced it. Credit is injected through sign-preserving advantage modulation, which redistributes advantage across steps without changing trajectory-level reward or relative trajectory rankings.
AI.result: On BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, and xbench-DS, STAMP improves the GRPO baseline by +2.0/+5.5/+3.0 points under matched SFT initialization, training data, and search tools. It composes with both outcome-only and citation-rubric base rewards. Component ablations confirm both provenance-based credit signal and sign-preserving advantage modulation contribute to gains.
AI.conclusion: STAMP successfully addresses the reward-credit mismatch problem in deep-search agents by providing step-level credit attribution for document exposure actions, leading to significant performance improvements across multiple benchmarks while maintaining compatibility with existing reward schemes.

title: Learning to Fine-tune Foundation Models under Resource Limitations
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 1 table, accepted and presented at ICMLCN 2026
summary: We study the problem of optimal continual fine-tuning for a pre-trained Foundation Model deployed at a resource-limited device. At each time slot, a new batch of training data arrives, and the controller is faced with two options: either use the data to fine-tune the model and incur a compute cost, or do not fine-tune the model and discard the data. After the decision, the performance of the current model is measured in terms of an application-specific performance metric such as classification accuracy. Our objective is to learn an optimal policy that determines \emph{when to fine-tune the model} on a single task (e.g., sentiment analysis), under a finite compute budget. We formulate this online decision-making problem as a constrained Markov Decision Process, where the system state captures three essential aspects: (\textit{i}) model's performance, (\textit{ii}) computational budget, and (\textit{iii}) data distribution relevance to historic data encountered up to that point. The transition to the next state is stochastic and therefore, we propose a reinforcement learning-based method to solve this problem, namely the \emph{actor-critic} algorithm. We also consider the special case where the performance of fine-tuning for a given model can be predicted or estimated prior to decision; in this case the problem becomes a Dynamic Programming one. Experiments with a large pre-trained model on a widely-used text classification dataset demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms fine-tuning approaches with the same compute budget by more than $4\%$ in terms of accuracy and achieves $97\%$ of full-parameter fine-tuning accuracy while requiring only $25\%$ of the fine-tuning steps.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning approach for optimal continual fine-tuning of foundation models under compute budget constraints, deciding when to fine-tune vs discard new data to maximize model performance.
AI.motivation: Resource-limited devices deploying pre-trained foundation models face the challenge of efficiently using limited compute budget for continual fine-tuning as new data arrives over time. There's a need to decide when to actually fine-tune vs. discard data to maximize model performance under budget constraints.
AI.method: Formulated as a constrained Markov Decision Process where state includes: (i) model performance, (ii) remaining compute budget, (iii) data distribution relevance. Proposed actor-critic reinforcement learning algorithm to learn optimal fine-tuning policy. Also considered special case with performance prediction as Dynamic Programming problem.
AI.result: Experiments with large pre-trained model on text classification dataset show method outperforms baseline fine-tuning approaches by >4% accuracy under same compute budget. Achieves 97% of full-parameter fine-tuning accuracy while requiring only 25% of fine-tuning steps.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reinforcement learning approach effectively learns optimal continual fine-tuning policies that maximize model performance under compute constraints, demonstrating significant efficiency gains over naive fine-tuning strategies.

title: The Path to Self-Evolving Clinical Systems: Scaling Medical Agents from Assistance to Autonomy
summary: The growing ability of large language models and vision language models to jointly interpret and reason over images and text is reshaping medical agents, moving them from task specific predictors toward autonomous systems that perceive, reason, plan, remember, and act in clinical environments. This work departs from the capability first perspective of existing literature and instead begins from clinical deployment, asking what tasks, contamination resistant benchmarks, and interactive training environments are required before medical agents can be trusted in practice. Medical agents are formalized as sequential decision making systems under partial observability, together with a three level autonomy taxonomy spanning assisted, cooperative, and fully autonomous operation. The field is organized along a unified scaling spine consisting of framework scaling, capability scaling, and environment scaling. Within this framework, clinical environment scaling, the integration of tools, data, and clinical gyms, is identified as the most actionable yet underexplored direction for agents operating in PACS, EHR, and FHIR ecosystems. Clinical self evolution, where agents improve through interaction with their environments rather than parameter scaling alone, is further positioned as a key research frontier, drawing insights from self improving agents, agent gyms, and test time compute scaling. Applications across radiology, pathology, ophthalmology, and hospital workflows are examined together with deployment challenges including hallucination, cascading failures, and fairness. By consolidating more than 300 references, with particular emphasis on advances from 2025 to 2026, this work provides a roadmap toward trustworthy, self improving medical imaging systems for real clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Medical agents are evolving from task-specific AI to autonomous systems that perceive, reason, plan, and act in clinical environments. This paper provides a roadmap for trustworthy deployment through clinical environment scaling and self-evolution rather than just model scaling.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to shift from a capability-first perspective (focused on what models can do) to a clinical deployment perspective - asking what's needed before medical agents can be trusted in real practice. Current approaches emphasize model capabilities but neglect practical deployment requirements, clinical benchmarks, and interactive training environments needed for real-world trust.
AI.method: 1) Formalize medical agents as sequential decision-making systems under partial observability. 2) Propose a three-level autonomy taxonomy (assisted, cooperative, fully autonomous). 3) Organize the field along a unified "scaling spine" with three dimensions: framework scaling, capability scaling, and environment scaling. 4) Focus particularly on clinical environment scaling - integrating tools, data, and clinical gyms. 5) Introduce clinical self-evolution where agents improve through environmental interaction rather than just parameter scaling. 6) Analyze applications across medical specialties and deployment challenges.
AI.result: 1) Clinical environment scaling is identified as the most actionable yet underexplored direction for agents in PACS, EHR, and FHIR ecosystems. 2) Clinical self-evolution is positioned as a key research frontier, drawing from self-improving agents, agent gyms, and test-time compute scaling. 3) Provides a comprehensive analysis of applications across radiology, pathology, ophthalmology, and hospital workflows. 4) Identifies critical deployment challenges including hallucination, cascading failures, and fairness. 5) Consolidates over 300 references with emphasis on 2025-2026 advances to create a roadmap for trustworthy medical imaging systems.
AI.conclusion: The path to trustworthy medical agents requires shifting focus from just scaling model capabilities to developing comprehensive clinical deployment frameworks. Clinical environment scaling and self-evolution are critical for creating systems that can operate safely and effectively in real healthcare settings. The paper provides a roadmap toward building autonomous medical imaging systems that can be trusted in clinical practice through structured frameworks, interactive training environments, and continuous improvement mechanisms.

title: On the modality gap and the contrastive loss in multi-modal representation learning
summary: We study the modality gap in CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive learning, where image and text embeddings remain misaligned despite being trained in a shared space. We argue that the gap is induced by a failure of the InfoNCE formulation with independent encoders. We conduct a uni-modal experiment with two independent encoders and identical initialization conditions and find that InfoNCE actively generates a gap at low temperatures. We provide a theoretical analysis of this phenomenon and show that the modality gap is indeed a mode-failure of InfoNCE, but only at low temperatures. We propose a simple modification called xNCE, which uses intermodal as well as intra-modality negative contrastive pairs. xNCE matches retrieval performance on MS-COCO while consistently reducing the gap even at low temperatures. Notably, xNCE improves zero-shot classification over the InfoNCE baseline across all benchmarks, whereas high-temperature InfoNCE and regularized InfoNCE both fail to do so, demonstrating that xNCE reduces the modality gap without sacrificing the discriminative geometry needed for transfer.

AI.tldr: CLIP-style contrastive learning suffers from a modality gap where image and text embeddings remain misaligned. The authors identify this as an InfoNCE failure at low temperatures and propose xNCE with intermodal negative pairs to reduce the gap without harming performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the "modality gap" phenomenon in CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive learning, where despite training in a shared embedding space, image and text embeddings remain separated and misaligned. This gap persists even though the training objective aims to bring positive pairs together and push negatives apart.
AI.method: The authors conduct experiments showing that InfoNCE with independent encoders actively generates a modality gap at low temperatures. They provide theoretical analysis showing this is a mode-failure of InfoNCE. They propose xNCE, a simple modification that uses both intermodal (across modalities) and intra-modality (within the same modality) negative contrastive pairs during training.
AI.result: xNCE matches retrieval performance on MS-COCO while consistently reducing the modality gap even at low temperatures. Notably, it improves zero-shot classification over InfoNCE baselines across all benchmarks, whereas high-temperature InfoNCE and regularized InfoNCE both fail to achieve this improvement.
AI.conclusion: The modality gap in CLIP-style models is a fundamental limitation of the InfoNCE formulation with independent encoders at low temperatures. xNCE successfully addresses this issue by incorporating intermodal negative pairs, reducing the gap while preserving the discriminative geometry needed for effective transfer learning, leading to better zero-shot classification performance.

title: SCALECUA: Scaling Computer Use Agents with Verifiable Task Synthesis and Efficient Online RL
summary: Computer use agents (CUAs) are emerging as a powerful interface for automating complex digital workflows through visual perception and GUI execution. Online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a key direction for scaling their capabilities. However, this paradigm is bottlenecked by verifiable data scarcity and online RL inefficiency. To break these barriers, we introduce ScaleCUA, a unified framework that scales online RL for CUAs via verifiable task synthesis and efficient training. At the data level, we design VeriGen, an end-to-end framework for generating verifiable RL tasks through iterative docker interactions and a multi-agent feedback loop. Scaled to 100+ concurrent agent workers via a shared docker interaction probe, this pipeline produces 24K+ verifiable tasks and nearly 3K high-quality RL tasks. To maximize sample efficiency, we propose Frontier Sampling, which tracks per-task capability and allocates rollouts to the current learning frontier. On the training side, we further design Visual Context Segmentation, a sliding window over recent visual context that balances rollout and training-engine pressure, yielding a 2.83x training speedup over step-wise decomposition. Together, ScaleCUA achieves 68.7% on OSWorld and 54.0% on ScienceBoard, establishing new state-of-the-art performance among open-source computer use agents. Code, models, and datasets are available at https://github.com/THUDM/SCALE-CUA.

AI.tldr: ScaleCUA is a framework that scales online reinforcement learning for computer use agents via verifiable task synthesis and efficient training methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance on OSWorld and ScienceBoard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is crucial for scaling computer use agents (CUAs), but it faces two key bottlenecks: verifiable data scarcity and online RL inefficiency. Existing approaches are limited by the lack of verifiable training data and inefficient online learning processes.
AI.method: ScaleCUA introduces a unified framework with two main components: 1) VeriGen - an end-to-end framework for generating verifiable RL tasks through iterative docker interactions and multi-agent feedback loops, producing 24K+ verifiable tasks and 3K high-quality RL tasks; 2) Frontier Sampling - tracks per-task capability and allocates rollouts to the current learning frontier for sample efficiency; 3) Visual Context Segmentation - a sliding window over recent visual context that balances rollout and training-engine pressure for 2.83x training speedup.
AI.result: ScaleCUA achieves 68.7% on OSWorld and 54.0% on ScienceBoard benchmarks, establishing new state-of-the-art performance among open-source computer use agents. The framework generated 24K+ verifiable tasks and nearly 3K high-quality RL tasks through scalable concurrent processing.
AI.conclusion: The ScaleCUA framework successfully addresses the key bottlenecks in scaling computer use agents by combining verifiable task synthesis with efficient training techniques. By solving both data scarcity and training inefficiency problems, it enables more effective online reinforcement learning for CUAs and advances the state-of-the-art in computer use agent capabilities.

title: Scaffold splits hide structural-frontier failures in ADMET models
summary: Molecular property models are commonly evaluated by holding out Bemis--Murcko scaffolds, yet a scaffold identifier is only one notion of chemical unfamiliarity. We introduce a label-free structural-frontier split that reserves the sparsest and most physicochemically remote scaffold groups, and evaluate it on six public experimental or curated ADMET tasks. Against a 70/10/20 scaffold control with identical acyclic grouping, the frontier inflates equally weighted primary error with a taskwise median of 87.0\% and a skew-sensitive mean of 130.3\% (descriptive task/seed bootstrap interval, 52.1--246.0\%). The mean falls to 75.9\% once BBB is removed; that endpoint is the one whose score ranking inverts at the frontier. A message-passing graph-network control still shows a large gap (mean 82.8\% over four tasks) and does not invert, so a low-capacity head does not explain the effect. We also test Multi-View Frontier Risk Extrapolation (\method), a count-adjusted tail-risk penalty over four molecular views, and treat it as a falsifiable probe. It changes normalized frontier error by only 0.16\% relative to empirical risk minimization for the perceptron head (interval, $-0.43$--0.84\%) and by $-1.9$\% for the graph network; three fixed robust-penalty controls are likewise inconclusive. Against the published Lo-Hi and DataSAIL splitters the frontier inflates error more on average, though no split is uniformly hardest. An audit of 31,561 marine natural products further shows that OOD status and agreement with legacy ADMET predictions depend on the molecular view, endpoint and teacher coverage. Split construction and label provenance are important evaluation constraints in their own right, and the tested training penalties do not resolve the frontier failures we observe.

AI.tldr: Molecular property prediction models fail dramatically on structurally frontier (chemically novel) compounds, with errors inflating 52-246% compared to standard scaffold splits, and current robust training methods don't fix this failure.
AI.motivation: Current molecular property model evaluation relies on scaffold splits, but this only captures one aspect of chemical unfamiliarity. The paper aims to test model performance on truly novel "structural frontier" compounds - those that are sparse and physicochemically remote from training data.
AI.method: Introduced a label-free structural-frontier split that reserves the sparsest and most physicochemically remote scaffold groups. Evaluated on six ADMET tasks with various controls: 70/10/20 scaffold splits, message-passing graph networks, and Multi-View Frontier Risk Extrapolation (MV-FRE) - a count-adjusted tail-risk penalty over four molecular views. Also compared against published Lo-Hi and DataSAIL splitters and audited 31,561 marine natural products.
AI.result: Frontier splits inflate error significantly: taskwise median 87.0% increase, mean 130.3% (bootstrap interval 52.1-246.0%). BBB endpoint shows score ranking inversion at frontier. Message-passing graph networks show similar large gaps (mean 82.8%). Tested robust training methods (including MV-FRE) show negligible improvements: MV-FRE changes error by only 0.16% for perceptron head, -1.9% for graph networks. Frontier splits generally inflate error more than other published splitters, but no split is uniformly hardest.
AI.conclusion: Current molecular property models fail catastrophically on novel frontier compounds despite using robust training methods. Split construction and label provenance are critical evaluation constraints. The frontier failure represents a fundamental challenge not resolved by current training penalties, highlighting the need for better approaches to handle chemical novelty in molecular property prediction.

title: What We Talk About When We Talk About LLM Planning: Evidence for Two Distinct Planning Abilities
comment: 19 pages. Keywords: Reasoning, Automated Planning, Item Responses Theory, LLMs as Planner Research Area: NLP and Symbolic Reasoning Research Area Keywords: neurosymbolic, planning in agents, symbolic reasoning Contribution Types: Model analysis & interpretability
summary: When LLMs exhibit uneven performance across planning tasks, these gaps are often attributed to task difficulty. We argue that this explanation is incomplete, as task-level variation may reflect distinct latent planning competencies rather than differences along a single ability spectrum. We study this question on ACPBench-Hard by evaluating multiple LLM families under varying test-time reasoning budgets and applying a multidimensional item response theory model to uncover the latent competency structure underlying LLM planning. The analysis reveals two principal dimensions that shape planning performance: operational reasoning, the ability to evaluate local action applicability and immediate state transitions, and structural enumeration, the ability to reason about goal reachability and landmark structure. Operational reasoning improving under model scaling and longer reasoning traces, while structural enumeration remains comparatively insensitive. Our findings motivate competency-level evaluation of LLM planning, shifting the focus from whether models improve overall to which planning competencies improve, under what conditions, and why.

AI.tldr: LLM planning performance reflects two latent competencies (operational reasoning and structural enumeration) rather than just task difficulty, with differential improvements under scaling and reasoning budgets.
AI.motivation: Uneven LLM performance across planning tasks is often attributed to task difficulty, but this explanation is incomplete. The authors argue task-level variation may reflect distinct latent planning competencies rather than differences along a single ability spectrum.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple LLM families on ACPBench-Hard under varying test-time reasoning budgets and apply multidimensional item response theory (IRT) modeling to uncover latent competency structure underlying LLM planning.
AI.result: Analysis reveals two principal dimensions shaping planning performance: operational reasoning (evaluating local action applicability and immediate state transitions) and structural enumeration (reasoning about goal reachability and landmark structure). Operational reasoning improves with model scaling and longer reasoning traces, while structural enumeration remains comparatively insensitive to these factors.
AI.conclusion: Findings motivate competency-level evaluation of LLM planning, shifting focus from whether models improve overall to which specific planning competencies improve, under what conditions, and why. This provides more nuanced understanding of LLM planning capabilities.

title: To Answer or to Abstain: Mitigating Search-Agent Hallucinations via Abstention-Aware Reinforcement Learning
summary: Recent advances in equipping Large Language Models (LLMs) with search tools and outcome-reward reinforcement learning (RL) have achieved new state-of-the-art results on open-domain QA tasks. However, we argue that current training paradigms harbor a critical vulnerability: they predominantly reward correct answers but fail to penalize fabricated ones when retrieval fails, thereby implicitly exacerbating hallucinations. To address this, we propose Abstention-Aware Reinforcement Learning (AWA-RL), which dynamically shapes the abstention reward utilizing the model's query-specific prior capabilities and continuous on-policy training observations. We also introduce a novel metric, RA-F1, to measure the capability-reliability trade-off. Compared to non-abstaining baselines, AWA-RL boosts absolute precision by up to 10.3% and overall RA-F1 by 2.9%, with only marginal sacrifice in raw accuracy. These results confirm that AWA-RL successfully yields highly capable and reliable search agents. The code, data, and model weights are publicly available at https://github.com/zfj1998/AWA-RL.

AI.tldr: AWA-RL is a new reinforcement learning framework that teaches LLMs to abstain from answering when uncertain, reducing hallucinations by shaping abstention rewards based on the model's query-specific capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current LLM training paradigms for search-augmented QA systems have a critical vulnerability: they reward correct answers but fail to penalize fabricated responses when retrieval fails, which implicitly exacerbates hallucinations.
AI.method: Proposed Abstention-Aware Reinforcement Learning (AWA-RL) that dynamically shapes abstention rewards using the model's query-specific prior capabilities and continuous on-policy training observations. Also introduced RA-F1 metric to measure capability-reliability trade-off.
AI.result: AWA-RL boosts absolute precision by up to 10.3% and overall RA-F1 by 2.9% compared to non-abstaining baselines, with only marginal sacrifice in raw accuracy. Successfully yields highly capable and reliable search agents.
AI.conclusion: AWA-RL effectively addresses the hallucination problem in search-augmented LLMs by teaching them to abstain when uncertain, achieving a better balance between capability and reliability in open-domain QA tasks.

title: PREF-Gate: Provenance-Constrained Relational Evidence Fusion with Validation-Gated Selection for Graph Fraud Detection
summary: Relational fraud detection can exploit both label-free graph context and label-derived neighborhood evidence, but these two information sources obey different validity conditions. In particular, neighborhood risk becomes invalid when a queried node's own label, or any validation or test label, enters its construction. We formulate this issue as provenance-constrained relational evidence use and present PREF-Gate, an auditable decision framework with two fixed experts and a finite validation gate. The context expert uses attributes, one-hop means, feature residuals, and degree descriptors without labels. The evidence expert adds self-excluded, training-label-only neighborhood risk and empirical-Bayes summaries that expose support, uncertainty, availability, and shrinkage. Before test inference, the gate selects either expert or one of three pre-specified probability mixtures and fixes the decision threshold. On Amazon, YelpChi, and TFinance, using five identical stratified splits and 14 same-protocol methods, PREF-Gate obtains mean AUPRC values of 0.9085, 0.8104, and 0.8913. It selects the label-free expert on all Amazon and YelpChi splits and an evidence mixture on all TFinance splits. Thus, the main result is conditional rather than universal: label-derived relational evidence is useful only where held-out validation supports it. The framework couples competitive ranking performance with an explicit label-provenance contract, finite selection policy, failure accounting, and review-budget evaluation, providing an auditable knowledge-based decision pipeline for graph fraud detection.

AI.tldr: PREF-Gate is an auditable fraud detection framework that separates label-free graph context from label-derived evidence, using a validation gate to select the appropriate expert to prevent contamination from test data labels.
AI.motivation: Relational fraud detection faces contamination issues where test labels can inadvertently leak into training through neighborhood construction, violating proper evaluation protocols.
AI.method: Two fixed experts: context expert (label-free attributes, one-hop means, feature residuals, degree descriptors) and evidence expert (self-excluded training-label-only neighborhood risk, empirical-Bayes summaries). A finite validation gate selects either expert or probability mixtures before test inference.
AI.result: PREF-Gate achieves mean AUPRC of 0.9085 (Amazon), 0.8104 (YelpChi), and 0.8913 (TFinance). It selects label-free expert for Amazon/YelpChi, and evidence mixture for TFinance.
AI.conclusion: Label-derived evidence is conditionally useful only where validation supports it. The framework provides auditable decision-making with explicit label-provenance contracts and competitive performance.

title: Multi-Scale Convolution with Optimal Transport Attention Effect on Multivariate Time Series
summary: The analysis of Multivariate Time Series (MTS) plays an important role in a lot of real-world practical applications, but it still remains some challenging problem about capturing multi-granularity structural patterns and suppressing noise appropriately. Multi-Scale Convolution with Optimal Transport Attention (MSC-OT) is proposed in this paper. MSC-OT is a useful architecture to optimize the attention mechanism. It combines multi-scale convolution with Sinkhorn optimal transport method based on inverted embedding. The inverted embedding approach embeds each variable as a token and allows the model to capture cross-variate relationships better. MSC-OT consists of two part: (1) Multi-Scale Convolution Enhancement, that applies multi-scale convolutions to attention score matrices based on inverted embedding, capturing local structural patterns in the variate-interaction space induced by compressed temporal representations; (2) Sinkhorn Optimal Transport Regularization, that formulates attention computation as an optimal transport problem and employs iterative matrix scaling to ensure balanced information flow across variates. Adaptive Fusion Strategy utilizes softmax-normalized learnable weights to dynamically combine base attention, convolution-enhanced, and OT-regularized scores. Experiments on widely-used datasets, including ETT, Electricity, Traffic, Solar-Energy, and Exchange-Rate, show that MSC-OT achieves well performance in both short-term and long-term forecasting tasks. Ablation experiments further validate the effectiveness of each proposed component and their synergistic contributions to improving prediction accuracy for multivariate time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: MSC-OT is a novel architecture that combines multi-scale convolution with optimal transport attention to improve multivariate time series forecasting by capturing multi-granularity patterns and suppressing noise.
AI.motivation: Multivariate time series analysis faces challenges in capturing multi-granularity structural patterns and appropriately suppressing noise, which limits forecasting performance in real-world applications.
AI.method: Proposes MSC-OT architecture with two main components: 1) Multi-Scale Convolution Enhancement that applies convolutions to attention matrices using inverted embeddings to capture local patterns, and 2) Sinkhorn Optimal Transport Regularization that frames attention as optimal transport problem with balanced information flow. Uses adaptive fusion to combine different attention scores.
AI.result: Experiments on five datasets (ETT, Electricity, Traffic, Solar-Energy, Exchange-Rate) show MSC-OT achieves strong performance in both short-term and long-term forecasting tasks. Ablation studies confirm each component's effectiveness and their synergistic contributions.
AI.conclusion: MSC-OT effectively addresses multi-granularity pattern capture and noise suppression challenges in multivariate time series forecasting through its novel integration of multi-scale convolution with optimal transport attention regularization.

title: Heterogeneous Agent Cohorts for Safe Open-Ended Exploration with Runtime Constraint Memory
summary: LLM agents today are caught in an awkward bind. Lock them down with static safety instructions and they rarely venture beyond the obvious; give them free reign with tools and multi-agent debate, and safety violations quickly follow. Rather than forcing a single model to juggle both creativity and caution, we separate the concerns across specialized roles. A Disrupter generates unconventional proposals, a Validator enforces hard runtime checks at the tool gateway, and a Broker pulls in distant but relevant analogies. Failures are not discarded -- they are compiled, via MCTS, into compact, signed constraint patches we call Scars. These patches are cached locally and inherited by future cohorts, turning repeated failures into reusable, low-cost runtime constraints. In a spatial-semantic sandbox (N=20 runs, p<0.01), our cohort reaches remote targets where debate fails, the Validator prevents all executed breaches, and Scars reduce token consumption by 15.1% by avoiding redundant validator checks. Furthermore, credit-based Communication Allocation Scores (CAS) restrict outbound bandwidth, reducing overall token costs by 55.9% under resource constraints.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent system with specialized roles (Disrupter, Validator, Broker) that uses MCTS to compile failures into reusable "Scars" constraints, achieving better creativity and safety than traditional approaches while reducing token consumption through communication allocation scores.
AI.motivation: LLM agents face a fundamental trade-off between creativity and safety: static safety instructions limit creativity, while free reign with tools leads to safety violations. Current approaches force a single model to balance both concerns, which is suboptimal.
AI.method: Separates concerns across three specialized roles: 1) Disrupter generates unconventional proposals, 2) Validator enforces hard runtime checks at tool gateway, 3) Broker pulls in distant analogies. Failures are compiled via Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) into "Scars" - compact, signed constraint patches that are cached locally and inherited. Communication Allocation Scores (CAS) restrict outbound bandwidth.
AI.result: In a spatial-semantic sandbox (N=20 runs, p<0.01): 1) Cohort reaches remote targets where traditional debate fails, 2) Validator prevents all executed breaches, 3) Scars reduce token consumption by 15.1% by avoiding redundant validator checks, 4) CAS reduces overall token costs by 55.9% under resource constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent architecture with role specialization and failure compilation into reusable constraints effectively resolves the creativity-safety trade-off, enabling both unconventional thinking and guaranteed safety while significantly reducing computational costs through learned constraints and communication management.

title: Policy-Driven CT-Agent: Modeling Phase-Aware Diagnostic Control for Clinically Consistent CT Reasoning
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) diagnosis often relies on dynamic selection of imaging phases, such as non-contrast, arterial, or venous phases, based on preliminary findings, clinical suspicion, and diagnostic guidelines. This phase-wise decision process is critical for reducing unnecessary radiation exposure while supporting timely staging and treatment planning. However, phase-selection protocols can vary across hospitals, regions, and guidelines, while most existing CT-based AI methods assume that all phases are available and focus on static tasks under a fixed imaging phase, failing to model whether additional phases are required. This limitation stems from heterogeneous multi-phase representations, the need for knowledge-guided phase control beyond visual cues, and the lack of supervision for phase-sufficiency decisions in existing datasets. To address these challenges, we propose Policy-Driven CT-Agent (PD-CTAgent) for clinically consistent CT phase selection and diagnostic reasoning. PD-CTAgent introduces a Clinical Structure Abstraction Module (CSAM) to harmonize heterogeneous CT phases into a unified, phase-aware evidence representation. Based on this representation, a Knowledge-Guided Diagnostic Control Model (KDCM) evaluates phase sufficiency and iteratively requests additional phases when necessary. The policy-driven agent design further allows PD-CTAgent to flexibly follow different institutional, regional, or guideline-specific diagnostic protocols. Together, PD-CTAgent bridges static CT analysis and real-world clinical workflows. Experiments on two public datasets, LIDC and MCT-LTDiag, and one private dataset demonstrate its effectiveness and clinical consistency. Code will be made public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PD-CTAgent is a policy-driven AI agent that dynamically selects CT imaging phases for diagnosis, mimicking clinical workflows by deciding whether additional phases are needed based on evolving evidence and following institutional protocols.
AI.motivation: Current CT-based AI methods assume all imaging phases are available and focus on static tasks, failing to model whether additional phases are required. Real clinical workflows involve dynamic phase selection based on preliminary findings, clinical suspicion, and guidelines to reduce radiation exposure while supporting timely diagnosis.
AI.method: Proposes PD-CTAgent with two modules: 1) Clinical Structure Abstraction Module (CSAM) harmonizes heterogeneous CT phases into unified phase-aware evidence representations, and 2) Knowledge-Guided Diagnostic Control Model (KDCM) evaluates phase sufficiency and iteratively requests additional phases when needed, following configurable diagnostic protocols.
AI.result: Experiments on two public datasets (LIDC and MCT-LTDiag) and one private dataset demonstrate the effectiveness and clinical consistency of PD-CTAgent in bridging static CT analysis with real-world clinical workflows.
AI.conclusion: PD-CTAgent successfully addresses the gap between traditional static CT AI methods and clinical reality by providing a policy-driven agent that can dynamically select imaging phases based on evidence and follow institutional protocols, with code to be made publicly available.

title: Bringing Back Rule Induction to Fluid Intelligence Research? An Initial Validation of the ARC-AGI Benchmark in Humans
summary: Two competing perspectives on fluid intelligence (gf) measures propose that performance is primarily constrained either by working memory capacity or by the ability to induce novel relations. The first perspective is currently dominant in measurement, as evident from the use of a limited set of recurring rules, whereas the second perspective is reflected in many definitions but rarely present in measurement. The ARC-AGI benchmark predominantly requires rule induction and was proposed as a measure of gf for both humans and artificial systems. However, its psychometric properties have not yet been examined in human samples. We therefore investigated the psychometric characteristics and nomological network of ARC-AGI in a first study with 100 participants. A compilation of ARC-AGI items showed good psychometric properties and correlated substantially with figural fluid intelligence as measured by a figural reasoning test (\r{ho} = .63). Associations with figural originality were weak. These findings provide initial support for the validity of ARC-AGI as a measure of human fluid intelligence. Future research should include more rule induction tasks as well as additional multivariate covariates. This study is unusual by studying a task in humans that was initially designed for machines. We suggest systematically embedding AI benchmarks into the nomological network of human cognitive abilities to enable more systematic evaluation and interdisciplinary cooperation.

AI.tldr: The ARC-AGI benchmark, designed for AI, shows promising psychometric properties as a human fluid intelligence measure, correlating well with figural reasoning but not with originality.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in examining the psychometric properties of the ARC-AGI benchmark in human samples, as it was initially designed for machines but proposed as a measure of fluid intelligence for both humans and AI systems.
AI.method: Investigated psychometric characteristics and nomological network of ARC-AGI in a first study with 100 participants, examining correlations with figural fluid intelligence and figural originality measures.
AI.result: ARC-AGI items showed good psychometric properties and correlated substantially with figural fluid intelligence (rho = .63), but had weak associations with figural originality.
AI.conclusion: The study provides initial support for the validity of ARC-AGI as a measure of human fluid intelligence, suggesting that AI benchmarks should be systematically embedded in the nomological network of human cognitive abilities to enable more systematic evaluation and interdisciplinary cooperation.

title: The VC dimension of partial concept classes via Radon's theorem
summary:
Following Alon, Hanneke, Holzman, and Moran (FOCS 2021), we define a partial concept class (PCC) as a family of partial functions \(f: V\to\{0,1,\ast\}\); equivalently, its concepts partition the ground set into black ($f^{-1}(1)$), grey ($f^{-1}(\ast)$), and white parts ($f^{-1}(0)$). Its VC dimension is defined by shattering sets on which the value $\ast$ is not taken. We study two geometric PCCs in real Banach spaces, both with a margin \(δ>0\): expanded half-spaces, where the grey part is a strip of width at least \(δ\) adjacent to a half-space, and expanded balls, where the grey part is an annulus of width \(δ\) around a unit radius ball.
  Our main results are dimension-free upper bounds on the VC dimension of the PCC of expanded balls in \(L_p\parenthμ\), \(1\le p<\infty\), including the non-Euclidean and algorithmically particularly relevant case \(\ell^d_1\). These bounds depend on the margin and on the radii, but not on the ambient dimension or the underlying measure space. These are extensions of the work of Bourneuf, Charbit, and Thomassé (FOCS 2025) who studied the PCC of expanded balls in Euclidean space, that is, $\ell_2^d$. We also prove lower bounds on the VC dimension that match the upper bounds in terms of the margin parameter $δ$. Finally, we derive a Dense Neighborhood Lemma in \(L_p\)-spaces, again extending the known Euclidean results.
  Our method relies on the linearization of the distance through a map into a space of non-trivial Rademacher type, and then the use of a balanced signed-sum estimate, or a no-dimensional Radon theorem. The arguments rely on ideas from functional analysis that are clearly explained for the non-expert in that field.

AI.tldr: The paper studies partial concept classes (PCCs) in Banach spaces, focusing on expanded balls in L_p spaces. It provides dimension-free VC dimension bounds for these geometric structures, extending previous Euclidean results to non-Euclidean spaces.
AI.motivation: To extend the study of partial concept classes from Euclidean spaces to general Banach spaces, particularly L_p spaces including the algorithmically important ℓ₁ᵈ case. This addresses the need for understanding learning theory concepts in non-Euclidean settings relevant to modern machine learning.
AI.method: Uses linearization of distance through maps into spaces with non-trivial Rademacher type, then applies balanced signed-sum estimates or no-dimensional Radon theorems. The approach leverages functional analysis techniques made accessible to non-experts.
AI.result: Provides dimension-free upper bounds on VC dimension for expanded balls in L_p spaces (1 ≤ p < ∞) that depend only on margin δ and radii, not on ambient dimension. Also proves matching lower bounds and derives a Dense Neighborhood Lemma for L_p spaces.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends partial concept class analysis from Euclidean to Banach spaces, providing complete characterization of VC dimension for expanded balls in L_p spaces. The work bridges geometric learning theory and functional analysis, with practical implications for algorithms in non-Euclidean spaces.

title: Valid $\ne$ Necessary: Diagnosing Latent Inefficiency in Chain-of-Thought
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet it often incurs substantial computational costs due to over-reasoning: the generation of redundant, verbose, or irrelevant steps. While existing reasoning step evaluators effectively detect logical fallacies and factual errors, our analysis reveals a critical blind spot: they fail to penalize valid but inefficient reasoning steps that inflate token usage without contributing to the solution. To systematically diagnose this limitation, we introduce RIV-GSM8K, a diagnostic benchmark injected with five distinct types of inefficiencies, including circular reasoning and excessive decomposition. Diagnostic experiments reveal that state-of-the-art evaluators struggle to distinguish these inefficiencies from necessary reasoning. To address this gap, we propose CAID (Context-Aware Information Density), a training-free metric grounded in information theory that identifies low-utility steps. To validate the metric's practical utility, we apply it within PACE, a post-hoc compression strategy. Additional control experiments show that the gains of PACE are not explained by trivial pruning: compared with random step removal and PRM-based compression baselines, it preserves accuracy at substantially higher compression rates. Empirical results on GSM8K, StrategyQA, and ARC-Challenge demonstrate that PACE reduces token consumption by 31-53% while maintaining accuracy, confirming that CAID successfully distills informational froth from reasoning chains without compromising deductive validity.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses over-reasoning in Chain-of-Thought prompting by proposing CAID, an information-theoretic metric to identify inefficient reasoning steps, and PACE, a post-hoc compression strategy that reduces token usage by 31-53% while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought prompting suffers from substantial computational costs due to over-reasoning—generating redundant, verbose, or irrelevant steps. Existing reasoning evaluators fail to penalize valid but inefficient reasoning steps that inflate token usage without contributing to solutions, creating a critical blind spot.
AI.method: 1) Introduce RIV-GSM8K diagnostic benchmark with five distinct inefficiency types to reveal evaluator limitations. 2) Propose CAID (Context-Aware Information Density), a training-free metric grounded in information theory that identifies low-utility reasoning steps. 3) Apply CAID within PACE, a post-hoc compression strategy for pruning inefficient steps. 4) Conduct control experiments comparing PACE with random step removal and PRM-based compression baselines.
AI.result: PACE reduces token consumption by 31-53% on GSM8K, StrategyQA, and ARC-Challenge while maintaining accuracy. CAID successfully distinguishes informational froth from necessary reasoning, outperforming state-of-the-art evaluators that struggle to detect inefficiencies. Gains are not explained by trivial pruning—PACE preserves accuracy at substantially higher compression rates than baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that information-theoretic evaluation via CAID enables efficient reasoning compression without compromising deductive validity, addressing a critical blind spot in current reasoning evaluation methods and significantly reducing computational costs of CoT prompting.

title: LSTrans: Efficient Knowledge Transfer for Lightweight and Automated ECG Classification
summary: Deploying deep learning models for automated electrocardiogram classification on resource-constrained wearable devices remains challenging due to high computational costs. To address this, we propose LSTrans, a lightweight hybrid model designed for efficient and sensitive ECG analysis. LSTrans introduces a specialized 1D convolutional backbone with an interleaved layer architecture to capture both macroscopic rhythmic trends and microscopic morphological variations. This backbone is cascaded with a Transformer encoder to model long-range temporal dependencies, incorporating Low-Rank Adaptation across critical layers to compress the model and reduce the trainable parameter space. We further employ homogeneous and heterogeneous knowledge distillation to transfer diagnostic expertise from high-capacity teacher models to the student. Experimental results on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that LSTrans achieves a competitive balance between diagnostic sensitivity and resource efficiency, substantially reducing peak memory footprints and training latency during downstream adaptation. The source code is available for review at https://github.com/zyee00128/LSTrans4BIBM.

AI.tldr: LSTrans is a lightweight hybrid ECG classification model combining specialized 1D convolutions and Transformer encoders with Low-Rank Adaptation and knowledge distillation for efficient deployment on resource-constrained wearable devices.
AI.motivation: Deploying deep learning models for automated ECG classification on resource-constrained wearable devices is challenging due to high computational costs. Current models are too computationally expensive for practical deployment on wearable hardware with limited resources.
AI.method: Proposes LSTrans: 1) A specialized 1D convolutional backbone with interleaved layer architecture to capture both macroscopic rhythmic trends and microscopic morphological variations; 2) Cascaded Transformer encoder to model long-range temporal dependencies; 3) Low-Rank Adaptation across critical layers to compress model and reduce trainable parameters; 4) Homogeneous and heterogeneous knowledge distillation to transfer diagnostic expertise from high-capacity teacher models.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that LSTrans achieves a competitive balance between diagnostic sensitivity and resource efficiency. The model substantially reduces peak memory footprints and training latency during downstream adaptation compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: LSTrans provides an effective solution for deploying ECG classification models on resource-constrained wearable devices, offering a good trade-off between diagnostic performance and computational efficiency. The model architecture with specialized convolutions, Transformer integration, and knowledge distillation enables practical implementation for real-world wearable applications.

title: Efficient Test-Time Optimization for Multi-Agent Proof Autoformalization
summary: Full-proof autoformalization bridges extensive mathematical proofs in natural language with formally validated reasoning, offering a pathway to elevate the ceiling of verifiable mathematical reasoning. Unlike statement-level formalization, proof autoformalization is a long-horizon challenge requiring coordination of claims, contexts, and dependencies across many proof steps, yet has only recently come under focused study. Current approaches either rely on costly model training or apply excessive, unguided repair at inference time. To this end, we introduce ToMap, a multi-agent framework that structures proof autoformalization as a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline with efficient test-time optimization guided by formal verification and semantic rubrics for proof quality. Rather than distributing test-time compute across all agents, we perform bottleneck analysis and identify the Decomposer as the critical bottleneck: the quality of its atomic, self-contained proof units directly determines whether downstream agents can successfully formalize and prove each step. ToMap therefore treats the Formalizer and Prover as downstream executors and efficiently focuses test-time compute on Decomposer refinement. This refinement follows a loop inspired by GEPA, evolving prompts over candidate decompositions and using formal verification progress together with semantic proof rubrics to define a Pareto frontier that guides the next decomposition update. Experiments on ProofFlowBench show that ToMap improves over the best previous method by 19.0% when evaluated by both syntactic correctness and semantic faithfulness, while requiring lower test-time cost. Scaling analysis shows that most gains emerge within a few iterations of decomposition evolution, guiding test-time budget selection.

AI.tldr: ToMap is a multi-agent framework for proof autoformalization that uses a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline with efficient test-time optimization focused on refining the Decomposer component using formal verification and semantic proof rubrics.
AI.motivation: Proof autoformalization is a challenging long-horizon problem that coordinates claims, contexts, and dependencies across many proof steps. Current approaches are either costly in training or use excessive unguided repair at inference time, creating a need for more efficient and effective methods.
AI.method: ToMap structures proof autoformalization as a Decomposer-Formalizer-Prover pipeline. It identifies the Decomposer as the critical bottleneck and focuses test-time compute on refining it through a GEPA-inspired loop. The refinement evolves prompts over candidate decompositions using formal verification progress and semantic proof rubrics to define a Pareto frontier guiding decomposition updates.
AI.result: On ProofFlowBench, ToMap improves over the best previous method by 19.0% when evaluated by both syntactic correctness and semantic faithfulness, while requiring lower test-time cost. Scaling analysis shows most gains emerge within a few iterations of decomposition evolution.
AI.conclusion: ToMap provides an effective multi-agent framework for proof autoformalization that achieves significant improvements over previous methods while being more computationally efficient by focusing test-time optimization on the critical Decomposer component.

title: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature
comment: 17 pages (9 main text), 10 figures. Accepted at the 42nd Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2026)
summary: Numerical integration is a cornerstone of various scientific computing applications, such as engineering simulations and model evidence computations in probabilistic machine learning. Bayesian Quadrature uses Gaussian process surrogates that explicitly encode structural assumptions about the integrand to obtain integral estimates with quantified uncertainty. These surrogates are predominantly based on stationary covariance functions, which results in model misspecification for integrands exhibiting nonstationary behavior. We tackle this issue through an adaptively growing, tree-based partition of the integration domain into local stationary models. Our method recombines the local integral estimates through a hierarchy of GP conditioning that reintroduces cross-subdomain correlations, while model selection criteria control the tree growth to avoid unnecessary partitioning. The resulting algorithm is simple, requires no MCMC, and adapts its evaluation budget to local integrand complexity. On benchmark integration problems and a model evidence computation for an epidemiological model, Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature achieves substantial gains over standard Bayesian Quadrature on nonstationary integrands while matching its performance on stationary ones.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature improves numerical integration for nonstationary functions by using tree-based domain partitioning with local stationary models, adapting evaluation budget to local complexity without MCMC.
AI.motivation: Standard Bayesian Quadrature uses stationary Gaussian process surrogates, which are misspecified for integrands with nonstationary behavior, leading to poor performance on functions with varying characteristics across the domain.
AI.method: Uses an adaptively growing tree-based partition of the integration domain into local stationary models. Recombines local integral estimates through hierarchical GP conditioning that reintroduces cross-subdomain correlations, with model selection criteria controlling tree growth to avoid unnecessary partitioning.
AI.result: The method achieves substantial gains over standard Bayesian Quadrature on nonstationary integrands while matching its performance on stationary ones. Demonstrated effectiveness on benchmark integration problems and an epidemiological model evidence computation.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical Bayesian Quadrature provides a simple, MCMC-free algorithm that adapts evaluation budget to local integrand complexity, successfully addressing the limitations of stationary models in Bayesian Quadrature for nonstationary functions.

title: Calibrated e-CUSUM Decoding for Quantized Reasoning Models: Why Token Log-Probability Is the Wrong Observable for Decoding Monitors
summary: Low-bit quantization makes small reasoning models inexpensive to deploy but can degrade their chains of thought. This motivates decoder-side monitors that intervene when generation becomes unreliable. We show that a natural candidate, the centered token log-probability increment $\log p(w_t)+H_t$, is the wrong observable for this purpose. Under the model's own sampling law it is a mean-zero martingale by construction, so it measures sampling self-consistency rather than trajectory health and is nearly silent during confident repetition, where both $\log p(w_t)$ and entropy are close to zero. We introduce a training-free decoding controller that combines (i) a degeneration-aware alarm score fusing token uncertainty with explicit verbatim repetition and (ii) a calibrated e-process-inspired sequential detector. The raw product process is Ville-valid under a conditional-mean null, while the deployed CUSUM-floored statistic is treated as an empirical change detector because the score is history-dependent and autocorrelated. On GSM8K with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B in FP16 and INT4, calibration turns a monitor that fires on 93--95% of generations into a selective detector of failing traces ($φ\approx 0.3$, precision $\approx 0.6$ against a 0.38 base rate). In this pilot, the controller reduces measured verbatim-degeneration signals and yields a positive but statistically inconclusive INT4 accuracy change from 63% to 69% (paired McNemar $p=0.18$, $n=100$), at a 28% token-budget cost. We also find that non-termination, rather than looping, is the dominant failure mode on GSM8K. The main contribution is methodological: an explanation of why centered token log-probability is inadequate for decoder monitoring and a calibrated, cautiously evaluated replacement.

AI.tldr: Proposes a training-free decoding controller that detects and intervenes in failing reasoning chains during low-bit quantization, replacing the inadequate centered token log-probability with a degeneration-aware alarm score and sequential detector.
AI.motivation: Low-bit quantization reduces deployment costs but can degrade reasoning chain quality. Existing decoder-side monitors using centered token log-probability (log p(w_t) + H_t) are inadequate because they measure sampling self-consistency rather than trajectory health, being silent during confident repetition where both log probability and entropy are near zero.
AI.method: Introduces a training-free decoding controller combining: (1) a degeneration-aware alarm score fusing token uncertainty with explicit verbatim repetition, and (2) a calibrated e-process-inspired sequential detector. Uses CUSUM-floored statistics as empirical change detector due to history-dependent, autocorrelated scores.
AI.result: On GSM8K with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B in FP16 and INT4, calibration transforms a monitor firing on 93-95% of generations into a selective detector of failing traces (φ≈0.3, precision≈0.6 against 0.38 base rate). Controller reduces verbatim-degeneration signals and yields INT4 accuracy improvement from 63% to 69% (statistically inconclusive, p=0.18) with 28% token-budget cost. Non-termination found to be dominant failure mode.
AI.conclusion: The main contribution is methodological: explaining why centered token log-probability is inadequate for decoder monitoring and providing a calibrated, cautiously evaluated replacement that selectively detects failing reasoning traces during low-bit quantized inference.

title: Weight-Adjusted Gradients Reveal Parameter Importance and Failure Modes in LLMs
summary: Understanding which parameters are influential in Large Language Models (LLMs) is central to improving their efficiency, reliability, and interpretability. We introduce Weight-Adjusted Gradients (WAG), a simple yet effective approach for estimating parameter importance that explicitly captures the interaction between model weights and first-order gradient information and identifies parameters that disproportionately influence model behavior, such as those responsible for collapse phenomena in LLMs. Across a range of models and settings, we show that WAG surfaces a tiny but critical subset of parameters whose modification leads to dramatic degradation in performance, a failure mode that existing importance metrics overlook. These findings reveal a previously underexplored interplay between weights and gradients, suggesting that parameter importance cannot be fully understood through either signal alone. The surprising effectiveness of WAG points to fundamental structural properties of trained networks and motivates new open questions about the role of zeroth-order and first-order information in deep learning. We demonstrate the practical utility of WAG across multiple applications, including expert allocation in mixture-of-expert architectures, parameter-specific unlearning, mixed-precision quantization, and layer selection for knowledge editing. Our results position WAG as a unified approach for analyzing, debugging, and controlling LLMs, and opens new directions for principled model-level interpretation.

AI.tldr: Weight-Adjusted Gradients (WAG) is a novel method that identifies critical parameters in LLMs by combining weight and gradient information, revealing influential parameters that existing metrics miss, with practical applications across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Understanding which parameters are influential in Large Language Models is crucial for improving their efficiency, reliability, and interpretability. Current importance metrics may overlook critical failure modes and don't fully capture the interplay between model weights and gradients.
AI.method: Introduces Weight-Adjusted Gradients (WAG), a simple yet effective approach that explicitly captures the interaction between model weights and first-order gradient information to estimate parameter importance. It identifies parameters that disproportionately influence model behavior.
AI.result: WAG surfaces a tiny but critical subset of parameters whose modification leads to dramatic performance degradation - a failure mode existing metrics overlook. It demonstrates practical utility across applications including expert allocation in MoE architectures, parameter-specific unlearning, mixed-precision quantization, and layer selection for knowledge editing.
AI.conclusion: Parameter importance cannot be fully understood through either weights or gradients alone. WAG reveals fundamental structural properties of trained networks and opens new directions for principled model-level interpretation, serving as a unified approach for analyzing, debugging, and controlling LLMs.

title: Verifier-Guided Twelve-Tone Composition: A Generate-Verify-Repair Harness for Symbolic Music Generation
summary: Large language models can produce superficially legal twelve-tone scores that collapse into degenerate textures. We introduce a neuro-symbolic harness that wraps a language-model proposer in a generate-verify-repair-trace loop with symbolic verification. The complete pipeline improves event-local consistency without claiming whole-piece legality. Across 40 controlled tasks and four paired models, audited delivery yield rises from 13.3% under raw generation to 48.1% with the harness, which explicitly abstains otherwise. The pass rate of a narrower collision and serialisation-consistency check rises from 33.5% to 58.3%, while degeneracy remains near 0.05, including under exploratory adversarial prompting. A blinded evaluation by five experts also shows a descriptive aggregate preference for harness candidates over raw generation in adherence, perceived legality, coherence, and overall quality.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic harness improves LLM-generated twelve-tone music by adding verification and repair loops, increasing event-level consistency while maintaining low degeneracy.
AI.motivation: Large language models often produce twelve-tone music scores that appear superficially legal but collapse into degenerate textures upon closer inspection, lacking proper serial consistency and event-level coherence.
AI.method: The authors introduce a neuro-symbolic harness that wraps an LLM proposer in a generate-verify-repair-trace loop with symbolic verification. This pipeline improves event-local consistency without requiring whole-piece legality.
AI.result: The harness improves audited delivery yield from 13.3% to 48.1% across 40 tasks and four models. Pass rates for collision/serialization consistency checks rose from 33.5% to 58.3%, while degeneracy remained near 0.05. Expert evaluations showed preference for harness candidates over raw generation across adherence, legality, coherence, and overall quality.
AI.conclusion: The neuro-symbolic approach effectively improves the quality of LLM-generated twelve-tone music by combining neural generation with symbolic verification and repair, significantly reducing degeneracy while maintaining musical integrity.

title: When does distribution shift break graph neural networks calibration?
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications where distribution shift is un-avoidable. However, how such shifts affect model calibration, defined as the agreement between predictive confidence and actual accuracy, remains poorly understood, and existing graph calibration methods typically rely on labeled validation data from the deployment distribution. In this work, I present the first closed-form theoretical characterization of GNN calibration under distribution shift. I show that calibration is governed by a single scalar quantity that explicitly depends on structural changes between the source and target graphs, as well as feature quality. This characterization precisely identifies when a model becomes over-confident, under-confident, or remains calibrated, and directly yields the optimal temperature scaling strategy. I further extend the analysis to graph convolutional networks with symmetric normalization, multi-class classification, and covariate shift, and derive a theoretical upper bound on the expected calibration error. My analysis also reveals that, under homogeneous distribution shift, a single global temperature is theoretically optimal, providing a principled explanation for why more complex node-wise recalibration methods offer no additional benefit. Building on these theoretical insights, I propose STAC, a source-free, label-free calibration method. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks demonstrate substantial calibration improvements, while evaluations on five real-world graph datasets show that reliable calibration without target labels remains challenging despite the strong predictive power of the theory.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first closed-form theoretical characterization of GNN calibration under distribution shift, identifies a governing scalar quantity for calibration, develops optimal temperature scaling strategies, and proposes a source-free, label-free calibration method called STAC.
AI.motivation: GNNs are increasingly deployed in real-world applications where distribution shift is unavoidable, but how such shifts affect model calibration remains poorly understood. Existing graph calibration methods typically rely on labeled validation data from the deployment distribution, which is often unavailable in practice.
AI.method: The author develops a theoretical framework for GNN calibration under distribution shift, deriving closed-form characterizations showing calibration is governed by a single scalar quantity dependent on structural changes between source/target graphs and feature quality. This framework precisely identifies calibration behavior (over-confident, under-confident, or calibrated) and yields optimal temperature scaling strategies. The analysis extends to GCNs with symmetric normalization, multi-class classification, and covariate shift, with theoretical upper bounds on expected calibration error. Building on these insights, the author proposes STAC, a source-free, label-free calibration method.
AI.result: The theoretical analysis reveals that under homogeneous distribution shift, a single global temperature is theoretically optimal, explaining why complex node-wise recalibration methods offer no additional benefit. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks demonstrate substantial calibration improvements, while evaluations on five real-world graph datasets show that reliable calibration without target labels remains challenging despite the strong predictive power of the theory.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first theoretical foundation for understanding GNN calibration under distribution shift, offering both theoretical insights and practical calibration methods. While the theory is powerful, real-world applications show that achieving reliable calibration without target labels remains an open challenge, highlighting the need for further research in this area.

title: AutoVSR: Automatic Visual-to-Symbolic Reasoning for Symbolic Expression Generation from Circuit Schematic
summary: Symbolic expressions can effectively characterize and predict circuit behavior, but deriving them directly from circuit schematics is challenging. This process requires accurate visual-to-symbolic construction of circuit structure from images and correct multi-step symbolic derivation, both of which impose strict correctness requirements. This work proposes AutoVSR, an automated framework for visual-to-symbolic generation of circuit expressions using Vision Language Models (VLMs). By reconstructing circuit diagrams into an executable intermediate representation (Executable IR) and leveraging a symbolic solver for reasoning, AutoVSR significantly improves the accuracy of symbolic expression generation. AutoVSR introduces two key innovations: an IR construction method guided by component rule retrieval and verification-based feedback, and a symbolic solver implemented as a planning agent equipped with a symbolic tool library for reliable multi-step derivation. Compared with end-to-end VLM approaches and specialized methods on the main symbolic expression generation task, AutoVSR achieves accuracy improvements of 30.01--59.45% and 41.96--51.84%, respectively. Moreover, AutoVSR surpasses closed-source state-of-the-art VLMs in inference cost and computational efficiency. Code is available at https://github.com/LongfeiLi1/AutoVSR.

AI.tldr: AutoVSR is an automated framework that uses Vision Language Models to generate symbolic circuit expressions from circuit diagram images, achieving significant accuracy improvements over existing methods through executable intermediate representation and symbolic solver techniques.
AI.motivation: Deriving symbolic expressions directly from circuit schematics is challenging because it requires both accurate visual-to-symbolic construction of circuit structure and correct multi-step symbolic derivation, both with strict correctness requirements.
AI.method: AutoVSR reconstructs circuit diagrams into an executable intermediate representation (Executable IR) guided by component rule retrieval and verification-based feedback, then uses a symbolic solver implemented as a planning agent with a symbolic tool library for reliable multi-step derivation.
AI.result: AutoVSR achieves accuracy improvements of 30.01-59.45% over end-to-end VLM approaches and 41.96-51.84% over specialized methods. It also surpasses closed-source state-of-the-art VLMs in inference cost and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AutoVSR framework successfully addresses the visual-to-symbolic generation challenge for circuit expressions by combining executable IR construction with symbolic solving, demonstrating superior performance and efficiency compared to existing approaches.

title: Lower Bound on the Cumulative Constrained Violation for the OGD+Projection algorithm for Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO)
summary: The problem of constrained online convex optimization is considered, where at each round, once a learner commits to an action $x_t \in \mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, a convex loss function $f_t$ and a convex constraint function $g_t$ that drives the constraint $g_t(x)\le 0$ are revealed. The objective is to simultaneously minimize the static regret and cumulative constraint violation (CCV) compared to the benchmark that knows the loss functions and constraint functions $f_t$ and $g_t$ for all $t$ ahead of time, and chooses a static optimal action that is feasible with respect to all $g_t(x)\le 0$. Currently, the best known algorithm is OGD+Projection algorithm of [Vaze and Sinha, 2025] that has simultaneous regret of $O(\sqrt{T})$ and CCV of $O(T^{1/3})$ for $d=2$ [Balasundaram et al., 2026], and simultaneous regret of $O(\sqrt{T})$ and CCV of $O(\sqrt{T})$ for any $d$ [Sarkar and Sinha, 2026]. In this paper, we show that the CCV of the OGD+Projection algorithm is $Ω(T^{\frac{d-1}{2d}})$. This is the first such lower bound result.

AI.tldr: Lower bound analysis showing OGD+Projection algorithm for constrained online convex optimization has CCV of Ω(T^{(d-1)/2d}).
AI.motivation: Previous research on constrained online convex optimization showed the OGD+Projection algorithm achieves O(√T) regret and O(T^{1/3}) CCV for d=2, and O(√T) for both regret and CCV for any d. The paper aims to establish the first lower bound on cumulative constraint violation for this algorithm.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis proving a lower bound on the cumulative constraint violation (CCV) of the OGD+Projection algorithm through mathematical derivation and lower bound construction.
AI.result: The paper proves that the CCV of OGD+Projection algorithm is Ω(T^{(d-1)/2d}), establishing the first such lower bound in the field of constrained online convex optimization.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a fundamental lower bound on constraint violation for the widely studied OGD+Projection algorithm, advancing theoretical understanding of constrained online optimization algorithms and their limitations.

title: Compile, Then Page: Executable SOP Programs and a Capability-Gated Runtime for Procedural LLM Agents
summary: Enterprise agents must follow long-horizon, conditional, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs). We compile machine-readable SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code and run them with a program-guided (PG) stack machine that pages the active frame while an LLM performs semantic execution. A three-arm SOPBench study across six models separates representation from runtime: compiled text never significantly hurts and gains up to 16.0 points where official prose underperforms. Runtime guidance is capability-gated. Two strong models independently show positive seven-domain PG contrasts (58:19 and 75:31 discordant pairs), whereas weak models are harmed. A full-program cursor ablation (active frame first, complete program retained) recovers much of the strong-model refusal gain; selective visibility adds a smaller improvement. Paired probe and audit measurements track this divide to spontaneous state discipline rather than reconstruction ability. On Bank the three primary arms rise from 70.4 to 86.4 to 92.8, with 100% refusal correctness. Practical guidance: compile first; enable active-frame paging only after a model-level discipline check.

AI.tldr: LLMs can execute complex enterprise SOPs better when procedures are compiled into executable pseudo-code and run with program-guided stack machines that page active frames, with benefits depending on model capability.
AI.motivation: Enterprise agents need to follow long-horizon, conditional, safety-critical standard operating procedures (SOPs), but existing text-based approaches may underperform compared to structured executable representations.
AI.method: Compile machine-readable SOP constraints into executable pseudo-code and run them with a program-guided stack machine that pages the active frame while an LLM performs semantic execution. Evaluated on SOPBench with six models using three-arm study design.
AI.result: Strong models show significant improvements (58:19 and 75:31 discordant pairs) with program guidance, while weak models are harmed. Full-program cursor ablation recovers much of refusal gain. On Bank domain, performance rises from 70.4% to 86.4% to 92.8% with 100% refusal correctness.
AI.conclusion: Compiling SOPs into executable pseudo-code improves execution for capable models; active-frame paging should only be enabled after verifying model-level discipline. Representation (compiled vs. prose) separates from runtime guidance effects.

title: Diachronic Sample Integration: Robust Tail-Risk Estimation with Generative Models
summary: Deep generative models are increasingly used as simulators for downstream decision-making under data scarcity, but in risk-sensitive applications their usefulness depends on rare adverse scenarios rather than typical samples. Standard generative objectives prioritize bulk distributional fidelity, leaving low-probability tails vulnerable to localized optimization noise and making tail-dependent functionals unstable under finite simulation budgets. We introduce Diachronic Sample Integration (DSI), a test-time inference framework that ensembles generated samples across checkpoints from a stochastic training trajectory. DSI targets a checkpoint-mixture distribution that averages checkpoint-specific tail fluctuations rather than relying on a single brittle endpoint. We formalize this mechanism through a finite-budget bias-variance theory.
